question: I’ve read in several texts that Vatuka Bhairava is connected to the seventh Mahavidya. Vatuka is described as a brahmachari, while the seventh Mahavidya is seen as a widow. This contrast caught my attention, and I’d appreciate any clarification.
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, we have a beautiful connection about our beloved Ista Bhairava. In the Devi Rahasya, during the great battle of Devi Mahatmya (Durga Saptashati), when Maa Mahakali was in her unstoppable Ugra Swaroopa after slaying Raktabija, something unique happened. To balance this fierce energy, Shiva took the form of a Bala Swaroopa —Vatuka Bhairava, who ultimately pacified Maa, Devi after her fierce battle. Interestingly, he was also blessed by all Dasha Mahavidyas, which makes his story even more significant. This narrative is NOT documented in the Shiva Purana, though there are different versions of the story as well. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: If I were to start the sadhana of the Ashta Bhairavas, is it mandatory to begin with Asitanga Bhairava and proceed in order, or can I follow my own inclination? For example, I currently feel the strongest connection with Ruru and Chanda Bhairava.
answer: Namaskaram, it's wonderful to hear that you're interested in starting Ashta Bhairava Aradhana. My humble suggestion would be that if you have already been initiated by a guru, it would be best to follow the prescribed sequence. If not, let's focus on the sadhana shared in the group for now, and after completing five mandalas, we can revisit this discussion. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: For the past 1.5 years, it has been my heartfelt wish to begin Batuk Bhairava sadhana. Can I simultaneously chant the Kal Bhairava Ashtakam, Batuk Bhairava Brahma Kavach, and a simple Batuk Bhairava mantra?
answer: Namaskaram, When we perform Bhairava sadhana, Nama Japa helps us stay focused through repetition, making our practice more active (Pragna or even Jagrata). On the other hand, an Ashtakam and Kavacham is a structured way of invoking and praising, Bhairava’s divine protection. It would be good to recite it regularly. Once you have completed 03 Ashtami Tithis or 01 full Mandala sadhana, you can gradually move towards Batuka Bhairava sadhana. ~ Jai Bhairava ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Can I start the second mandala sadhana from 4th of May Ashtami. Can there be a gap between two mandala sadhana? If yes, how many days of gap is allowed?
answer: Namaskaram, Regarding Mandala sadhana, taking a break between mandalas is not a strict rule—it is a personal choice and depends on one's Anusthana. Whether it is 48 days or 144 days, it remains an intimate connection between you and the daiva. I would like to reiterate in this group that we are merely instruments in this sacred journey, seeking to experience and know Him with utmost love and reverence. Here, no one is a Guru or Shishya—there is only one Guru for all of us, Guru Bhairava, who alone can lead us closer to Him through His grace. As for the next step in sadhana, as mentioned, completing 03 Ashtami Tithi Sadhanas or 144 days (03 Mandalas) is a recommended path. In the upcoming Ashtami, we will share insights on Nyasa Paddhati in sadhana, which will deepen our connection with our Param Guru, Bhairava. May his grace be on all of us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have completed 48 days of Anusthana. I look forward to continuing with more Anusthanas. I have a few queries—would it be possible to share some guidance, please? Can I recite the Kalabhairava Ashtakam daily towards the end of the puja? I currently recite the Bhairava Brahma Kavacham. I had stopped saying the Apaduddharaka Mantra in the Kavacham after hearing one of your videos. May I include that mantra again? Can I recite the 108 names of Bhairava? What should be done with the red cloth that has been kept in the puja?
answer: Namaskaram, @lakshmi_prasanna_kotagiri Ji, it’s his grace that you are able to see progress in sadhana of Guru Bhairava! Kalabhairavashtakam in Pooja: Yes, reciting the Kalabhairavashtakam in your Pooja is highly beneficial, especially on Kalashtami days. It brings protection, positive energy, and deeper connection with our Istha. Shani Prabha on March 30th: This day coincides with Shani Amavasya, a significant time for Shani Dev. Also, a rare celestial event will occur—six planets (Sun, Moon, Mercury, Venus, Rahu, and Saturn) aligning in Pisces from March 30 - April 1, 2025. This is highly important in Vedic astrology. Doing Bhairava sadhana will be highly supportive! Brahma Kavacham in Pooja: Yes, you can include Brahma Kavacham. It is a protective hymn, and its power depends on your faith and intent. Yes, Sri Kalabhairava Ashtottara Shatanamavali, I personally use this every day and perform 5 malas. Use of red cloth used in Bhairava Puja (especially on Ashtami) should be kept for every Bhairava Pooja and not discarded after a single use. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: What if mandala sadhana gets extended into third prahar? For example, if we start it around 10.30 pm and it finishes at 12.15 am. Is daily hair washing necessary? Is the same time every day mandatory, or can we be a bit flexible if the schedule is too tight?
answer: Namaskaram, It’s great to know your sadhana aligns with Sandhyakala (transition times). This period is powerful for deepening your connection with the Daiva! Let me share quick thoughts on key benefits of sadhana in Sandhyakala: Stronger Connection – The energy flow helps you feel the Devata’s presence more deeply. Better Focus – The mind turns inward, making meditation and rituals more effective. No Need for Multiple Head Baths – Only the first bath before starting matters; no need at the end. May your sadhana be fulfilling and transformative! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: What should we do with the black sesame seeds that are kept in the puja every Saturday? Could you please guide me?
answer: Namaskaram, After completing Bhairava Puja and offering black sesame seeds, you can: Distribute as Prasadam – Eat a small portion with devotion or share it with others by preparing some sweet or any kind of food to receive Bhairava’s blessings for protection, wisdom, and strength. Offer to Nature – Sprinkle the seeds under a sacred Peepal or Banyan tree or immerse them in a river or pond to strengthen the offering and seek divine grace. Use in sadhana – Keep some seeds for future Bhairava sadhana, energize a yantra, or mix them with oil for lighting a diya to enhance spiritual power. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have been practicing our Guru Bhairava’ s sadhana since last year and have completed three Sankalpa so far. I was blessed with the opportunity to visit Kashi earlier this year during my trip to India. I continue the practices from my Sankalpa as part of my daily puja, which I perform twice a day—morning and evening. I started with three malas and have gradually increased to ten malas a day. During Maha Shivaratri, I completed 100 malas. Recently, I began chanting the Batuka Bhairava Nama Mantra and currently do three malas of nama japa. I have a couple of questions: Is it acceptable to use the same mala for both “Aum Bhairavaya Namah” and “Aum Batuka Bhairavaya Namah”? How can we surrender more deeply to Him?
answer: Namaskaram, Anish Ji, it is truly Guru Bhairava’ s grace that you have been able to perform His Nama Smarana and Japa consistently. As per our sadhana Paddhati, the next step would be to deepen your practice through a structured approach—either by participating in three consecutive Ashtami Sadhanas or undertaking a 48-day Mandala sadhana. In some cases, an extended 144-day practice with 22 malas per day is advised. You can refer to the document in the sadhana Paddhati folder for details. Regarding your queries: Yes, the same mala can be used for any Devata, but it should not be worn until you complete three Mandala Sadhanas, as it then becomes a protective armor through the accumulated mantra shakti. As mentioned in the Q&A session, true grace is received through dedicated sadhana and the love you offer to Him. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Can we continue the mandala during menstruation? I perform all activities during my cycle and do not experience any physical weakness. If I do sadhana today at 6 PM and tomorrow at 9 PM, there is a gap of 27 hours (more than 24 hours). Is that acceptable? Should the next day’s sadhana be done within 24 hours of the previous one? Is it okay to move the legs while sitting, or should the sitting posture be maintained throughout? I usually switch from Sukhasana to Vajrasana due to numbness from prolonged sitting. While chanting, my mind stays focused on the mantra for about three malas, but then drifts for one mala and becomes mechanical. It eventually returns to focus. This back-and-forth movement of the mind keeps happening.
answer: Namaskaram, It is a divine and natural process. Even in Devi Aradhana, it is considered highly auspicious. You may continue your Mandala sadhana with trust in Guru Bhairava’s grace. Follow your inner strength and devotion to Him. Sadhana is ideally performed within 24 hours for continuity, as a full day is divided into eight praharas of approximately three hours each. If delayed, it’s not a problem for a day or two, but stay sincere in your intent and maintain a steady routine. Adjust your posture for comfort. Sukhasana or Vajrasana—choose whichever helps you stay focused. As your connection deepens, sitting still for longer periods will become effortless. It is natural for the mind to wander. Gently bring it back with awareness. With practice, your focus will strengthen. Guru Bhairava’s grace is always with us, guiding our path. ~ Jai Bhairava

question:
How important is asana (seating posture)?
What if one cannot sit—can one stand and chant? If I cannot sit near the diya or altar, can I sit elsewhere in the house and continue with nama japa? What about long journeys in a bus or flight?
How can one do simple nama japa without sitting properly? Is it less effective? I’ve heard that energy flows through the feet to the ground if proper asana is not maintained.
In such cases, is mantra japa wasted?
answer: Namaskaram, Let me share the response in two simple parts — “What is Asana” and “Why it matters” in Bhairava sadhana, along with how to adapt when sitting isn't possible. Asana is not just a mat or cushion; it is the space where your energy settles and becomes steady—like unmoving awareness. With time and devotion, your asana can (and will) become a sacred energy point — a micro pīṭha — which sages of the past even carried with them. When traveling, even if you can't use your physical seat, you can still connect mentally by visualizing your regular space and asana, and by keeping your spine as straight as possible. Sitting near the altar is beautiful, but not mandatory — what truly matters is the inner connection (bhāvanā / bhāva) and how clearly you can hold the deity in your awareness. Even when standing or moving, Bhairava listens to the heart — your intention and feeling are what give power to the mantra. It is never wasted. Wherever you are, and however you chant His nāma, do it with love and deep surrender — He is with us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Guruji, could you please shed some light on this? Today, while I was doing japa — which I usually do with my eyes closed — for some reason, I opened my eyes and looked at the deepam. I saw a red and black bug walk straight into the deepam fire. I observed how the fire spread from its antennae to the rear end of its body. The moment it caught fire, it became shiny with a lot of sparks. It was windy near the deepam for 2–3 minutes, and I noticed the flame moving intensely before it eventually stabilized. I was watching the entire scene while continuing my japa. What am I supposed to take from this experience?
answer: Namaskaram, Beautiful observation, with His grace. This is a subtle sign — such moments are symbolic, like the ego burning in the fire of awareness. Eyes opening mid-japa to witness it is not a coincidence, but grace. Stay with the experience; let it speak more deeply. Another gentle insight: while hearing Bhairava’s name, the bug surrendered fully, ready for its next birth. A soft reminder that, with Guru Bhairava’s grace, all our burdens can be burnt away, preparing us for the next step in our journey. ~ Jai Bhairava ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Could you please clarify — should the nama mantra be chanted aloud or silently in manasa? I find it difficult to chant aloud for 22 malas. I usually chant internally while visualizing Kaashi Bhairava and mentally performing puja. Sometimes, I even feel a telepathic connection with Him during japa. Is this approach acceptable? On days when I feel mentally drained, I simply gaze at His image while chanting. Is that okay? How can I improve my practice? Also, I’ve recently felt drawn to Maa Tara. I recall your guidance to complete 144 days of Bhairava sadhana first. Would it be alright to chant one mala of Her nama mantra during this period?
answer: Namaskaram, Connecting to the way of chanting—whether manasa or aloud (Vaikhari)—is perfectly valid, especially since your focus and bhavana are so deep and heartfelt. Visualizing (dhyana) Kashi Bhairava and inwardly connecting with Him as you described is not only okay, it’s a beautiful form of sadhana. On days when energy is low, just looking at His form with complete surrender, love is more than enough—presence matters more than effort. As for Maa Tara, yes, it's best to complete the 144 days with Bhairava first, but doing 1 mala softly with devotion is fine if the pull is genuine—just keep Bhairava sadhana as your anchor. May His grace be on all of us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Is Bhavana possible without Surrendering? What is the true meaning of Surrendering?
answer: Namaskaram, Bhavana without surrender is limited, like a lamp without oil. True surrender means letting go of control, ego, and fully trusting the Daiva (Guru Bhairava) with love. When the heart becomes soft, bhavana naturally flows. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Is letting go of control truly possible? The mind likes to control everything — it creates the questions, provides the answers, sows doubt, and then uses the ego to justify those doubts.
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, the mind does try to control—but the path is to gently watch it, not fight it. Letting go isn’t sudden, it’s a soft unfolding. With our Guru Bhairava only grace and awareness, even the mind learns to surrender. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I’m unsure where to place my dharana during japa. I try visualizing the mantra syllables, which naturally leads to a meditative state and silence. But when I focus on Batuk Bhairava’s form as per the sattvic dhyana shloka, maintaining that image for 11 malas feels mentally exhausting. Using an external image repeatedly also feels overwhelming. Is visualizing the syllables during japa a valid method? How should I refine my practice? चरण स्पर्श"
answer: Namaskaram, Your inner experience is already guiding you beautifully. If visualizing the mantra syllables brings peace and helps you enter a meditative state, that is a valid and effective method. Not everyone connects easily with form-based dharana (Concentration) is the sixth limb, and that's okay. Our Guru Bhairava is beyond form and name—He responds to sincerity, not complexity. Let your practice be natural and heart-led. The mantra itself will shape the right dharana for you over time. May His grace be on all of us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have a question about Nitya sadhana. Is it okay to remove a photo of one form of Bhagawati from my altar — whom I’ve been worshipping for nearly two years with Panchapachara Puja and a common stotram — so I can focus on another form of Bhagawati, whose puja, stotra, and japa I’ve already begun in recent months?
answer: Namaskaram, Thank you for asking with such sincerity. Yes, it is completely fine to shift your focus to another form of Devi if you feel a deeper connection there now... The Divine Maa is one, though her forms are many. She understands the love in your heart, not just the image on the altar. You may respectfully remove the previous form with a small offering or prayer of gratitude, acknowledging the journey so far. Follow where your devotion flows naturally. May His grace be on all of us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Is the presence or movement of Kuldevi’s energy within the body considered beneficial or harmful? I seek your guidance.
answer: Namaskaram, Kuldevi sanchar (presence or movement) in the body is not bad—it is a sign of her grace and nearness. As mentioned in the Purana, when the Devi chooses to awaken or express through the body, it is to guide, protect, or purify the devotee (Upasaka). Just stay humble, devoted, and grounded. Let her lead the way. May His grace be on all of us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: What if we don’t know our kuladeva? How to pray in that case?
answer: Namaskaram, If the Kula Devata is not known, there’s no need to worry. Many people today don’t have access to their ancestral lineage information — yet the Divine always listens to a heart that calls with sincere faith. The Shastras tell us that when the original deity is unknown, universal forms such as Durga Maa, Parama Shiva, or Guru Bhairava can lovingly take that place, as they are the very source from which all devatas arise. With heartfelt devotion and a clear sankalpa, your prayers will always reach the right place. You may simply say with bhāva: “O Divine Maa, if I do not yet know the form in which You protected my lineage, I still surrender to You in the form You reveal to my heart.” Begin your worship with trust. This bhāva is enough to open the door to Divine connection. May Bhairava’s grace be upon all. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Can Bhairava Homa be performed at home? I’ve been watching your videos to deepen my understanding of Deva Aradhana, Upasana, and Anusthana. Kindly guide us on this aspect.
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, Bhairava Homa can be performed at home with devotion, purity, and the right intention. I’m truly happy to know you’re watching the videos and deepening your understanding of Divya Aradhana, Upasana, and Anushthan. This pure intention itself brings the grace of Bhairava closer to you. Bhairava is bhakta-vatsala — he is easily pleased by heartfelt devotion. Even a small fire offering done with love becomes powerful in his worship. If you are doing the 144-day sadhana, it is best to complete your Sankalpa and 3 Mandala first. After that, we can talk on these rituals in detail. If you feel ready now, you may start with a basic Agni Hotra kit and offer Bhairava’s Ashtottara Shatanamavali (108 names) daily through it. This itself becomes a beautiful beginning. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Whenever I chant or do mantra japa beyond five malas, I often enter a deeply relaxed, almost sleep-like state. I feel incredibly peaceful and calm, with a clear mental visualization of the mantra. My focus remains intact, but after five malas, the japa seems to guide my mind into a different, meditative state. Why does this happen? Do you have any suggestions to improve my method?
answer: Namaskaram, What you’re feeling during your japa is a good sign. When you start feeling peaceful or almost sleepy after a couple of malas, it means the mantra is taking your mind inward. This gentle state is known as manolaya—where the outer mind becomes quiet, and you enter a calm, meditative space. Why this happens: As you repeat the mantra, it begins to calm your thoughts. After a few rounds, your mind may start to go to a deeper level beyond thinking. The mantra is guiding you inward (antar-mukhi), which is a very sacred process. How to improve your practice: Before starting, set a clear intention (sankalpa)—ask the mantra to guide you while keeping you softly alert. Try to sit with your spine straight, and after 4-5 malas, gently open your eyes slightly or look downward to stay present. You can take a short pause between malas—breathe deeply, feel thankful, and then continue. Doing japa during early morning or evening can also help, especially near a lamp or sacred space. Most importantly, don’t worry about this peaceful state—it’s not laziness or distraction. It may be yoga-nidra, where the mantra is still working deeply inside you.... Let us surrender this (Known or Unknown) at the lotus feet of Guru Bhairava! May Bhairava’s grace be upon all. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Is there a subtle difference between Yoga Nidra and regular Nidra (sleep)? How can one discern which state they are in during practice?
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, there is a subtle but deep difference. Nidra is unconscious sleep—where awareness fades. Yoga Nidra is conscious rest—where the body sleeps, but a thread of awareness stays. You’ll know it’s Yoga Nidra when you come out feeling deeply rested, yet inwardly clear, almost as if the mantra or breath continued its own. In other words, if you can witness the stillness while resting—that’s Yoga Nidra. If you completely lose track—that’s natural nidra. Don't be in a hurry to know..., Let the state reveal itself gently, by His Grace. Over time, your awareness will know the difference. May Bhairava’s grace be upon all of us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: At times, I experience a sense of sleepiness during my practice. It feels pleasant, but I lose control over my thoughts — almost as if I begin daydreaming or drifting into a dream-like state. I then have to consciously break out of it to return to awareness. This makes it challenging to go deeper, and sometimes it feels like I’ve simply fallen asleep. I’m still new to sadhana and don’t have much experience yet.
answer: Namaskaram, What you’re experiencing is natural and very common in the early stages of sadhana. As the mind begins to quiet, it often slips into dream-like states. Don’t worry—this too is part of the process. Just keep returning gently to the mantra. With steady practice, Guru Bhairava’s grace will deepen your focus and clarity. Let the experience come and go—your only task is to stay sincere in the sadhana. Keep walking the path. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: After meditating for a year on “Om Bhairavya Namah,” followed by the Kaal Bhairava Ashtakam, I have now added the Vatuk Bhairava naam mantra — “Om Vatuk Bhairavya Namah.” So currently, I am practicing both. After two to three months of this combination, I find myself feeling uncertain. I wonder whether I should focus on just one mantra for a full year. When I switch from “Om Vatuk Bhairavya Namah” back to “Om Bhairavya Namah,” the meditative state gradually fades, and the chanting begins to feel mechanical. Should I continue with the current combination, or would it be better to dedicate myself to a single mantra for deeper absorption? चरण स्पर्श
answer: Namaskaram, Trust in your intuition and let the flow of practice guide you. Both mantras have profound power; if the transition feels mechanical, honor that by focusing on one mantra until it becomes natural again and active!, then explore the other with a renewed connection. This sadhana path is unique, and the right rhythm will emerge with patience. Guru Bhairava's blessing is always with you. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I am currently observing a 144-day mandala. With your blessings and guidance, I intend to begin the Ashtami Agni offering to Bhairava Deva after completing this mandala. Kindly help and guide me in performing the detailed homa paddhati with your continued support and blessings.
answer: Namaskaram, it’s only Bhairava’s grace that guides our lives. He is our true Guru. Don’t worry about too many things—just enjoy the process of these 144 days. Stay connected with the Daiva and do everything with a pure heart and love. Bhairava will lead the way. The Homa steps will come with the Dwitiya Charana sadhana later steps of sadhana, but for now, focus on his wonderful sadhana - A lifetime of connection. May Bhairava’s grace be with you always. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Could someone kindly shed light on the following aspects of sadhana? The practice of wearing a japa mala and using the hand to keep count during chanting. The significance of maintaining count — is it acceptable to use time as a measure instead, perhaps estimating the number of malas based on duration? What considerations should be kept in mind when invoking sadhana outside a traditional pooja room — for example, in a quiet Shiva temple, resort, or farmhouse with a small Shiva Lingam? The role of pranayama and the sequence of practices in sadhana — including the importance of bandhas (energy locks) and their proper order. Understanding the difference between mantra and stotra — and when each is appropriate in one's spiritual journey.
answer:
Namaskaram, Happy to see so many questions at once—but let me answer them one by one, with clarity: Wearing a japa mala and using the hand to count helps anchor the mind. It's not just about numbers—it's about presence. Time can’t measure bhava (feeling), but if it helps you stay consistent, it’s okay for now. Still, try to feel each bead as a pulse of Bhairava. Doing your sadhana outside a Pooja room—like in a quiet Shiva temple or near a small Shivling—is absolutely fine. Bhairava is not limited by space; He is invoked by intent, purity, and presence. Cleanliness, mental quietude, and inner reverence matter more than walls. Pranayama comes first to calm the winds (prana)—it prepares the vessel. Then bandhas (energy locks) hold that prana, like sealing sacred fire in a vessel. Skipping order may scatter the energy. Mantra is a seed, stotra is its flowering—both are sacred. But start with mantra—it calls Him by name. Above all, begin your 144-day sadhana. This is not about theory but entering the current. Bhairava doesn’t give knowledge—He awakens it in you...! Start. Sit. Breathe. Chant. Be.
The rest will reveal itself. May Bhairava’s grace be upon all. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Is "Hreem Guru Bhairavaya Namaha" a valid mantra?
answer: Namaskaram, In Bhairava Consciousness, there's no concept of valid or invalid — it's the mind that creates such distinctions, often blocking us from the experience the Self is already aware of. Instead of seeking validation, immerse yourself in the experience. That’s why I always approach completing a full 144-day (3 Mandala) sadhana, minimum. Once you deeply engage, all doubts naturally dissolve. Until then, the mind will keep questioning, “Am I doing it right?” As for the mantra — it’s a specific hymn or seed used for invoking Bhairava for a particular purpose. It’s not general or for everyone. So please focus on the steps and sadhana paddhati shared under the sadhana tab. And if anything still feels unclear, I’d be truly happy to share more. May Bhairava’s grace be upon all. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Are there any subtle differences in how Bhairava sadhana may affect women compared to men — particularly for women beginning this sadhana in their 50s, or those experiencing menopause or hormonal changes? The recent video touched upon the theme of the body becoming the yantra. Given that certain fluctuations naturally occur in the body, I find it challenging at times to maintain consistency in the quality of my japa.
answer: Namaskaram, Such a heartfelt and important question. Yes, Bhairava sadhana can unfold differently for each person—especially for women, and more so during phases like menopause, where the body naturally shifts. The body becoming the yantra also means these changes are part of the sacred process, not separate from it. It’s okay to witness fluctuations in your japa. What matters is the sincerity of your presence. Bhairava, as Om Vaidyaya Namah, is indeed the supreme healer—of body, mind, and karmic layers. So even if consistency feels challenged, your devotion anchors the sadhana. You’re not alone in this path—many experiences such waves. Keep holding his name with trust, even gently. May Bhairava’s grace be upon all. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Could you please share some insights on Nyasa and its relevance to japa in future videos? I’ve been practicing Bhairava sadhana for a year. Last year, I completed one mandala of the Bhairava Ashtakam over 43 days, and I’m currently halfway through a 48-day nama japa. I’m curious to understand when Nyasa is typically integrated into the practice. Does it have healing properties, or should the body be in a certain state of balance or readiness before incorporating Nyasa? The recent video mentioned the body becoming the yantra, and I’ve noticed fluctuations in my physical state that sometimes affect the consistency of my japa.
answer: Namaskaram, Thank you for your sincere sadhana and beautiful question. Yes, Nyasa will be shared as part of Prathama Charana of Guru Bhairava sadhana, and Homa will be in the Dwitiya Charana. For now, please continue to immerse yourself in the divine flow of Nama Smarana—it is the most sacred and direct way to align with our Iṣṭa, Guru Bhairava. Everything unfolds in grace. Kindly stay with the process—your dedication and love will be key to unlock his grace. May his grace be on all of us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I find it difficult to access content on Matanga Bhairava, the consort of Bhagavati Matangi. If any insights can be shared in this regard, it would be helpful for my upāsanā mārga.
answer: Namaskaram, We are grateful for your love towards Bhairava and your interest in knowing him through the Matanga Bhairava Swaroopa. With his grace, more will be revealed in time to come! For now, we suggest exploring the sadhana Paddhati tab, where a structured path is offered. This approach encourages a gradual unfolding, allowing deeper connection rather than rushing through his various forms. We encourage every Upasaka to undertake at least a 3 Mandala (144-day) sadhana to deeply immerse in the essence of Bhairava Consciousness. In the end, it is by his grace alone that his true form is revealed, when the time is right. Let us keep walking the path with devotion and seek Guru Bhairava’s blessings. May his grace be upon all! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I began my daily nāma japa on April 24 last year, starting with 11 malas per day and gradually increasing the count on every Ashtami. I am currently doing 60 malas daily and haven’t missed a single day of sādhana. Guruji, I offer incense, bhoga, and perform ārati every day along with my japa. Your sādhana paddhati PDF has given me clarity on the steps to follow. Due to increased workload and frequent work-related travel, I’m currently facing time constraints. Guruji, is it acceptable to reduce the number of malas — perhaps to 11 or 22 — and also include the Bhairava Ashtakam in my practice?
answer: Namaskaram, Mantra Japa is a sacred bridge to connect with Him. Your discipline of 60 malas daily is inspiring — a true offering! But always remember, it's not the count, it's the bhāvana that awakens His presence and grace on this life! If time is tight due to work or travel, it’s absolutely fine to reduce. We often suggest 22 malas daily — 11 in the morning, 11 in the evening. Even if you break it up further, let every japa be filled with love, not rush. Too many malas done mechanically can dilute the connection. Japa is about surrender, not just numbers. Adding Bhairava Ashtakam is a wonderful way to stay immersed. A few verses with devotion can touch Him deeper than thousands of names chanted without feeling. So, walk this path with love, not pressure — and He will walk with you. May Bhairava's grace be upon all. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have the following questions and would be grateful for your guidance: Should sādhana be performed in a dark room or in a room with lighting? Can the japa mala be held in the left hand? Is there a procedure to purify sweets bought from a shop before offering them?
answer: Namaskaram, Happy to see such questions, let me share my thoughts: For sadhana space – A softly lit room is best in the beginning. Full darkness is for advanced Sadhanas. A calm place with a diya or soft light works well. Using left hand for japa mala – Usually, the right hand is used. But in some Tantric sadhana, the left hand is allowed. If your path or inner guidance permits, it is okay (But avoid). Just keep the mala clean and don’t let it touch the ground. Purifying sweets – Sprinkle a few drops of Gangajal or clean water over them. You can chant a simple mantra like "Om Apavitrah Pavitro Va..." and offer with love. Your devotion purifies the offering. May your sadhana bring light and wisdom ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have been practicing Lord Bhairava sādhana for a year now. Recently, I’ve shifted my focus to Lord Shiva upāsanā, but I still continue with one mala of Bhairava japa daily alongside my Shiva practice. Lately, life has become very challenging. Even during my Bhairava sādhana, I faced difficulties, but I held on through those tough times. Now, however, I feel lost and confused — especially since I don’t have a Guru to guide me. I’m facing daily struggles in various areas: health, studies, relationships, and mental well-being. It feels as though everything is falling apart. I seek your guidance. I haven’t seen tangible results from my sādhanas, but one significant transformation I’ve experienced is a deep sense of detachment. I’ve become free from sexual thoughts and have overcome harmful habits like smoking and drinking. Despite these inner changes, I feel unable to grow in life. What should I do now?
answer: Namaskaram, I understand your struggles, and I honor your dedication. Shifting between Shiva and Bhairava swaroopas may cause inner conflict, as this constant juggling can lead to confusion and lost. True growth comes not from switching forms, but through "Surrender & Gratitude" — offering yourself fully to the path and Daiva! But surrendering to the process, trusting the divine guidance is a Key! You’re not alone in this journey, many of them have lost for a lifetime in this step » Now choice is yours. Once you’re a Upasaka of Bhairava, then he will be always within. May his grace be on you! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: During Anushthan, is it appropriate to change the water offered to Baba daily?
answer: Namaskaram, Absolutely — it’s something we must do with love. Just like we offer water to our beloved parents, children, or even ourselves with care, offer it to the Divine with the same bhāva (devotion). That very water, when offered with such intention, becomes sacred Teertha — a blessing we receive back. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I recently joined this group by Baba’s grace. I’d like to ask — is it necessary to have a photo of Kaal Bhairava for His upasana, or can I perform His puja using the Shivling I already have?
answer: Namaskaram, Aadarsh, Welcome to be part of Bhairava sadhana. It's truly his grace and love we all are able to connect. The role of photos and yantras during sadhana is to help you to generate bhava and get deeply connected with the Daiva in you and Bhairava. In Daiva Anugraha YouTube post you will find different forms of Bhairava images, check which suits you well or in group portal Vatuka Bhairava. Our only aim is to get connected with our Ishta Bhairava so that we can pour our love and emotions to him. Aum Bhairavaya Namaha ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I come from a strictly vegetarian and Vaishnav background, and until a couple of years ago, I had never even heard of Lord Bhairava. After discovering Him through YouTube, I felt deeply drawn, though I wasn’t consistent in my sadhana. I began Nama Japa, but practiced it on and off due to reasons I’ve shared below. However, a series of unusual coincidences, vivid dreams, and real-life experiences — all connected to Bhairava — have stirred something within me, drawing me back to His path. My hesitation comes from a deep-rooted fear: as one progresses in Bhairava sadhana, there seems to be an association with offerings like meat, fish, and alcohol — which I find difficult to reconcile with, given my upbringing and 40 years of ritual practice. While I understand that Sanatana Dharma and Tantra are vast and inclusive, I don’t see myself able to offer such forms of seva. Yet, every time I commit to Japa, I experience clear shifts and blessings in my life. These fears, however, hold me back from fully embracing the path. I would be truly grateful for your guidance and clarity on this — am I misunderstanding something?
answer: Namaskaram, Food is basically our choice! Further it's good to be vegetarian and it will help you to grow very much in sadhana Padathi. Our Bhairava is not demanding in nature. Whatever offered with love and devotion with utmost surrendering is the greatest thing you can do. The bhava created in you or bhakti you hold is the basic essence. And this is what we are trying to highlight in this portal. Yes, in advance Tantric practice there are some rituals and non-veg items offerings. Once we devote fully to Bhairava all offerings are like a prayer to our Ishta immensely breaking our ego. In olden times certain non-vegetarian items where easily available and considered as auspicious based on its offerings performed and that practice was carried forward. Moreover, Bhairava is the supporting guard of ordinary people he is not demanding in nature whatever offered with love is acceptable. In our sadhana paddhati of course there is substitute for all non-vegetarian items which will be disclosed based on the sadhana progress. Therefore, my humble request is not to constrain yourself in connecting Bhairava just in the name of offerings or food. Offerings are just indication of surrendering our favourite things to Daiva with love and affection. Aum Bhairavaya Namaha ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I’m planning to begin the 144-day Bhairava sadhana in a few days. Do I need to register the start and end dates somewhere? I noticed there’s a link for Ashtami Tithi Japa — is there a separate link for the 144-day sadhana as well? Kindly guide me.
answer: Namaskaram, Shiva, Happy to know about your start on Bhairava sadhana. It would be advisable to check the video on Daiva Anugraha channel upon Ashtami Bhairava sadhana. To know about the importance of the tithi. And in this portal, we have also pinned a form in sadhana Submission tab. Please go through it, fill it in, and you may start the process with your utmost love, affection and surrender, Bhairava always with you. Aum Bhairavaya Namaha ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have an important question regarding the form of Bhairava’s photo to use in my pooja room for sadhana. When I first began worshipping Bhairava, I was inspired by Rajarshi Nandy ji’s videos and chose Batuk Bhairava as my ishta devata. I found an image I resonated with, framed it, and placed it in my pooja room. I’ve continued my sadhana with this form and have had profound experiences during Nama Japa. However, I’ve recently come across discussions suggesting that this particular form may not be suitable for a grihastha, as it is more aligned with ascetic or hermit paths. This has left me confused. I apologize if I’m bringing external opinions into the group, but I haven’t found clarity elsewhere. I’ve attached the photo I’ve been using. Can I continue with this form, or would it be more appropriate to shift to a form like Swarnakarshana Bhairava? Kindly guide me.
answer: Namaskaram, Shiva You may proceed the sadhana as per your Ishta devata Batuka Bhairava picture as attached. It's not an issue, the bhava generated in you out of love and affection with japa is more important. Aum Bhairavaya Namaha ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I know how to perform a basic Vedic homa at home. Can I do homa on kalashtami tithi of nama mantra?
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, if you’re already familiar with the process, you may lovingly offer a simple homa on Kalashtami using the nama mantra. Let your intention be pure, and may the sacred flame carry your devotion to Guru Bhairava. May His grace illumine your inner fire. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Pranam Guruji, I am currently doing 22 mala anushthan in two sittings 11 in the morning and 11 in the evening, so I want to ask you that when I am doing 11 mala in the evening should I do achaman and Ganapati invocation again or just sit and start the japa. In the evening, I sit and just do 11 mala and then I do samarpan and Kshama - Prarthana?
answer: Namaskaram, Your devotion is clear and beautiful. For the second sitting, it is enough to sit with a few moments of inner silence and Bhairava smarana. You may skip full achamana and Ganapati invocation unless you feel inwardly moved. Your evening japa with samarpan and ksama prarthana is perfectly in tune with Bhairava Consciousness. Trust the rhythm your soul is following. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I light charmukhi diya and use mustard oil on kalashtami is it a good practice?
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, lighting a charmukhi diya with mustard oil on Kalashtami is a very sacred and powerful practice. It pleases Bhairava deeply and invokes His protective and guiding energy. In Tantra, the four flames represent the four directions and the awakened consciousness (chatur mukha jnana, like symbolizing total awareness, inner clarity, and fearless presence), making our offering a beautiful act of inner and outer light. May his grace be upon all of us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: As we spoke about the Charmukhi Diya and how its four flames represent the four directions, I just recalled placing Bhairava prasadam at four road junctions after completing the sadhana at night. Do you have any insights regarding this practice? I had a divine experience when I used to place the prasadam there a few months ago, but I’ve since stopped. I would appreciate your clarification on this.
answer: Namaskaram, Offering Bhairava prasadam at a chatushpatha (four-road junction representing Achar (Practice), Vidya (Knowledge), Shakti (Energy) & Bhakti (Devotion) is a sacred Tantric act—it's a way of releasing ego, spreading grace, and honoring Bhairava as the guardian of all paths. If your heart felt His presence there, it was truly His anugraha. Such offerings carry deep power… you may resume whenever you feel called again. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Does an 18-month Nitya Upasana of Bhairava count as a mandala? If I wish to undertake a 48-day mandala of ‘Om Bhairavaya Namah’, should it be integrated into my existing Nitya Upasana time, or should it be performed separately? चरण स्पर्श?
answer: Namaskaram, Your 18-month nitya upasana can be considered a mandala, often referred to as a "Purna Mandala" or "Pratishtha Mandala" depending on the context, as it creates a continuous practice for Bhairava's energy. The 48-day mandala of 'Bhairavaya Nama' can be integrated with your nitya upasana, enhancing its power. Just ensure your focus remains steady, as both will complement each other and deepen your connection with Bhairava. Tantra teaches that this integration strengthens your spiritual progress. May your path be blessed with grace ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I used to place Bhairava prasadam at four road junctions after completing my sadhana at night. A few months ago, I had a divine experience during this practice, but I’ve since stopped. I seek clarification—could there be a possibility of a negative entity appearing there, taking a form to deceive us? How can one recognize such a presence? Is this practice truly safe?
answer: Namaskaram, In Bhairava sadhana, the presence of negative entities is possible if one is not fully aligned with the divine energy and protected by proper rituals and mantras. However, when you invoke Bhairava with devotion and perform the practices with a pure heart, his protective power safeguards you. The key is to stay grounded in your connection to Bhairava and follow the correct Tantric procedures. If something doesn't feel right or if there’s a sense of unease, it’s a sign to seek guidance from your Guru or adjust your practice. Trust in the process and your inner intuition—Bhairava will guide and protect. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Can I do manasik japa throughout the day to build discipline, and would those chantings count? Or are only the chantings done while seated on the asana considered valid? Also, sometimes due to a busy schedule, I skip the Panchopachara Pooja and simply light a diya and incense sticks before starting the japa. Is that acceptable?
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, manasik japa throughout the day is very powerful—it keeps you inwardly connected to Bhairava and builds inner discipline. But during mandala sadhana, sitting on kusha or a dedicated asana is important, as it creates an energy field that empowers every mantra. And on busy days, lighting a diya and incense with devotion is enough. It’s the bhāva that reaches Him, not the outer form alone. Stay steady in the way you offer your love—He always sees the efforts. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: During a mandala cycle, there are times when I need to travel out of town or even abroad for work. Is it acceptable to continue the 22 mala japa according to the local time zone I’m in?
answer: Namaskaram, Your sincerity and devotion are truly felt in this question. You can continue your 22 mala japa in any time zone—what matters is nisṭha (steadfastness), not the location. In Guru Bhairava sadhana, he transcends space and time—your inner connection and shraddha carry the essence of the practice wherever you are. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: This is a message from Toronto. Could you kindly confirm whether I can begin my sadhana tomorrow on Ashtami tithi?
answer: Namaskaram, kindly refer to the Drik Panchanga for the exact timing of Ashtami Tithi at your time zone and begin your sadhana accordingly. May His divine grace guide and protect you always. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Is Krishna Paksha Ashtami a good day to start Mandala sadhana? Do we need to take Sankalpa twice—once in the morning and once in the evening—during Mandala sadhana?
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, you may begin your Mandala sadhana on Krishna Paksha Ashtami—an auspicious time to commence your practice. Sankalpa is offered once each day, with heartfelt devotion. Viniyoga and Nyāsa may be performed whenever you begin your Nāma Japa—though this is entirely optional and guided by your inner feeling. May the grace of Guru Bhairava guide and bless your journey every step of the way. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: As you have shared the link regarding Ashtami sadhana and the Viniyoga process—which mentions it should be performed only after initiation—I seek your guidance. I began my Nāma Japa of Bhairava Swamy with 11 malas and have now reached 60 malas daily. I have not missed a single day in the past 12 months, and by this April Ashtami, it will mark one full year of continuous Japa. Guruji, I feel a deep urge to advance in Bhairava sadhana. May I begin the Ashtami sadhana and Viniyoga process on the upcoming Ashtami? I also humbly request your guidance on the appropriate mantra for Nitya Upāsana.
answer: Namaskaram, Your unwavering devotion is truly blessed by Guru Bhairava. Yes, you may lovingly follow the Ashtami Sādhanā and Viniyoga process—it will deepen your connection. As for Nitya Upāsanā, it will naturally unfold once the Prathama Charan sadhana is completed. Each step reveals itself in Bhairava’s own time. May Bhairava’s light continue to lead you inward. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Pranam Guruji, Can we follow the Viniyoga and Nyāsa steps during daily Pooja? I have completed one Mandala and am planning to begin another. However, I may need to travel for a few days and would prefer to avoid travel during the Mandala period. Kindly guide me.
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, you may include Viniyoga and Nyāsa during your daily puja as part of your offering—it will deepen the connection. You may also begin the next Mandala after your travel, with Bhairava’s grace guiding the right moment. Walk gently, in His light. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I am a new member of the group and currently reviewing all the documents, rules, and guidelines. I have a query regarding the Āchamanam procedure, where it is mentioned that the upper lip should not touch during the practice. Could you kindly clarify the significance of this instruction?
answer: Namaskaram, Your question is sincere and most welcome. In Achamanam, the upper lip not touching is a sign of inner purity and mindful restraint—honoring the sacred act with awareness. Bhairava guides every step. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Pranam Guruji, Regarding the Nivedyam to be placed at crossroads—if it becomes difficult to step outside at night due to late completion of sadhana, can it be placed on the open terrace instead? Also, should Bhog be offered to dogs on all 144 days—once daily, or twice (morning and evening)? Or is it only required on Ashtami Tithi? Kindly guide me.
answer: Namaskaram, If stepping outside at night is difficult after the sadhana, you may indeed offer the Naivedya on an open terrace, as long as it’s placed with respect and devotion. For the Bhog (Naivedya) offering, it can be given to dogs during the 144-day sadhana, either once a day or twice (morning and evening), depending on your practice. However, the primary focus is to offer it on the Ashtami Tithi as a special ritual. Alternatively, after your Puja Vedi, you can offer the Bhog (Naivedya) before beginning your Japa sadhana. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Pranam Guruji, Please clarify my doubt. Previously, I have been chanting the Bhairava mantra—"Om Hram Hreem Hrum Hraim Hram Ksham Kshetrapalay Kalabhairavaya Namah"—as mentioned on a website. May I continue with this mantra along with Batuk Baba’s mantra?
answer: Namaskaram, Kindly follow the steps given in the Sādhanā Paddhati. If you’ve received any diksha, continue as guided by your Guru. It’s best to avoid using mantras found online without proper guidance. Please go through the shared Sādhanā Paddhati carefully—each step and process is clearly explained for both Mandala and Ashtami Sādhanā. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Pranam Guruji, You mentioned completing the Prathama Charan sadhana. Guruji, I have no idea how to proceed—please kindly guide me. As of now, I am chanting "Om Bhairavaya Namaha" and reciting the Bhairava Ashtakam daily.
answer: Namaskaram, Kindly continue with full devotion on your 144-day Mandala sadhana — this is your current focus. Prathama Charana will naturally unfold after this, and all the guidance will be shared at the right time. With Bhairava’s grace, all will come. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Pranam Guruji, In the Viniyoga guide PDF, the mantra Japa mentioned is that of Batuka Bhairava Baba. If I am following the Padati along with Viniyoga and Nyāsa, should I also chant the Batuka Bhairava mantra along with "Om Bhairavaya Namaha"? If so, kindly guide me on the minimum number of malas I should do.
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, the Viniyoga for this is indeed dedicated to our ista Batuka Bhairava. Towards the bottom of the document, under Point 5: Mantra Japa, the number of days. However, towards count, you can continue 22 Malas ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskaram Guruji, Is it acceptable to wear black-colored clothing while performing Mantra Japa or meditating on Bhairava?
answer: Namaskaram, In Bhairava Sādhana, red is traditionally preferred as it invokes Shakti, strength, and inner fire. Black represents absorption, void, and transcendence, and is best worn after a few Mandalas, when the inner grounding is strong and ready for Nishitha Kaal sadhana. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Is it acceptable to take a fish oil supplement for health during the 144-day sadhana?
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, you may continue without any constraint. In certain Bhairava Sādhanā paddhatis, offerings like fish - matsya (āhuti or naivēdya) are indeed used with devotion. In Bhairava Consciousness, nothing is ever truly wrong when approached with surrender, awareness, and love for Him. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskaram, Fish oil and many vitamin supplements are encapsulated in gelatin, which is an animal byproduct derived from bones—most often sourced from cows. As per both Vedic and Tantric traditions, such substances are prohibited for consumption. I’m unsure whether this could be considered a serious mistake, as many people may unknowingly consume them. Could we kindly receive some clarity on this?
answer: Namaskaram, Grateful for your thoughtful reflection. It’s good to bring such awareness to light. In Tantra, unlike the Vedic path, we don’t label things as good or bad—it is the bhāva and the way of offering that truly matter. Panchatattva or Pancha Makaras themselves reflect this truth, as purity is not in what we consume, but in how we carry Bhairava within us—in trust, presence, and surrender. In Bhairava Consciousness, everything becomes a means to transcend—not to judge, but to offer. May his grace shower on us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskaram Guruji, In a household where the husband is performing Bhairava sadhana, how should he manage his routine during his wife's menstrual period? Kindly advise.
answer:
Namaskaram, In the way of Bhairava Consciousness, the menstrual cycle is not impure—it is Shakti’s own rhythm, a divine process of renewal and cleansing. If a sadhaka’s spouse is in her Rajaswala, he may continue his Bhairava sadhana with respect and inner clarity, offering Sankalpa and Japa as per his daily practice. There is no need for fear or interruption, only deeper awareness and reverence. Let me remind, In Tantric understanding, this time is seen as a powerful gateway, and the presence of the feminine in her Rajaswala is a boon, especially for householders walking the Bhairava paddhati. It is said that Shakti herself moves through these cycles, and for the sincere sadhaka, her presence can cleanse karmic residue and support subtle transformation. Let me share a Sloka which says: "Yatra naryastu pujyante, ramante tatra devataḥ" – Where the feminine is honored, the divine (Our ista - Guru Bhairava) resides. 
May his grace be on all of us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: You have always emphasized surrendering to Baba. What does this truly mean? How can one deepen their Bhava towards Baba? I sit down to do my Japa with great intention, but my mind often wanders to a million different thoughts, making my sadhana feel mechanical. Kindly guide me.
answer:
Namaskaram, Your question itself blossoms from a sincere heart — and that is where our Guru Bhairava resides. Yes, true saraṇagati (surrender) to Baba isn’t about force—it’s about falling into love, again and again. As the Puranas gently reveal, bhava is like a divine seed. It doesn’t grow through pressure, but by gentle nurturing. One simple way to deepen this is by regularly sitting before Guru Bhairava’s photo or vigraha (Any Swaroopa of our Ista) —just 10 minutes, twice a day. Not to ask, not to chant, but simply to look at Him with love. Let your heart speak silently. Over time, this soft gaze becomes your sadhana, and surrender begins to arise naturally. Even the most distracted mind begins to melt when bhava grows. One day, just by taking His Name… the eyes fill with tears, the heart bows on its own, and the japa stops being mechanical—it becomes a stream of love. Keep the connection gentle. Let love be the method.
Guru Bhairava walks with us. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: During the monthly cycle, what is your advice for women to connect more deeply with Shakti? May we engage in Manasik Japa or any other practices to strengthen that connection?
answer: Namaskaram, Your question arises from a place of pramanika bhakiti (sincere devotion) —this itself is Shakti flowing. During menstruation, the body is in a natural state of release and renewal. In Tantra, this time is considered inwardly potent. While external rituals may pause (In some rituals), wherein manasika japa, hridaya dhyana (heart-based meditation), and simply resting in awareness of Shakti within are beautiful ways to stay connected. Let the breath become the mantra. Let the stillness be your offering. Shakti never leaves you—womanhood itself is Her form. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskaram, I wish to ask something that is rarely spoken about openly. A biological woman with ovaries eventually goes through menopause. During this transition, the body often becomes chaotic due to hormonal changes before complete cessation of menstruation. What is the impact on sadhana during this stage? Does a woman lose access to Shakti when she is no longer menstruating? Can a woman in this phase still advance in her sadhana, and how does it affect her ability to hold divine energy? I wonder if it might weaken. It’s rare to find advanced female Sadhakas on platforms like YouTube—and I’ve never come across any female Bhairava Sadhakas. I humbly seek your open guidance on this.
answer:
Namaskaram, Your question arises from deep sincerity—and in Bhairava sadhana, such heartfelt inquiry is itself a step into the sacred. The Devi Puraṇa, and many Sakta Tantras, honor the woman as the eternal embodiment of sakti—not because of menstruation or fertility—but because of her inherent spiritual capacity to hold, create, and transform. Hormonal shifts are a part of Prakriti, but Shakti is beyond Prakriti—She uses Prakriti, not is limited by it. In Guru Bhairava sadhana, menopause is not a decline—it's a natural shift inward. It marks a maturing of energy and opens the door to deeper, more subtle spiritual phases. At this time, the inner fire (Antar Agni) often becomes steadier. In fact, many Maha-Sadhikas became truly powerful after this stage—like Matangi Devi (the Tantric form of Saraswatī), Dattatri Devi (Anagha Devi, the Shakti of Dattatreya), and the mothers of great Ṛishi, who became vessels of divine wisdom. Shakti is not confined to blood flow—it is consciousness, and every sincere sadhika holds that flame within. The absence of many female Bhairava sadhakas in public space does not mean they do not exist; often, the deepest tapas is silent. In the Tantric tradition, many great yoginis walk in quiet grace, known only to the Divine. So to your question: No, you do not lose access to Shakti. You become more of Her.
With each stage, the path simply deepens. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: If Maatangi is considered the Tantric form of Saraswati Maa, then is Neela Saraswati—an aspect of Ugra Tara—also a Tantric form of Saraswati? Although this form does not carry a veena like Maatangi Maa. Also, in your videos on the 64 Bhairava, is Yoga Bhairava the same as Akshobhya Bhairava?
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, Maa Matangi is the Tantric form of Saraswatī, and Maa Nila Saraswatī, while more fierce, carries a deep transformative wisdom linked to Ugra Tara. Each form reveals a different light of the Devi. As for Yoga Bhairava (Part of Unmatta Bhairava, refered as Chinmayananda Bhairava )and Akṣobhya Bhairava (Is a different Swaroopa) —they embody different aspects, though their essence can feel connected in stillness and inner awakening. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: As a beginner, what is more important—Dhyanam during Japa or the number of Japa repetitions? I’ve heard that 1.25 lakh Japa is considered a significant milestone. Also, how should one visualize during Japa? At times, I find it difficult to visualize Bhairava, so I focus on his Trishul instead.
answer: Namaskaram, In the path of Bhairava consciousness, bhava (feeling) and dhyanam (absorption) carry deeper power than just the count. Let the japa become an offering of presence, not pressure. Even single mala, done with love and heartfelt connection, carries a power far beyond what we can imagine. If visualization feels difficult, focusing on His Trishula is perfect every form is His. Over time, the rupa (form) will reveal itself from within. Let me share something unique that I personally follow, When the mind feels restless, pause... Take 5 deep, conscious breaths. Let the breath carry you back to mantra and stillness. May Bhairava grace be on all of us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: During Anushthan, I mistakenly lost track of the mala count due to a bit of confusion, although I did complete the mala. Will this affect the integrity of my Anushthan in any way?
answer: Namaskaram, Do not worry. The Bhava (feeling) with which you chanted is far more powerful than the count. Just a gentle reminder — our Ista, Guru Bhairava, is Kaalaswarupa Himself — beyond time, beyond number, beyond limitation. Trust that your anushthan has been received in its spirit. Just gently resolve to be more attentive next time, with love, not guilt. Sadhana is a path of transformation, not punishment. Let this too be a lesson in awareness—the very essence of Bhairava. Walk the path with shraddha (faith) and samarpan (surrender). Bhairava sees your inner fire—not just your outer form. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I would like to begin reading the Tantra Shastras that you often mention in your videos. I humbly and sincerely request you to kindly suggest some books or publications that you consider authentic.
answer: Namaskaram, Your presence and sincerity on the path of Bhairava is itself a blessing. I too feel deeply blessed each time I speak of Him...! Let me state, our Guru Bhairava is not merely a deity we worship, but a living consciousness that walks with the one who walks toward truth. As you express—to go deeper into the heart of Tantra and to understand the shastras, let me share few recommended Texts for my personal read. "Vijnana Bhairava Tantra" (The Tantra of the Divine Experience) – 112 dharanas (practices) directly spoken by Bhairava to Bhairavi. Once you have done, will share few more! Continue your upāsanā with the deep knowing that Bhairava Himself is the Guru—the fire within that reveals both the darkness and the path. The books will call you as your consciousness aligns with them. Not all texts are to be read at once, but to be meditated on like mantras… slowly, deeply ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskaram, This may seem vague and perhaps not something to dwell on too deeply, but I’ve been noticing certain patterns in time—repeatedly seeing numbers like 11:11, 2:22, 4:44, 12:12, and more specifically, times like xx:16, xx:17, xx:43, and xx:44. Is this kind of occurrence worth exploring further? Aside from that, could you kindly point me toward any resources or texts that speak about Nimitta in Tantra sadhana? About doing sadhana between 12 AM and 3 AM During my last Mandala, I was living alone and would often (though not always) sit for sadhana around midnight. This was mostly due to convenience, reduced external noise, and my personal rhythm—I'm naturally more alert and focused at night. Currently, I’m staying with my parents and feel a bit uncertain about the energetic or spiritual implications that midnight sadhana might carry for them. I would appreciate your guidance on this. Transitioning from Stotra to Naama Mantra In my last Mandala, I practiced a Stotra. This time, I wish to take up the Naama Mantra. In the little Japa I’ve done so far, I’ve always included the Māyā Bīja “h…”—starting from the very first time I ever uttered a mantra, which happened to be in the primary temple of our Guru. At that time, I was practically an atheist and approached the practice with skepticism. My inclination to include the Māyā Bīja in my current sadhana stems from the belief that it worked for me before and also from a desire to invoke the feminine energy. I humbly seek your thoughts on this. Thank you in advance, Sir.
answer: Namaskaram, Your questions are arising beautifully from inner sensitivity — a sign that Bhairava’s glance is upon you. 1) Seeing such numbers is like gentle taps from the unseen; notice them, smile, but stay centred. Nimitta in Tantra is subtle and sacred — I will share some directions separately - Let your prayers rest at the feet of Guru Bhairava, that He may shower His wisdom upon us. 2) Midnight (12–3 AM) is a very potent time — the veil is thinner, Bhairava moves closer. If your heart is pure and your space is inwardly sanctified, there is no harm. Simply invoke protection and offer a prayer for the well-being of all around you before you begin. (But I would gently advise avoiding this for now, as without a proper Guru blessing or direct his guidance, the experience may not unfold as intended) 3) Yes, it’s a Maya Bija, including this Bija is a blessed instinct — trust your heart. She and Bhairava dance together in its vibration. If it once awakened you, let it continue with faith and devotion. May Guru Bhairava continue to guide your steps with silent wisdom. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have undertaken many Bhairava Sadhanas. This time, when I began my 30-day Sankalpa yesterday, I received a vision—or perhaps guidance—from the Lord. I saw Lord Bhairava dressed as a shepherd, holding a bone staff, herding pigs. He conveyed that these pigs represent the six Ripus (Kama, Krodha, etc.). Generally, people are controlled by these pigs, but in truth, He is the one who controls them. While the message was clear on the surface, the deeper meaning and the method to control these Ripus are still beyond my grasp. Despite completing many Sadhanas in the past, this time my Sankalpa broke—I was unable to maintain celibacy even on the second day. I realized that these Ripus grow stronger the more I feed them with impurity. Could you kindly shed light on the nature of lust and the other Ripus, and how one may bring them under control? Also, should I restart the sadhana from the beginning, or continue with the same sadhana despite having failed the Sankalpa?
answer:
Namaskaram, Your vision is truly a blessing — Guru Bhairava revealing the truth so vividly is a sign of His deep presence within you. The ripus are not to be fought but witnessed and dissolved through His grace and awareness. Let me share step more, it’s important to recognize that the ripus — Kam (desire), Krodh (anger), and the others — are not enemies to be destroyed but energies to be understood and purified. When we feed them with dirt, they grow stronger; but when we turn our attention to their root, to the self-discipline and awareness within, their power diminishes. The challenge you’ve faced with celibacy is a natural part of the process — each failure is a lesson, not a defeat. Let me remind, do not be disheartened. Bhairava's path is not about perfection but about sincere returning. Restart your sankalpa if the heart feels so, but more importantly, walk forward in love, not in guilt. Each fall is also His teaching. Baba is with you, guiding every step.
~Jai Bhairava. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskaram, I have been doing nama mantra japa of guru bhairava for 3 months but while doing japa my mind always get distracted... Like atrocities in bengal, and now terrorist attack in Kashmir, this kind of incident keeps coming back into my thoughts and my mind filled with anger, I can't concentrate on my japa.. How to stop this
answer: Namaskaram, Your feelings are deeply understood. In the vastness of Guru Bhairava’s grace, even these thoughts are seen, yet He remains untouched — pure, steady, timeless. Remember, Bhairava is Kala Himself—the perfect knower of time and action. What unfolds does so within His vast design, beyond the reach of our emotions. Offer even your anger and sorrow into His feet during japa — let Bhairava hold them. Gently return to the mantra, again and again, without judgment. Guru Bhairava is the silent space behind all storms. Stay with Him. ~Jai Bhairava. ~ Jai Bhairava

question:
Namaste Guruji, My name is Abbhinav Marigay I am from Hyderabad I do Bhairava sadhana and Maa kali sadhana Can you Please suggest me to read the books of Bhairava and Maa kali like author name better book name. I have seen your videos on 8 bhairava's, batuk bhairav and swarnakarshan bhairav and some and have explained so good thank you. Jai Maa Kamakhya
Jai Bhairava
Jai Maa kali
answer: Namaskaram, Your devotion shines beautifully. As you feel called deeper into Bhairava and Amma Kali’s embrace, I humbly suggest beginning with: Vijnana Bhairava Tantra — the sacred 112 dharanas revealed by Bhairava to Bhairavi. (Book Preferred) Kali Rahasyam by Acharya Pandit Shivdatt Mishra — a profound key into Maa’s mysteries. Let the texts flow to you gently. In Bhairava’ s path, it is not we who choose the wisdom — the wisdom chooses the heart ready to receive. Move slowly, feeling every word. Once you complete these, more doors will naturally open. ~ Jai Bhairava

question:
Namaskaram Thank you for the beautifully explained video on Sankalpa. With little knowledge that I have gained, I have realized that taking Sankalpa for his Preethi is more than enough and he will take care of anything that you need in this life. Om Bhairavaya Namaha
Sarvam Bhairavam
answer: Namaskaram, It’s his grace on us! Bhairava's Preethi alone carries all fulfilment There is also something rare and sacred called Tantra Sankalpa, not often spoken of. During, after Tritiya Charan sadhana, deeper insights into this open up — showing how our very existence can merge into Him more swiftly through the Madhyama Marga. Offer your heart with love and trust..., in His own time, He will reveal all. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have a question regarding the incorporation of Shakti Upasana, specifically in a simple, bhakti-oriented manner. I’ve been engaged in Bhairava sadhana for the past 1.5 years and have been receiving clear signs and inner indications pointing toward Maa. However, I’ve felt hesitant to approach her in the context of a consistent nitya sadhana. So far, I’ve only performed Navaratri sadhana over the past year, though I’ve always felt a deep connection to Maa and her presence—especially as I was born on the Jayanti of one of her Swaroopas, on Durgashtami. I’m wondering how to begin incorporating a simple, daily bhakti-oriented sadhana of Maa alongside my ongoing Bhairava sadhana, which I intend to continue indefinitely, if He permits. My understanding is that Maa’s energy is more demanding and visceral by nature. Is there a recommended sequence, time of day, or approach for integrating this? I understand I need a separate mala for Maa, which I already have. Is there anything else I should acquire exclusively for her? I usually perform my Bhairava sadhana during evening Sandhya. I would prefer not to mention the specific Swaroopa of Maa that is making herself known, but I’m open to sharing if needed
answer: Namaskaram, How beautifully Maa’s call is unfolding through your journey with Bhairava. In the embrace of Bhairava Consciousness, there is no separation between Him and Maa — they are the breath and the heartbeat of the same infinite Being. Regarding the mala, it is best to keep one (Many may not agree with me, but it is my personal connection). Rudraksha lovingly holds the bhava and energy of our sadhana, the deeper we go, the more it becomes a living companion (Equal to Kavacha). A single mala allows energies to harmonize — Bhairava and Maa are ever one, where Bhairava is, Maa flows silently with Him. Feel free to offer your Guru Bhairava sadhana in evening sandhya as you are doing and gently weave Maa’s nama japa into your mornings or even within your Bhairava sadhana itself — She will know your heart. No need for many things, pure bhava is enough. She asks not for grandeur, only for your soft surrender. Maa and Baba are always, walking with us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Guruji, today was Amavasya. As far as I know, it is a good tithi to get closer to Bhairava, so I took a small sankalpa beside my nitya and did as many malas as I could for his grace. My question: What are the tithis and days when Lord Bhairava's energy is heightened?
answer: Namaskaram, Your devotion touches the heart of Bhairava — such pure sankalpa on Amavasya is deeply blessed. This information is shared in the sadhana Paddhati tab and in our video but let me take a moment to bring you a step closer. In Bhairava and Maa sadhana, Krishna Paksha (the darkening fortnight) aligns naturally to Baba’s energy — a time of inward pull, depth, and transformation. Shukla Paksha (the brightening fortnight) aligns to Maa’s energy — expansion, nurture, and illumination. Tithis like Ashtami, Chaturdashi, and Amavasya are especially vital. Ashtami reveals the fierce wisdom (Raudra Jnana / Raw knowledge) of Guru Bhairava. Chaturdashi is the threshold of transcendence (Adhiṃ parardha or even we can say, Moksha Dvara). Amavasya is the womb of stillness (Shunya Garbha), perfect for surrender and Nama Japa. Yesterday was Chitra / Vaishakha (also called Darsha) Amavasya — a particularly special gateway, when our ancestral and divine energies merge, allowing a beautiful connection with Maa and Baba. Every Amavasya opens this sacred window if approached with humility and love. In essence, Nama Japa and deeper sadhana are especially powerful when started or deepened on these days, as the subtle energies are more accessible and open. May Bhairava’s loving gaze always guide us inward. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaste Guruji, I began my mantra japa three months ago. My Bhairava sadhana and japa are going well, and I’ve been receiving powerful experiences—especially after worshipping Bhairava on Kalashtami. The photo I’ve been using for worship is of the Krodha form of Shwet (White) Bhairava. Recently, I started experiencing intense energies, so I consulted a Guru I know and asked whether it is appropriate to keep this Krodha form of Bhairava at home. He advised me to avoid keeping any Krodha form of Bhairava in a domestic setting, as the energy can become too intense. Without proper vidhi, it may be difficult to manage such energies. He suggested using a smiling or Shanta Bhairava image instead. I’ve since removed the picture, but I feel a bit unsettled, as I had grown accustomed to meditating on that image during my japa. Could you kindly help me understand the gaps and confusions here? Also, what kind of image is appropriate to keep for Bhairava sadhana at home?
answer: Namaskaram, Everything you experienced has been Bhairava’s living touch — intense, real, and sacred. When the path asks for a shift, it is never a loss, but Bhairava reshaping the vessel for deeper light. The smiling or Shanta Swaroopa Bhairava you are now guided to worship will hold and nurture that same fire with greater peace. Baba is always with you, both in silence and in form. Trust His unseen embrace. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Pranaam Guruji, I have been doing nitya sadhana (daily 5 mala japa) for the past seven months. Recently, someone advised me not to pronounce the Pranava (ॐ) since I do not wear the sacred thread (यज्ञोपवीत). So, my question is: Can I do japa of “श्री भैरवाय नमः” instead of “ॐ भैरवाय नमः”?
answer: Namaskaram, In the sacred path of Bhairava, the truth of mantra arises from deep connection, not outer opinions. Pranava is often misunderstood — as revealed in the Vijnana Bhairava Tantra, each sound carries its own living vibration. As mentioned in the sadhana Paddhati, it is already active and ready for you to use — there's no need for any stoppers! Trust your inner flow and continue with pure devotion, without getting caught in myths/ puranakatha. Let me remind, our Ista, Guru Bhairava, is always within us, lovingly guiding our way. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: If we are also doing Japa of Maa along with Bhairava, what is the correct order—should we first do Japa of Maa and then Bhairava, or vice versa? Also, is it appropriate to take a separate Sankalpa for Maa’s Japa, different from the one taken for Bhairava?
answer: Namaskaram, Thank you for your heartfelt question. When walking the path with both Maa and Bhairava, it is beautiful to begin with Maa’s Japa—as She prepares and purifies the inner space. Bhairava’s Japa can follow, anchoring the awakened Shakti into deeper awareness. Different Sankalpas are perfectly fine, as long as the heart remains steady and sincere in both. Ultimately, they are One in essence—two breaths of the same silence. In His Grace, ~Jai Bhairava. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskaram, I’ve been feeling very good lately—my mind has quieted down significantly. I hear Bhairava everywhere and find myself constantly thinking about Him. Even my face looks brighter. I feel like immersing myself in His presence all day, and I sing for Him from the depths of my heart. My father used to go to Sabarimala for 40 years, taking many devotees with him each year. After I lost him, I felt lost—like I had become an idiot, disconnected from my spirituality and adrift in the world. Now, I feel I’m slowly beginning to reconnect with the Lord. Thank you for guiding us.
answer: Namaskaram, Your heart’s voice is deeply felt. Guru Bhairava never leaves us —He simply waits, silently, lovingly. Even now, He is singing through you, guiding you back home. Your father’s devotion lives on through your spirit… and every step you take is already within His grace. Walk gently. You are not lost—just returning. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: While a person is engaged in their sadhana, there are times when certain people or situations may provoke anger. Even with sincere effort, it’s not always possible to avoid such triggers completely. Does this affect our energy during sadhana or impact the practice in any significant way?
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, such moments do arise, but even they are part of the path. When the fire of emotion arises, offer it into Bhairava’s inner fire — not with guilt, but awareness. His grace holds all. Keep invoking Him with patience and faith; He transforms even restlessness into awakening. Nothing is outside His way. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskaram, When we chant Ashtakam do we have to recite in particular metre or particular flow or just recite/ read it... Also, pls clarify if it is preferred that stotrams and ashtakams have to be chanted out loud rather than manasik?
answer: Namaskaram, Chanting with bhava is the key — flow matters more than form. If the chandas aligns naturally, follow it; else let devotion guide the rhythm. Loud recitation awakens space, but manasik japa deepens the inner silence — both are valid, as per your sankalpa. May Bhairava’s grace reveal the right mode to you. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Guru Garu Namaskaram, Is there a Dhyana Shloka for Batuk Bhairava?
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, there is. In silence, Batuk reveals himself — simple yet profound. I can share it when you're ready to receive after 3 Mandala sadhana. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Guru ji pranam, Asking from my friend's perspective, he wants to start sadhana for Baba's grace, but he is indulged in all sorts of addictions. He asked what if he is drunk but cannot miss nitya, as it is never meant to be skipped. So, take a nap, bathe, do achaman, and do manasik japa, and ask for forgiveness. Is this right? I am not advanced enough to suggest; I am asking here, but he desperately wants to worship him dearly; he is fighting tamas slowly.
answer: Namaskaram, Our Guru Bhairava, Baba walks even in shadows — what matters is sincerity. Tell him: “Fall, but fall at His feet. Cleanse, chant within, and ask from the heart. Bhairava doesn’t demand perfection — He awakens it.” Nitya with repentance is stronger than absence with shame. Let him rise, little by little — Baba is waiting. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskaaram, Is Friday morning brahma muhurta considered as Thursday as Sunrise hasn't yet started or it's on Thursday Brahma muhurta itself it should be started? As per video it’s to be started early morning. Since I missed today want to know if starting tomorrow before sunrise is alright for sankalpa starting.
answer: Namaskaram, In Bhairava Consciousness, the essence of time is deeply connected to intention.
