If you missed the Brahmamuhurta on Thursday, beginning the Sankalpa before sunrise on Friday is absolutely fine. The key is to align your inner focus and devotion with the moment. Trust in the divine timing and let your practice begin with sincerity and connection. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskaram, in your last video you spoke about doing a certain pranayama technique before visualisation of Lord bhaiarava in the Ajna chakra (at night before bed) to activate yogic awareness - can you disclose this pranayama technique please
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, the breath is the key. The technique is Kumbhaka with awareness on the space between the brows—gentle inhale, hold with silence. This pranayama is subtle but deep. Inhale slowly through the nose (4 counts) Hold the breath (Kumbhaka) for 4–8 counts—in this stillness, bring awareness to the Ajna chakra Gently visualize Bhairava as formless radiance or your Ishta swaroopa of Guru Bhairava Exhale slowly (4 counts) Repeat for 7–11 rounds in silence. Let the breath dissolve… let Bhairava arise in the space between thoughts. This still breath becomes the gateway to Bhairava Consciousness awareness. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Whenever I meditate or do Japa sadhana, I often enter a trance-like state. I would like to understand more about this experience. Why do we go into trance during such practices?
answer: Namaskaram, Such states are glimpses of Guru Bhairava within—when ego dissolves, and pure presence remains. Trans happens when mind surrenders, its grace touching your being. Stay with it, without fear. Let it guide you deeper. Bhairava is near! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Upon completing 48 days (the first Mandala), is there anything special that needs to be done on the concluding day—specifically on the 48th day? Also, can I begin the next Mandala with a count of 11 malas instead of 22? I found 22 malas a bit overwhelming. Is it acceptable to start the next Mandala the very next day, or is it recommended to take a gap of two to three days before resuming?
answer: Namaskaram, Once you complete 48 days (1st Mandala) of Naama Japa, simply offer your japa with gratitude and love—no elaborate ritual is needed unless your heart feels called. A deep prarthana (silent prayer) and inner offering to Guru Bhairava is enough. About continuing — yes, you may begin the next Mandala the very next day. If 22 malas feel intense, 11 malas is completely fine. What matters is steadiness and Bhava. The idea behind 22 malas for 48 days holds a sacred significance — it completes a divine count of 1056 malas, which carries its own spiritual vibration and reason rooted, in time, as you step into the Dwitiya Charana sadhana, the deeper reason will gently reveal itself to your inner knowing. For full clarity, please refer to the sadhana Paddhati tab where Mandala details and transitions are given. May His grace shower on us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: What does Dwitiya Charana sadhana entail? The last video was helpful in understanding Guru Bhairava as the guardian of our body. My question is: If He alone can grant liberation, what is the purpose of Shakti sadhana? I’m also seeking clarification on how Shakti sadhana may differ for men and women. Additionally, I’ve noticed that although Kala Bhairava is associated with cemeteries or cremation grounds, His vahana is a dog. Are there any risks involved in chanting His nama during Japa?
answer: Namaskaram, Dwitiya Charana sadhana is the second stage, which naturally unfolds after the 144 days of Prathama Charana sadhana—which itself follows the foundational 144-day Sankalpa sadhana, as detailed in the sadhana Paddhati tab. Guru Bhairava is the ground—Kshetra—on which Shakti descends. Without the grounding presence of Bhairava, Shakti cannot stabilize. He is not just the guardian of the body, but also the stillness into which all movement resolves. Shakti sadhana arises once Bhairava is awakened within. For men and women, the outer form may differ slightly, but inwardly it's the same: Shakti seeks to merge into Bhairava, and Bhairava holds her in silence. As for chanting Guru Bhairava’s nama —even though He rides a dog and dwells in smashana—He is the purifier, not the defiler. He is fire, but He burns away only what is false. Walk gently. The path unfolds. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: When performing sadhana during a Mandala, how many mala japa are considered compulsory?
answer: Namaskaram, For mandala sadhana, minimum 11 mala daily is essential for 48 Days — but more with bhava deepens the connection. Please refer to the sadhana Paddhati tab for detailed steps if you take Anustana and Sankalpa. And revisit the earlier chats for deeper insights. ~Jai Bhairava. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I think what’s confusing for me is that I was already praying to Shakti—like Durga and Mariamman—before this wave of Bhairava sadhana emerged on social media. I came to know that Bhairava sadhana would be beneficial for me through an astrological recommendation, especially due to the influence of Shani, who is also my beneficiary. Social media further supported this with information about Him. Tantra seems very structured, yet also confusing—because unlike academic schooling, we’re not all starting from the same foundational level. What adds to the confusion now is: which form of Shakti should one align with? If it’s Kala Bhairava, does that mean it must be Maha Kaali? Can one continue Durga sadhana while also connecting deeply with Kala Bhairava? The generic Nama Japa felt mechanical to me, but once I shifted to chanting Kala Bhairava’s name, something changed—it no longer felt mechanical. Is it okay to explore these paths experientially, or is one expected to strictly follow a structured approach? So many questions—my apologies.
answer: Namaskaram, I feel it's meaningful to go a little deeper into this at this point! Also, let me reminder, there is no need to apologize—for sincere questions are the first offerings at the feet of our Ista, Guru Bhairava. They arise not from confusion but from the inner stirring of awakening. Yes, Tantra appears structured, but it does not demand that we all begin from the same point. Unlike academic learning, the spiritual path is spiral, not linear. What you feel—this shift from mechanical to meaningful when chanting KaalBhairava's nama—is already a sign that the path is choosing you, just as much as you are choosing it. To your question: Can one walk with Durga and yet serve KaalBhairava? Absolutely, yes. Bhairava is not separate from Shakti—he is her guardian, her secret, and her flame. Durga, Mariamman, Kaali, they are not in conflict. Here, we need to know that our Guru Bhairava dances in the very heart of Durga's roar and Kaali's silence. The form you are drawn to—whether Durga or Kaali—is the Shakti expression your soul recognizes. Let that bond deepen without fear. Guru Bhairava sadhana does not mean turning away from Durga. It means descending more deeply into her fierce grace with the guidance of Bhairava's unwavering presence. You are not doing anything "wrong" by learning experientially. In fact, Guru Bhairava loves the one who experiences, not the one who merely follows structure blindly. The path reveals itself to the one who walks it with courage and surrender. So yes, trial, feel, observe the inner responses—this is how Tantra lives. When you chant this Nama that moves your being, that is the structure being revealed to you. May your path unfold with clarity and divine. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I began my Bhairava sadhana some time ago with “Om Bhairavaya Namah.” After completing 1.25 lakh japa, I started Batuka sadhana—chanting the Apaduddaraka Mantra and Nama Mantra, along with recitation of the Batuk Bhairava Brahma Kavach. I'm new to your group and was previously unaware of the Mandala sadhana. My question is: while performing Mandala sadhana, should I pause my Batuka mantra recitations and focus solely on the basic Bhairava mantra? Or is it acceptable to perform the Mandala while continuing with the Batuka Bhairava mantras?
answer: Namaskaram, Your sadhana is already blessed — from Om Bhairavaya Namaha to Batuka’s Apaduddaraka, Nama Japa, and Brahma Kavach. These are not separate steps, but deeper movements into Bhairava’s grace. For Mandala sadhana, you don’t need to stop Batuka mantra. Instead, let the foundational Bhairava mantra anchor your daily practice, while Batuka Japa continues as a sacred limb (anga) — especially if Batuka Bhairava has become your inner guide. Think of the basic mantra as the lamp and Batuka Japa as the incense — both vital in sanctifying the inner temple. Just ensure your focus during the Mandala sadhana is one-pointed, grounded in a clear sankalpa — whether for grace, protection, or deepening darshan. Align all limbs of your practice toward that intention. Our Guru Bhairava does not bind. He expands. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Can a Yatha Shakti Sankalpa be taken by beginners to complete 1 lakh japa in 48 days—so that on days when it’s difficult to find time, we can do what’s possible without breaking the Sankalpa? If we succeed in this, it might be a little easier to attempt 11 malas for the 144-day Sankalpa. I feel guilty about diluting the practice, so I plan to take a proper Sankalpa only after successfully completing the Yatha Shakti Sankalpa, since 144 days is a long commitment, and I worry I may falter closer to the finishing days. I felt sad when I broke my last 48-day Anushthana Sankalpa on the 31st day.
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, Yatha Shakti Sankalpa is a sacred and compassionate doorway for the sincere. Our Guru Bhairava honors effort with Bhava more than rigidity. Begin with full heart—complete what you can with shraddha, not guilt. Let this soften your path, and when your strength mature, the full Sankalpa will naturally arise from within. Every bead chanted in truth is accepted! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I just joined this group a few days ago. I’ve started doing Bhairava Japa without keeping track of Mandala counts or days. Every day, I simply chant it 108 times, as our Kuladaiva is Kala Bhairava. I also chant the Kalabhairava Ashtakam once a day. Is there anything specific you would advise me?
answer: Namaskaram, It’s truly Guru Bhairava’s grace that your Kula Daiva is Kala Bhairava—he himself calls his own. Yes, eventually in Bhairava sadhana, we go beyond counting. But that flowering doesn’t happen to everyone at once. That’s why we follow a sacred sequence—after 3 Mandalas (144 days) of Japa, the shift naturally begins: from Vaikhari (loud chanting) → Upamshu (whispered) → Manasika (mental) Japa and beyond towards Ajapa! In Bhairava’s anugraha, we advise all upasakas to begin with rhythm and feeling. Trust—he knows exactly where you are, and what is next! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Guruji, kindly guide me—how should I begin Bhairava sadhana?
answer: Namaskaram, With humble pranama, for those who are wondering how to begin or what steps to take in their journey, we lovingly invite you to explore the sadhana Paddhati section. There, you will find detailed guidance on how an individual can begin their journey—be it Maṇḍala sadhana or Aṣṭamī sadhana—with clarity and sraddhā (faith). Please also revisit the links shared in the group—they are pathways that offer deeper understanding and connection to the tattva (essence) of Guru Bhairava. Let your journey be one of antarmukhi yatra (inner turning), beginning with His anugraha (grace) In this sacred space, all sincere questions are welcome. Through the krupa (blessing) of Guru Bhairava, may we deepen our knowing of Him—step by step, moment by moment. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: A news article reports that local dogs in the Jammu region of J&K have become noticeably more alert amid the current situation. These dogs are assisting the army and, in some cases, detecting infiltration attempts even before the forces are aware of them. By barking at night, they alert troops, helping to prevent intrusions and enabling timely action to neutralize potential threats. Remarkably, these dogs are neither trained nor cared for by the army. Yet, according to the media clipping, even the latest sensors installed by the forces appear less effective than these vigilant animals. Kshstrepal has awakened!!
answer: Namaskaram, As we talk more about Shvana, from couple of post from most of you., let me share a step closure, to understand from Bhairava consciousness why they are more than just animals. In Bhairava Tantra, Bhairava is not just a deity — He is a living state of pure awareness, the stillness behind all action, the silence beneath all sound, the ever-watchful presence. Now think — who lives silently, watches deeply, hears more than humans, senses the unseen, and reacts before danger arrives? The dog — Bhairava’s companion. In scriptures like Rudrayamala Tantra, Bhairava Rahasya, and Tantra Sara, the Shvana - dog is seen not merely as an animal, but as a symbol of awakened awareness (Jagrata) and sharp consciousness (Tīksna Chaitanya). In higher Bhairava sadhana, the “inner dog” or Antar-Shvana reflects the upasaka’s own vigilant awareness, instinctive perception, and deep loyalty to the Self. We have now entered the Bhairava Yuga — a leap in time, where everything is shifting, and Bhairava is present all around us in many forms. This is the moment to awaken him within us, in this very life. Guru Bhairava knows exactly when and how everything is meant to unfold. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Today I am completing my very imperfect 48-day mandala, and I am unsure whether it can be counted. For the first 35 days, I completed 22 malas daily, but later had to reduce to 11 malas due to difficulty sitting cross-legged for extended periods (I am 53 years old). My total count stands at 98,604—just short of the 100,000 mark. Additionally, I switched from Bhairava Nama to Maha Kala Bhairavar Nama about three-quarters of the way through. This mandala has been a learning experience for me. I am hoping to reach 100,000 today by doing extra malas, especially since it is the full moon. Should I repeat the 48 days, opt for a shorter duration, reduce the number of malas, or try again in a different way? I humbly seek your guidance, Sir.
answer: Namaskaram, What a beautiful offering you’ve made, walking steadily with Bhairava through these 48 days. Even in what you call "imperfect," the sincerity, effort, and bhava (inner feeling) shine brightly. Bhairava does not count numbers—He feels the shraddha (devotion), the surrender, the honest struggle to stay with Him despite limitations. Changing the mantra midway and adjusting the count is part of your inner tuning. It seems this mandala became your teacher—and that is how Bhairava works, through experience, not rigidity. If your heart feels called to deepen, you can gently begin another cycle—maybe a 21-27-day sankalpa, with fewer malas, but greater stillness and ease. Let your body be your ally, not your barrier. On this full moon, as you complete the 1 lakh with devotion, offer it as a flower at the feet of Guru Bhairava. He knows. He watches. He accepts. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Could you kindly shed some light on mandala sadhana? As a grihastha, I find it difficult to perform the practice continuously. Is it necessary to complete all 48 days without interruption? If the sadhana is broken midway, should it be restarted from the beginning? Also, I humbly request you to share the Kshama Prarthana Mantra. I had taken sankalpa for only 11 days, as I couldn’t manage more than that. Should a sankalpa be taken before beginning a full mandala, or will it still be effective if we simply continue japa for 48 days without a formal sankalpa? Seeking your kind guidance.
answer:
Namaskaram, Thank you for your heartfelt message and sincere effort towards sadhana, even while walking the path of a grihastha.
Regarding your question on Mandala sadhana — yes, traditionally a Mandala is observed as 48 continuous days, as this duration allows deeper energetic alignment and inner transformation. However, in Bhairava consciousness, the bhava (inner feeling) and shraddha (devotion) hold even more power than rigid rule. If a break occurs unintentionally, you may restart the count, but do not carry guilt. Instead, offer a Kshama Prarthana (prayer for forgiveness) from your heart, and resume with deeper love. Here is a gentle Kshama Prarthana you may recite: II Manovakkayadosham me kshamasva Parameshvara Sarvam Bhairavarupena pujitam me shubhaya bhuyat II (“O Parama Deva, please forgive the errors of my mind, speech, and body. May all my acts offered unto You in the form of Guru Bhairava bring auspiciousness.”) As for Sankalpa, it brings clarity and intention, especially in Mandala sadhana. However, if one does the japa regularly with devotion and steadiness, even without a formal sankalpa, it still bears fruit — because Bhairava responds to sattvika bhāva, not just ritual precision. Your 11-day Sankalpa was not wasted — it was a seed. You can begin again with a renewed intention, even if for fewer days. The important part is to stay lovingly connected to the vibration of the mantra and the presence of Guru Bhairava. May He guide your steps with compassion and grace. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Are there any specific rules that must be followed during the 48-day mandala sadhana? Also, what practices should ideally be included as part of the mandala sadhana? Is it recommended to perform Panchopachara Pooja daily? Are there any particular Ashtakams, Kavachams, or Ashtottaras that should be recited? Additionally, how many malas should be completed each day during the mandala sadhana? Seeking your kind guidance.
answer: Namaskaram, Good one! Thank you for asking this—it's something many sadhakas also wonder about. During the 48-day Mandala sadhana, it's best to follow the same protocol outlined in the sadhana Paddhati—but with a gentle shift. At this stage, don't burden yourself with too many things. Instead, create space, stillness, and openness to feel his presence deeply. Start with love. Let each offering be a doorway. Yes, Panchopachara can be done daily, not as a rule, but as a heartfelt act. Ashtakams, kavachams, and ashtottaras are beautiful to include, but let them arise naturally from within, not as a checklist. The number of malas is not fixed—each of us walks a different rhythm with our istha,. Guru Bhairava. That said, 22 malas a day were mentioned in the Paddhati so that gradually, without pressure, you complete 1056 malas in 48 days. This becomes a blessed offering of 114,048 Names of Bhairava—aligning with the deeper intent of 3 Mandalas, preparing you for higher sankalpa and a sacred connection with Guru Bhairava. Above all, remember—do it with love. Speak to him. Invite him. Everything you do in this time is for him, with him, as him. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have a few queries regarding Maa Tara and Akshobya Bhairava. Whilst I deeply adore Kala Bhairava and consider him my eternal guru, I also feel drawn to the archetype and energy of Akshobya Bhairava, which resonates with my current work and future spiritual goals. Is it acceptable to regard both as gurus? In such a case, how should sadhana be approached—one at a time or both simultaneously? I’m also seeking a method that is sustainable alongside a 9-to-5 job. I’ve heard that Maa Tara worship can be intense or even dangerous, and that baali (offerings) are required to please her. I currently pray to the Neela Saraswati form but have not begun formal sadhana. Could you kindly share some insights on the concept of baali? Even if we approach an Ugra Deva with love, will baali eventually be expected—especially since Maa Tara’s Ashtottara mentions her fondness for baali? Regarding Akshobya Bhairava, I’ve noticed that his Tibetan form appears human, while in Hindu tantric depictions, he is shown as a serpent on Maa Tara’s head. When beginning his nama japa, which form is recommended for visualization and devotion? I humbly seek your guidance.
answer: Namaskaram, It’s truly beautiful to feel such deep devotion toward both Kala Bhairava and Akshobya Bhairava—both are expressions of the same undivided Guru Bhairava Tattva. Yes, it’s absolutely fine to revere both as Gurus, the inner consciousness recognizes no conflict when the devotion is sincere, respective any swaroopa. You may begin sadhana with one (It’s best to begin with just the Nama Japa. Kindly avoid adding any Beeja Mantras unless they’ve been received through proper Diksha from a Guru.), then gently include the other as your rhythm allows—especially with a daily office hour. As for Maa Tara, her true grace protects the sincere. Baali is a deeper Tantric concept—It’s not required for everyone, and such practices generally need proper guidance and a steady inner readiness to walk that path with care. Approaching her with pure love, surrender, and mantra is always the first step. Neela Saraswati is a beautiful path; no fear is needed when the heart is clean. For Akshobya Bhairava, you may begin with his peaceful Tibetan form if that resonates—Nama Japa aligns more with the energy than the outer image. He will reveal the rest. Always here to support your path. ~Jai Bhairava. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have a few doubts and humbly seek your guidance: After completing one mandala, can we begin the next one immediately, or is it advisable to take a few days' break? Instead of Naam Japa, can we recite Baba’s Sahasranamam? While chanting the Batuk Bhairava Sahasranamam, is it necessary to offer a Samarpan Mantra specifically for the Sahasranamam, or is the Samarpan Mantra only required for Naam Japa? Is it appropriate to perform mandala sadhana during Brahma Muhurta in the morning? After completing three mandalas, how should one proceed with the next phase of the practice?
answer: Namaskaram, Beautiful questions. Let’s walk with Bhairava's grace, step by step: 1) Yes, you may begin the next mandala immediately if the energy feels steady. Otherwise, take 2–3 days of silence and reflection before starting again. 2) Sahasranama of Baba can be done, but in Mandala sadhana, Naam Japa is the core foundation. It builds the subtle connection layer by layer. 3) Samarpan Mantra is ideally for Naam Japa. If you're chanting Batuk Bhairava Sahasranamam with devotion, simply begin and end with prayerful surrender—no rigidity. 4) Yes, Brahma Muhurta is most sacred. Bhairava walks closest in that silence. 5) After 3 mandalas (144 Days), if you've been sincerely following the steps for more than a couple of months, let the next layer open naturally. If not, gently return to the sadhana Padathi—it is designed to align you deeply. No checklist is needed—only connection. Just ask: “What brings me nearer to my Guru?” May Guru Bhairava bless you, guide you, and walk with you always. Trust the path, it is alive within you. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I think about Baba all the time—both directly and indirectly—but being human, I make mistakes and struggle with certain habits. For instance, when I’m drunk, I consciously avoid thinking about Baba, and afterward, I feel too guilty to meet His gaze. I understand that a guilty mind creates more inner conflict and becomes an obstacle on the spiritual path. That’s why I’m seeking your guidance on how to overcome this. I’ve already recognized the issue and am sincerely trying to work through it.
answer: Namaskaram, Thank you for your honest sharing. Remember, every soul carries the three gunas—sattva, rajas, and tamas—and none of them can be completely separated from us as long as we live. The key is not to fight or reject them but to find balance with the grace of Guru and Baba’s compassionate presence. Feeling guilt shows your awakened awareness, which is already a step forward. Instead of resisting those moments, invite Baba’s energy softly, allowing that connection to gently transform your habits... He will come! Bhairava’s fierce compassion guides us to rise beyond our limitations with patience and love. Keep walking steadily, knowing every effort made in awareness brings you closer to light. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I recently watched a podcast where the guest, when asked about baali (sacrificial offerings), said that some deities require it, and if one has issues with animal sacrifice, they should choose another deity. This statement caused some alarm for me. Technically speaking, even for Goddess Durga, baali is traditionally offered. In the midst of so many differing views on social media, I feel the need to embrace the stillness of Akshobya Bhairava. For me, it's important to remember that love holds a higher place in devotion than animal sacrifice. That’s my personal view, though I understand that some traditional or technical tantrics may see it differently.
answer: Namaskaram, Thank you for sharing your thoughts. In Tantra, Bali holds a deeper and subtle significance—beyond just the external form. As sadhakas, when we connect sincerely with our Ishta Devata, the true meaning and method of Bali reveal themselves. Each Devata, including Devi Swaroopa embodies the 03 Gunas (Sattva, Rajas & Tamas) and their worship can vary accordingly. In certain sadhana paddhatis, Bali is an essential part (Tamas), but it’s not limited to any sacrifice alone—there are many other symbolic and internal ways to offer Bali in Tantra. What matters most is the deep connection and activation of the Devata’s presence within us. When this happens, the divine dissolves us into that form, transcending external rituals. So, while opinions differ, love and devotion remain the highest paths. Let’s keep embracing the path that resonates with our heart and sadhana. Guru Bhairava reveals everything according to his own will and divine timing. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: If I chant Om Bhairavaya Namah after taking sankalpa, in the viniyoga shloka, should I refer to the deity as Sri Bhairavaya Devata, or is Sri Kaalabhairavaya Devata the correct form? At present, I do not visualize a specific form of Bhairava Baba during my practice.
answer: Namaskaram, Our Bhairava is beyond any one form—He is the very consciousness of awareness itself. So, you need not limit yourself to a single swaroopa. Whether you chant Sri Bhairavaya or Sri Kaalabhairavaya, both are correct, as He is present in all forms simultaneously. Stay open in your heart and feel His presence beyond form. Chant: Om Bhairavaya Namah... Just chant with devotion—He will reveal Himself. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Could you please explain how one might connect with Bhagawan Akshobhya Bhairava during the early stages of practice? Is it appropriate to add His name japa after completing our daily sadhana?
answer:
Namaskaram, Your gratitude flows like a gentle offering at Baba's feet. To connect with Bhagawan Akshobhya Bhairava in the early stages, simply call His name with feeling — even a whisper from the heart reaches Him. Yes, after your daily sadhana, you may softly add His name japa — “Om Akshobhyaya Bhairavaya Namah” — allowing it to ripple in silence within. Completing the 144-day (03) mandala is indeed the sacred foundation—a stable ground where the deeper mysteries naturally unfold, in perfect timing and grace. May Baba’s calm strength guide and support your every step.
~ Jai Bhairava. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I chant Shri Batuka Bhairava Brahma Kavacham daily before beginning my Naam Mantra chanting. My question is: Is it necessary to perform Samarpan after reciting the Shri Batuka Bhairava Brahma Kavacham, or is Samarpan required only after Naama Japa?
answer: Namaskaram, So beautiful to hear of your daily offering through Shri Batuka Bhairava Brahma Kavacham and naama japa. In the path of Bhairava consciousness, there's no checklist—only loving presence. Sadhana is not about ticking what comes before or after… it's about surrendering your time, breath, and energy at the feet of your Ishta. Let every chant, every silence, and every pause be your Samarpan. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Every day, you share a new method from the Vigyan Bhairava in great detail. Should we practice a new technique each day, or is it better to choose one that suits us best and stay with it? Also, I have one more doubt. As you mentioned, we should observe the pause between two thoughts. So, should we simply remain present—watching thoughts as they arise, pass, and then the next one comes? It feels endless at times. How will I know if this process is working for me, and what is its ultimate goal? When I observed the pause between breaths, it felt a bit easier. But observing thoughts seems more subtle and challenging.
answer: Namaskaram, Beautiful reflections. In Bhairava’s wisdom (Vijnana Bhairava Tantra), there are 112 ways — each a doorway (Portal) to the same Presence. Right now, we are walking through the learning curve, gently understanding each method like petals unfolding. No rush to do all — just allow yourself to know them, and baby step to experience. Once the knowing becomes natural, you will sense which portal truly opens your connection to Bhairava within. The stillness between thoughts is subtler than breath — like the space between heartbeats — but with patience, it reveals its own light. For now, just stay with openness and trust. Our Guru Bhairava is already guiding from within. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Is Raag Bhairavi related to Bhairava Baba? If so, what is its significance? Can we play it in the background during chanting? Also, I personally feel that Raag Yaman has a very cooling effect on me (not in a literal physical sense). To my surprise, when I researched more about it, I discovered that it is associated with the Moon. It is said to create a yantra of the Moon in our mental space.
answer: Namaskaram, A beautiful observation indeed. While Raag Bhairavi is not directly linked to Bhairava Baba in name alone, its depth, emotion, and pathos resonate with the Bhava of surrender — which aligns deeply with Bhairava Consciousness. You may gently play it in the background during chanting if it helps you stay inward and open. Bhairavi Raga, it invites stillness and inner reflection, making it ideal for ending musical journeys or entering meditative states. Let us gently remember — for anyone on the path of sadhana, a connection to classical music and its subtle notes often arises naturally. It doesn’t need to be forced; it is a sacred and quiet awakening from within... It’s a process! And yes, Raga Yaman carries the cooling lunar grace — like the calm gaze of Bhairava through the Moon. If it centers your being, it’s a sign your inner sound is aligning with your own Soma tattva. Trust that feeling. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I’m planning to take a 40-day sankalpa to chant 27 malas of the Batuk Bhairava Mool Mantra daily. However, I’m not familiar with how to perform homa, tarpana, and marjana. What should I do in this case?
answer: Namaskaram, It’s wonderful to hear about your devotion to Batuk Bhairava. Before starting with specific counts like 27 malas or advanced steps like homa, tarpan, or marjan, we humbly suggest going through the guidance shared in the sadhana tab. We’ve outlined a structured path there—starting without fixed numbers—because the first 144 days (3 mandalas) of sadhana are the foundation. This helps you connect deeply with your Ishta and awaken inner bhava (devotion), where clarity naturally arises. If you have received diksha specifically in Batuk Bhairava sadhana, you may follow the instructed path. If not, please begin with the initial steps. Nama Japa itself is a Guru’s grace—it's not about which mantra has more power, but about the purpose and feeling with which we invoke Guru Bhairava. Follow each step with quiet faith... Bhairava knows the right time for every grace. Sadhana is not a race to know—it is a gentle unfolding, a deep inner evolution. Let go of the need to grasp everything at once... Just dissolve in His love and allow Him to carry you beyond. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have been doing Bhairava Naam Japa for the past 7–8 months. Recently, I brought a Trishul for Bhairava Baba. Is there any specific puja or mantra associated with the Trishul?
answer: Namaskaram, Beautiful offering. Trishul is Baba’s symbol of divine protection—just placing it with devotion invokes his presence of the space. But after completing 3 mandala (144 Days) sadhanas, the connection deepens immensely. Rituals help when done in the right spirit, but as sadhakas, our true path is to hold Bhairava not just in glimpses, but as a living presence within. When that happens, his protection is always ON—seen or unseen, he takes care of all. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have been doing Batuk Nath Naam Japa for over a year now, and the Mool Mantra Japa for more than six months. I’ve also completed multiple anusthans. Each time I did an anusthan, I followed the prescribed mala count and offered food to people as part of the practice. Recently, I learned that after completing an anusthan, one is supposed to perform homa, tarpana, and marjana. I was planning to begin another anusthan, but since I don’t know how to perform these rituals, I wanted to ask if there’s an alternative. I’ve heard that if one is unable to perform homa, tarpana, and marjana, they can compensate by doubling the number of malas. However, I’m a bit confused—does doubling mean 27 malas per day × 2, or does it mean doubling the total count over 40 days (I. E., 27 × 40 = 1080, then 1080 × 2 = 2160)? That seems quite a lot and may take me a long time to complete. I humbly seek your guidance on what I can do in this situation.
answer: Namaskaram, Thank you for sharing, your inner though. Your one year of Naam Japa and sincere effort is never forgotten by Him — Bhairava sees every heartbeat of devotion. Homa, Tarpana, and Marjan are indeed part of the next stage in the sadhana Paddhati, not just as rituals, but as ways to ground the energy you’ve invoked. It’s not about doing more but about aligning deeper. We’ve detailed a clear sadhana paddhati tab with Viniyoga, Sankalpa, Astami Sadahan and more into the inner connect of each step, take some quiet time to read through that, especially the Q&A tab — it’s a small treasure of insights. Rather than doubling the japa mala mechanically, it’s more fruitful to walk with awareness, step by step. Bhairava doesn't count — He connects. May He guide you always, from within and beyond. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I completed a 48-day mandala with 100,000 chants, although for the last 13 days I switched the mantra from Om Bhairavaya Namaha to Om Kalabhairavaya Namaha. I finished this on 11 May. On 12 May, I began an 8-day sankalpa of chanting the Kala Bhairava Ashtakam 21 times daily, which I will complete tomorrow. On Ashtami, I’m keen to begin a 48-day nama japa of Akshobya Bhairava. Can these three mandalas be dedicated to different archetypes of Bhairava? Also, I feel that doing 11 malas per day is less mechanical and more mindful for me. Would you suggest any other formats or approaches?
answer: Namaskaram, Beautiful effort and deep dedication – Bhairava surely witness the sincerity of your journey. Yes, the mandala counts, as long as the bhāva (inner feeling) remained steady through the name change. Still, it's best to stay with one form deeply at a time. Bhairava responds not to how much we do, but how connected we are while doing it. Rather than three parallel mandalas, consider completing one archetype fully before beginning the next. This allows the Bhairava-tattva to truly awaken within. Even 11 malas can become mechanical if the heart isn’t immersed. Fewer japas with full presence are more powerful than many done in haste. Let each step be a conscious offering. He walks with you. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Could you please elaborate on how one should properly perform Nyasa? Should both hands be used together, or is it right hand first followed by the left? A video demonstrating this particular Nyasa would be greatly appreciated.
answer: Namaskaram, Good one, let me share something connected, In Bhairava sadhana, there’s no strict “right or wrong” way—what truly matters is how deeply you connect. Viniyoga sets the sankalpa, like gently knocking on Baba’s door with devotion. Nyasa isn’t just a step—it’s how we awaken Bhairava within, making our body his garbhagriha or temple. Whether with both hands or one at a time, do it with full awareness. That’s where the magic lies. Video tutorials often explain things academically, but sadhana is different—it’s not just about learning, it’s about feeling, presence, and inner connection. True sadhana begins when grace flows. So don’t focus too much on “how” or “what.” Just do it with surrender and let Bhairava guide you from within. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: What is the best time to perform Bhairava Japa?
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, His Naam can be taken at any time—He is beyond time. But in the early stages, it’s best to avoid midnight hours (especially 11 PM to 4 AM), as the Tamasika tattva is more active then. It’s like offering sattvic devotion in a space still veiled in tamas—requiring deeper grounding and guidance. Brahma Muhurta or daytime is most ideal for japa. As your connection deepens, the inner guidance will grow stronger. Just trust His grace—Bhairava will show the way when your 'self' is ready. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: While checking the panchang for my area, I noticed that there are auspicious and inauspicious times throughout the day. How does this influence one’s sadhana? Also, I observed that Ashtami falls during Panchaka. Does this have any impact on beginning a mandala practice?
answer: Namaskaram, Good one, let me share something on panchanga! Panch- anga indeed holds five - limbs — Tithi (lunar day), Vara (weekday), Nakshatra (star), Yoga, and Karana — and is deeply rooted in Vedic timing. But in the path of sadhana, especially under the loving gaze of Guru Bhairava, we transcend outer timings. When He becomes the center, every moment turns auspicious. Bhairava is "Kaalanataka", the Master of Time — and in His presence, there is no good or bad moment, only the right one. So, if your heart is ready and surrendered, that itself becomes the sign. Begin the Mandala. Baba walks with us. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Can Chameli (jasmine) flower oil be used to light a lamp for Bhairava Baba? For my daily nitya upasana and japa, I usually light a sesame oil lamp in the evening after sunset. My family lights a ghee lamp in the morning, and I also use ghee when sesame oil is unavailable. I light a mustard oil lamp whenever I use a chaumukhi diya, specifically during visits to the nearby Bhairava temple on the occasion of Masik Kalashtami. Also, which ittar does Bhairava Baba prefer? I currently apply sandalwood ittar to Baba’s photo in my home temple.
answer: Namaskaram, In the path of Bhairava, the essence is always bhava — the feeling behind the offering. As per Shastra and Tantra Marga, sesame oil is most traditionally used for lighting the deepam, especially during Nitya Upasana and Ashtami sadhana. Yet, Bhairava is not bound by form — when we offer with love, surrender, and gratitude, even a drop of jasmine oil becomes sacred. He accepts it all, and in time, begins to reflect through you. As for ittar, sandalwood is beautiful — serene and grounding. Bhairava also resonates deeply with earthy, raw fragrances like kasturi (musk), khus (vetiver), and sometimes camphor blends. But truly, what carries the scent to him is your devotion. May your path be ever lit by the grace of Baba. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Is it acceptable if a kavacham contains embedded beeja mantras?
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, absolutely. When a Kavacham carries embedded Beeja Mantras, it becomes a living field of protection and awakening. Kavacha Rahasya lies in its sacred alignment — the way each Beeja vibrates with intention and modulation. It is complete in its own way. Yet, its deepest resonance unfolds when received through Guru Diksha — not by seeking, but when He wills it, in His time, with His grace. All is Bhairava’s doing. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: At 53 years of age, living outside India, receiving diksha from a human guru seems unlikely in this lifetime’s design. I remain deeply grateful for all that already is.
answer: Namaskaram, As this surfaced naturally, here's something meaningful to add! Age and geography hold no boundary in the eyes of Guru Bhairava. His ways are timeless, His grace — beyond form. Just as Ratnakar transformed through the subtle spark of Narada - Maha Muni, and Swami Tapovan Maharaj began his radiant path at 55, so too, each soul awakens in its perfect moment. In Bhairava Consciousness, even "Bhaya - Fear" becomes "Bhairava." What seems distant becomes near. What seems delayed arrives precisely on time. When the disciple is ready, the Guru indeed appears — within or without. Trust His rhythm. He knows. He guides. He is already walking with us now! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have been doing daily japa of 5 malas of Om Bhairavaya Namaha since 20th April 2025, considering Bhairava as my Guru. Yesterday, on Kala Ashtami, I completed 10 malas of japa. I am currently using a spatika (crystal) mala purchased from Isha Foundation. Is it appropriate to use a spatika mala for this japa, or should I switch to a rudraksha mala? I humbly seek your guidance. If a rudraksha mala is recommended, could you please advise me on where to find a genuine one?
answer: Namaskaram, Guru Bhairava, Grace to do even a single Mala. Your daily Japa are truly seen by Him — He walks with every sincere breath of devotion if been done with love and surrender. Spatika mala is pure and calming, and it's absolutely fine to continue your Japa with it. If Bhairava guides, you inwardly toward Rudraksha you may honor that call later. Both are sacred when the heart is steady. For deeper guidance, please do explore the sadhana Padathi tab — it carries the living map of Bhairava sadhana. Also, the Q&A tab is a treasure of insights; spending time there will open many subtle doorways for you. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: In mandala sadhana, if I begin my practice during Brahma Muhurta and it extends significantly past sunrise—say, by about an hour—will it still be considered valid for the day I started it, assuming the day is counted from sunrise to sunrise?
answer: Namaskaram, Yes, absolutely. In Bhairava consciousness, what matters is your bhava and discipline. If you begin during Brahma Muhurta with full intent, it is valid for that day—even if it flows beyond sunrise. The Divine counts presence, not the clock. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I’m keen to understand energy leaks in the context of sadhana, especially while functioning in society where we must focus on external matters. In this process, what kind of external content tends to cause a leak in sadhana progress? Is it content with emotional attachments? What about learning material, especially since so much of it is online these days? How can we prevent such leaks?
answer: Namaskaram, A very important and timely question — often missed by most sadhakas. Many feel that once daily rituals are done, sadhana is complete. But true sadhana continues throughout the day — especially in the world, not away from it. "Energy leaks" happen at the level of our awareness. Even what we hold throughout the day — whether it's a mobile, tablet, or laptop — affects us. Be conscious of the context you engage with and how it connects to your inner state. Connection to Daiva Tattva should not be limited to just the time of sadhana — it must flow through your day. In truth, it's the other way around: when your awareness remains steady, everything becomes part of the sadhana. This is exactly where Vijnana Bhairava Tantra becomes our guide — it teaches us awareness in action. Bhairava consciousness is not about escaping the world but staying inwardly connected while functioning in it. Here are two simple steps to protect your energy in today’s fast-paced world: Be mindful of what you consume — especially through your phone. Content with strong emotional charge or mindless repetition drains subtle energy. Choose what uplifts or deepens awareness. Pause and return to centre often — even for 10 seconds. Throughout the day, gently witness your breath or sound around you. This reconnects you to Bhairava within. Let this be a reminder: each day, each moment is a doorway to Bhairava Tattva. Follow the ongoing 17-day series — it’s not just content, but a living vibration. Use it when you're truly ready to go deeper. Tantra is not theory — it's prakriya (living practice) through self-effort. Stay connected, stay inward., Bhairava is not hidden, he is awareness! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: The use of red Rudraksha for japa is specified in the sadhana guidelines. Kindly confirm the appropriate type of Rudraksha to be used, such as the 2 Mukhi.
answer: Namaskaram, Rudrakshas come in many variants, with each Mukhi representing a unique energy and significance. The choice of Rudraksha isn’t just about the number of Mukhis—it also involves color and usage context. While lighter and naturally brown Rudrakshas are commonly used for Japa and spiritual practices, black Rudrakshas are traditionally reserved for specific sadhana Pradhutis and should be approached with care. For daily practices like Japa, 5 Mukhi and 6 Mukhi Rudrakshas are particularly beneficial and widely accepted. In my personal experience, whenever we set out to choose a Rudraksha, it’s never really us making the choice—the energy of the Rudraksha chooses us. It’s a subtle connection, something beyond logic, where the right bead naturally aligns with our energy, drawing us toward it without force. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: You mentioned that black Rudraksha should be approached with care. I was gifted a black Rudraksha mala some time ago and have been using it for my Bhairava japa for almost a year now. Could you please explain what precautions I should take when using it? I also have a light brown Rudraksha mala that I reserve for chanting ‘Om Namah Shivaya.’ Should I consider using that one instead?”
answer: Namaskaram, In the space of Bhairava Consciousness, even your question is a form of sacred seeking. Yes, black Rudraksha carries the subtle vibration of Shudra varna as per ancient Rudraksha Shastra. This doesn’t make it negative—but it aligns with tamasic energies—those of grounding, protection, and fierce devotion and used on differnt sadhana stages. When used with awareness and devotion to Guru Bhairava, who embraces all (Sristi) —this mala can become a powerful tool. Yet, care is needed because it can intensify emotions and karmas if not anchored in right sadhana. If your Bhairava japa feels deep, still, and fearless, you may continue. But if you ever feel scattered, heavy, or restless—pause. The light brown Rudraksha—closer to Rajo–Sattva, often resonates with Shiva guna - tattva—ideal for any nama Japa. You may shift to it for Bhairava japa too, if you seek balance, lightness, or clarity. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskaram, could you please tell me about Rishi Kashyap? I’ve heard that if one does not know their Kul Rishi, they may consider Rishi Kashyap as their Kul Rishi. Is that true? Kindly explain.
answer: Namaskaram, Such a beautiful question, let me share what flows through me on this. Yes, what you're referring to is true in many traditions. If one does not know their Kul Rishi, many are guided to connect with Rishi Kashyapa, one of the ancient Sapta Rishis, as a universal lineage guide. But more importantly — it’s not just about a name, it’s about the Gothra — the spiritual and energetic DNA of our being. Gothra shows the lineage of seers through which one’s soul and karmic memory travels. It tells us not only where we come from, but who continues to hold us. Rishi Kashyapa is a profound Rishi, mentioned across Vedas and Puranas. He is the father of Devas, Asuras, Nagas, and all living beings — the root seer of vast creation. That is why many align to him when the Kul Rishi is unknown. And yes, when there is uncertainty, Narayana Gothra is often suggested — as Narayana (the eternal Vishnu principle) is the savior, and in Vedas it is said: “Where the gothra is unknown, let the remembrance of Narayana be established" Ultimately, the Kula Guru or Rishi is not just about bloodline, but soulline — and through devotion, inquiry, and grace, the right connection will awaken within us. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Since childhood, I’ve felt a deep connection with Maa Kali and Bhairava Baba. However, due to family stereotypes and restrictions, I was mostly kept away from their worship. As a child, I only practiced Durga Upasana. Everything changed when a Narmadeshwar Lingam entered our home—though we don’t have it now; we currently worship a Sphatik Lingam engraved with the Sri Yantra. The Lingam was brought in despite resistance from some family members, as certain elders believed a Shiva Lingam shouldn’t be kept at home. But I felt strongly about it and remained adamant. Once Baba entered our home, things began to shift positively—especially financially. It felt like life became a cakewalk. Since then, I’ve been naturally drawn to this path. Despite making some mistakes, I began to intuitively understand the basics of worship—like fasting and a bit of astrology—even though I’ve never formally studied them. But during my third year of college, everything suddenly stopped. All that I knew seemed to vanish. My Upasana and meditation went off track and declined sharply. Since then, it’s been an on-and-off journey. But in the last few months, I’ve become disciplined and consistent. I now do my Nitya Japa daily, regardless of how I feel. Sometimes it’s blissful, sometimes just mechanical repetition—but I stay with it. I wanted to ask: Could my birth tithi (Jyeshtha Krishna Ashtami) have any connection to my inclination and attraction towards Bhairava Baba and Maa Kali? Is it normal to experience this kind of spontaneous spiritual attraction and an on-off journey? Am I straying from the path by overthinking these things? Is this ego in some form, or is my mind playing tricks to make me feel special in some way?
answer: Namaskaram, Your questions arise from deep sincerity — a sign of Bhairava’s subtle pull on your soul. Jyeshtha Krishna Ashtami carries the fierce grace of Maa Kali and the transformative fire of Guru Bhairava. Your attraction is not accidental, it is remembrance — smarana — from lifetimes past. This path is not linear. The on-off rhythm is natural during initial stages of sadhana. Let me remind, Our Guru Bhairava walks with those who fall and rise, again and again. Overthinking is the mind’s way of trying to control what the soul already knows. Release it. Ego questions itself in disguise, but true longing dissolves ego. Let Bhairava guide without resistance. You are not straying — you are being refined. With Bhairava’s grace, everything finds its rightful place. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: In the sadhana Paddhati guidelines, I came across the term “Mandala,” which referred to a period longer than 41 days. I understand that “Anushthana” typically lasts exactly 41 days. Is a Mandala literally just one round of practice? Are we expected to follow Anushthana rules during a Mandala—such as eating less, avoiding intimacy, and maintaining other disciplines? Also, do the lengths of Mandalas vary depending on the deity?
answer: Namaskaram, In Bhairava sadhana, Mandala refers to 48 days — this aligns with the body's subtle energy cycle completing one full loop. Unlike strict Anushthana, Mandala is more about consistency and deepening connection. External disciplines like food or intimacy are secondary — what truly matters is your inner dissolution, your surrender, and how completely you offer yourself while sitting before Him. Let love and presence guide you. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: What are some creative and intense ways to place myself in a critical situation—just to test whether my sadhana is bearing the fruit of His grace? Something akin to how the protagonist stands in front of a train in the movie HanuMan.
answer: Namaskaram, Such a heartfelt and raw question… In Bhairava consciousness, testing doesn’t need danger — it needs deep presence. The real test is, can you remain still in chaos, aware in darkness, fearless in the unknown? Sometimes, the fiercest path is simply to sit utterly still in front of him and being conscious in your own mind for 4 hr stretch! Still if you need something, then do tapasya... If you truly wish to challenge and refine yourself, then begin a 48-day Mandala sadhana — skip 1 meals (lunch or dinner), offer pure love, and dedicate at least 8 hours a day to your practice, only in sadhana. Do this with a sankalpa of 8 lakh japas. That is the true fire — and Bhairava will surely reveal. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Usually, I complete my Mandala sadhana before midnight, but on one occasion, my pooja extended beyond 12—around 12:15 AM. Also, a few days ago, I had a dream in which I was unable to complete my Mandala sadhana. Today, while using my japa mala, a knot formed, and I had to untie it before resuming my japa. Will any of these incidents affect the sanctity or continuity of my Mandala sadhana?
answer:
Namaskaram, Thank you for sharing with such sincerity. In the path of Bhairava, intention and bhava hold greater power than the clock. If one night your sadhana crossed into the next day, or a knot came in the mala — take it as Bhairava’s way of deepening your awareness, not as a mistake. Dreams and symbols arise not to scare us, but to guide us gently. Stay steady in devotion, with love and trust. Our Guru Bhairava sees the fire in heart, not the time on the clock. Continue… he is with you.
~Jai Bhairava. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I read in Shivabalayogi’s biography (he is considered an avatar of Shiva and spent most of 12 years—from age 12 to 24—in Samadhi as a Sannyasin) that Tapas, in the context of Dhyana, begins when one surpasses six hours of unbroken, continuous Dhyana or Samadhi. Is there a deeper significance behind that specific duration, or was it simply observed by him through experience? Since he hadn’t read any scriptures until after his 12-year Tapas, all his knowledge came from within and through direct experience—so he wasn’t referencing the Shastras in this matter. I’ve noticed that something shifts when one crosses that threshold—even if not yet in Samadhi. It feels like a consuming fire, as though it’s burning away Samskaras and Vasanas. Does this process relate to Tapas? Is this Shakti the same as Iccha Shakti, or are they distinct? And does this ‘fire’ have any connection to Kundalini Shakti?
answer: Namaskaram, Such a beautiful reflection, and deeply felt… yes, what you’ve touched is the very Agni of inner transformation — that sacred fire beyond technique, where grace begins to move. Indeed, in many yogic traditions and certain tantra sadhana, crossing the threshold of 6+ hours in unbroken Dhyana is need, and also is seen not as a rule, but as a lived gateway — where mind begins to dissolve, and Tapas awakens on its own. It's not about the number, but the inner burn — the fire you described, that begins to consume Samskaras and Vasanas, is very real. This fire is intimately connected to Iccha Shakti — the will to dissolve into the Divine — and it often merges with Kundalini Shakti, rising not just from the base, but from the very core of longing. We are currently walking through the sacred revelations of Vijnana Bhairava Tantra, exploring 112 ways to enter the heart of Bhairava's grace — and what you are touching is a direct echo of those doorways. Let’s keep walking gently. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Today I woke up, drank some water, and turned on mobile data—which I usually don’t do first thing in the morning. I saw a YouTube notification for the Daiva Anughra podcast and started watching it. I got completely absorbed in it, and then suddenly realized I hadn’t even washed my face. So my question is: was it disrespectful toward the knowledge? My intention was sincere, but my body wasn’t clean. I know this question might sound superstitious, and I feel that’s one reason many people are pushed away from religion—they’re told too many rules without explanation. For example, the other day, a priest at a nearby temple told me that we should remain barefoot within the temple boundary and, while leaving, we shouldn’t turn our back to the deity. When I politely asked why, he simply said, “Paap lagta hai” (meaning “It’s a sin”). So everything just boils down to ‘paap lagta hai,’ but no one explains the reason. Are we simply expected to follow without understanding?
answer: Namaskaram, Your question reflects genuine sincerity, and that itself is the answer—Shuddhi was already present as your intention was pure. Bhairava sees not just outer actions but the bhava behind them. There are two aspects of shuddhi—bahya (external) and antara (internal). Rituals help purify energy (Needed), but in Bhairava sadhana done on its bhava and surrender that open the door to daiva anugraha. That you were pulled to the teaching before even cleansing the body shows your inner fire and readiness. Bhairava doesn’t judge the rawness—He embraces it. That is why in this path, even the inauspicious becomes auspicious. Many sadhaka's and Mahaguru parampara echoed this truth: Ramana Maharshi showed us that the true temple is the still Self within. Beda Kannappa offered water from his mouth and raw meat — yet won the heart of Shiva. Sri Ramakrishna reminded us: God sees the heart, not the outer form. They all revealed one truth — Bhava is everything. In Bhairava path, it's not just a connection — it's an offering of the self, raw, real, and surrendered. So, never feel guilt — rather, stay aware. Let your inner fire of devotion be the offering. Bhairava is closer to the heart that beats in truth, not the feet that follow rituals without knowing why. Always remember: In Bhairava, the raw is sacred. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have two questions related to Bhairava sadhana: I have a Sphatik Lingam at home, along with a small black marble Nandi Maharaj and one Trishul. Should I get a separate Trishul specifically for Bhairava Baba’s abhishekam, or is it acceptable to use the one I already have? Alternatively, can I perform abhishekam on the Lingam itself, mentally invoking Bhairava Baba within it? During meditation, is it appropriate to chant Bhairava Baba’s name with the breath—for example, “Bhai” on inhalation and “Rav” on exhalation—or mentally repeat “Bhairava” during both inhalation and exhalation? Is it also acceptable to use the “Ham (हं) – Sa (स)” method (Ham on inhale, Sa on exhale), which I learned through YSS and also read about in Principles of Tantra by Sir Arthur Avalon? I still practice this after my daily japa. Is that method suitable within Bhairava sadhana?
answer: Namaskaram, I bow to the awakened flame of Bhairava within you. The Spathik Lingam you have, with Nandi and Trishul, is already a complete yantra of devotion. Bhairava is not separate from Shiva — in temples across Tamil Nadu and other sacred kshetras, Bhairava is worshipped in Linga Swaroopa. The same trishul can be used; the bhava (inner feeling) and shraddha (faith) invoke His presence. You may continue abhishekam to the Lingam, mentally offering it to Bhairava — He is the essence of Parama Shiva. The breath chanting you shared — whether "Bhai-Rava" or "Hamsa" — both are valid. What matters is ekagrate (one-pointedness). Bhairava Tattva is beyond method, yet fully present in any method done with awareness. You may continue as guided inwardly — He walks with us. Keep your path simple, sacred, and steady. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: With Krishna as my Kuldevta, I once held cleanliness as a sacred priority. But since beginning Bhairava sadhana, that meticulousness seems to have softened.
answer: Namaskaram, Excellent Question, let me share in a different view: Cleanliness born of ego dissolves in Bhairava. What was outer perfection becomes inner nakedness. Even our Istha is Digambara — He strips away what no longer serves, revealing only what is essential for this life's evolution and connection. Bhairava doesn’t make us impure, He shows us what lies beyond purity. He is Suddhatattvasvarupaya (One who is above then all Tattva). Let his grace shower on all of us! ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskaram, Sri Gurubhyo Namaha. Thank you for the amazing and informative podcast. I’m truly grateful for the guidance you’re-offering on the path toward light. I wanted to seek your advice: can the shared Paddhati be started now, or should it be taken up only after completing three Mandalas? I’m currently close to completing my first Mandala.
answer: Namaskaram, Start the shared padhathi now with full devotion and sincerity. Bhairava’s grace flows when the heart is ready, not bound by mandalas’ completion. Continue your mandala with focus and let the new practice strengthen your path. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: If Nitya gets interrupted for a few days, do we need to reset our count? How many malas of japa should be completed in Nitya before we begin the Mandala Sankalpa? If Nitya practice is inconsistent, can we still include the japa from previous counts, or do we need to reset everything each time we miss a few days?
answer: Namaskaram, Our Guru Bhairava is the embodiment of Dharma and Karuna. If your Nitya paused, ask yourself with honesty—have you been true in your Dharana, Nithya and Japa? If yes, continue... If not, seek Kshama and begin again with fresh Sankalpa. Our Ishta doesn’t hold a checklist—He watches your commitment, Surrender not your count. Sadhana is not about perfection but about presence. When done with Shraddha and Bhava, even missed steps become part of the path. Let surrender guide you more than structure. With Guru Bhairava Kripa, all restarts become progress. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Whatever you say resonates deeply with my experience. Sadhana becomes more intense each time I restart, but it often gets interrupted—whether by karma or other forces in life around me. I’ve taken sankalpas for various anushtanas with great vigor, but they always seem to get broken. I used to think it was because my Nitya japa hadn’t reached one lakh, and that was stopping the anushtanas. But even Nitya itself gets interrupted due to karma. I’ve tried the method of taking His name during daily chores, because He’s always present in my mind. Yet simply sitting down with Him doesn’t seem to happen. I pray that He graces me with supportive circumstances around me—so I can effortlessly sit with Him, without disturbances or breaks in my Nitya japa. Thank you, Guruji. Your channel truly helps me regain confidence, stay positive, and turn inward—asking myself what it all really means.
answer: Namaskaram, Your honesty is your strength. Bhairava knows every rise and fall, every return and retreat. He is not outside this struggle—He is the one witnessing it through you... He is with you! Sankalpa may waver, but His grace never does. Take refuge in His name, even in motion, in silence, in fatigue. Call Him—Lokatrayavyapine —He who pervades the three worlds, in your mind, your karma, and your longing. You’re not alone. The force that keeps you turning inward is His call. Just ask. He will arrange the support, for only He can hold what we cannot. Let Him sit through you. Let Him chant through you. Everything else will follow. I’m only a instrument. With His Bhiksha, I serve. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Usually, it’s said that one should increase sadhana during times of difficulty. But to me, that feels somewhat superficial—perhaps because I’m still in the early stages of my practice. How can we increase sadhana when problems arise, especially when the natural tendency of the mind is to focus time and energy on resolving the issue rather than surrendering? That’s when sadhana tends to take a back seat, and daily life circumstances seem to take precedence. Sadhana is said to accelerate the unfolding of karmas. So ideally, how does increasing it help a sadhaka? Wouldn’t it make more sense to reduce sadhana during such times to slow down the karmic flow? I find it difficult to understand these nuances at this nascent stage of my journey.
answer: Namaskaram, Your question reflects a deep and sincere inquiry — one that arises in many hearts on this path. Sadhana is not just for peaceful times — it is the shelter in storms. When life becomes turbulent, it’s not about increasing or decreasing sadhana mechanically, but about shifting its quality — from effort to surrender, from doing to being with Him. Think of daily sadhana as filling a sacred vessel — during calm days, we prepare... During difficult days, we draw from it. Even a few moments of remembrance, done with true love and raw honesty, invoke His presence. Our Guru Bhairava doesn’t withdraw — He simply stands behind the veil.. A shield, waiting for our inner gaze to turn. Let your tears and confusion also become offerings. Don’t strive to perfect the practice — just stay with Him, even broken. That is true sadhana. – ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Sri Gurubhyo Namaha, Kindly could you clarify the steps—am I following the correct order as mentioned in the podcast? Nyasam, Pooja, Kavacham, Mukhya Dhyanam, Satvika Dhyanam, Verses 1 to 14 of the Stotram (10 times), Verses 1 to 39 (11th time), Nama Japam (108 times)
answer: Namaskaram, It’s perfect! But I've a piece of advice: Your intent is pure, and the flow you’ve shared aligns well. Yet always remember—our Guru Bhairava is not bound by steps, but by Bhāva. He doesn't measure with checklists, but with your inner stillness, surrender, and presence. Let the practice unfold as a living offering—not by order, but by oneness. Flow with devotion. He himself will guide the next step. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: I’ve been watching your recent videos on the Dasha Bhairavas and am keen to understand how we can integrate this into our basic sadhanas. Also, when you say “Bhairava reveals,” what does that actually mean? Is it something that happens during meditation? Could you please clarify the major difference between Akshobya Bhairava and Tryambaka Bhairava? Both seem to be more serene forms compared to Kaala Bhairava. I’m likely to sit out of the recent Batuka Bhairava Vidhi, as the book you shared appears to be in Hindi.
answer: Namaskaram, Gratitude for your sincere seeking. Bhairava reveals not through logic but by dissolving the walls of separation — it can arise in meditation, in stillness, or even in deep life presence. It is not always an event, but a subtle unfolding. Akshobhya Bhairava holds the mirror of inner stillness — unshaken awareness. Tryambaka, the seer beyond time, brings integration of dualities. Both are serene, yes, but their directions of grace differ — Akshobhya calms turbulence, Tryambaka dissolves the knower itself. Regarding the Batuk Bhairava Vidhi — what was shared, is exactly as received in the flow. Yes, the material is in Hindi, and we are open to translating. If any seeker feels called to assist in making it accessible in other languages, that would be a beautiful offering to the Bhairava Anughara Sangha. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: With Bhairava’s grace, I won the Best Actor award at the Toronto Theatre Fest—without any formal training. Every day, I used to practice in front of Bhairava and ask Him to be my guru. He answered my call and lifted me from nothingness to excellence. I cried that day and fell at His feet in gratitude. I read the Ashtakam and learned it by heart. Now, when I chant, my 9-month-old son dances to it. I’ve had so many profound experiences, and I feel a deep urge to dedicate more time to learning and practicing so I can grow closer to Him.
answer: Namaskaram, Bhairava Arpanamastu Your journey is a living testimony of how our Guru Bhairava transforms nothingness into divine expression. When we offer our time, heart, and presence to Him, He fills every breath with grace. Continue walking this sacred path—He is already dancing through your child and awakening deeper truths through you. Stay steady, stay surrendered. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: When a person attains self-realization (logically, we can grasp some aspects through mystical concepts found online—such as “I am a soul having a human experience”), what does the world look like to them experientially? What gets revealed, in your view? If such a person walks through a busy city like New York, how might their surroundings appear to them? I imagine they would stop seeking material comforts and perhaps prefer a life of solitude. If, say, 100 people in your group were to become self-realized, what would they begin doing? What would they be interested in?
answer:
Namaskaram, Your questions arise not from mere curiosity—but from a heart that is beginning to remember. That itself is grace! When Self-realization dawns, it is not that the world outside changes—but the seer is no longer caught in its dance. Walking through New York or a forest, the realized one sees the same Divine play—each form, each sound, each face as a mask of Shiva, of Bhairava Himself. There is no resistance, no grasping, just witnessing. This is not renunciation of life, but full presence in it—without being pulled or pushed by it. Loneliness may arise, yes—but not out of avoidance. It blossoms as inner fullness, where company or crowd makes no difference. And if 100 truly become Self-realized—not through mind, but through Being—they won’t "do" much out of ego. Yet the fragrance of their presence will nourish the world silently, each in their own dharmic rhythm. So, ask freely. Bhairava doesn’t restrict—He invites, breaks, and embraces. May His Grace reveal what words cannot.
~Jai Bhairava ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Today was my first-ever day of doing sadhana for Batuk Bhairava. I didn’t have His photo or any way to offer bhog, as I live in a hostel and couldn’t get my hands on anything special. This was my first sincere attempt at sadhana, and I tried my level best. After I finished around 5:30 in the morning, I felt very grounded and peaceful, and went to sleep as I had a long day ahead. But when I woke up, I felt drained and couldn’t muster the same faith toward Baba. I felt guilty for struggling to feel the love I had just a few hours earlier. Given Batuka’s swabhava—that He can be approached through love—it felt as though I had betrayed Him. Still, I’ve been silently remembering Him throughout the day. I understand that sadhana is not a one-day process and that I must continue to see any real results. Though I feel discouraged by what happened, I will persevere. You’ve always said to approach Him with love, and I will continue to do so. Please help me understand and deepen my devotion toward Baba.
answer: Namaskaram, What you experienced is not failure, but a deep beginning. Our Guru Bhairava sees the sincerity of your heart beyond any offerings or outer form. Love and remembrance—even in silence or doubt—are received by him with grace. Feeling drained is also part of the cleansing; stay steady and trust the path. Your yearning itself is devotion. Keep walking… he is already walking with you. ~ Jai Bhairava. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Could you please shed light on the significance of Shukla Paksha Dashami Tithi for Batuk Bhairava Jayanti? Why is it observed in the month of Jyeshtha, and not on an Ashtami?
answer: Namaskaram, In Bhairava Tattva, nothing is random—each moment is chosen by the Divine pulse of our istha. Shukla Dashami in Jyeshtha holds the agni tattva—purifying, maturing, and revealing the fierce yet playful form of Batuk Bhairava. Unlike Ashtami, which holds deep kala energy (more linked to Kaal Bhairava), Dashami reflects the energy of victory and upward movement—a rise after inner battle. Jyeshtha is ruled by Indra and Surya, holding Tejas, where Batuk—child-form of Bhairava—shines as a light amidst darkness, showing us the playful yet penetrating path to awakening. Tantra honors time not by number, but by vibration. And in this alignment, Dashami becomes the perfect mirror for Batuk’s leela and guidance—a balance of innocence and intensity. Stay open. Guru Bhairava reveals when your connection and stage is ripened. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskaram, I’m currently facing a deep and mysterious question: I want to understand which form of Bhairava is truly mine—who is my Bhairava? Which form should I worship? I regularly worship Mahadev, visit His mandir, and offer jal to Him. But a few days ago, I came across Batuk Bhairava through Rajarshi Nandy Sir on Ranveer Allahbadia’s podcast. I then did a lot of research on Batuk Bhairava. Yesterday, on Batuk Bhairava Jayanti, I also learned about Swarnakarshana Bhairava and did some research on that roop as well. Then I found your YouTube channel and watched your videos on both Batuk Bhairava and Swarnakarshana Bhairava. Now I’m wondering: between these two forms, which one should I worship? I know this might sound greedy, but I say this with love and sincerity. I want: Love from the girl I truly care about Financial stability Happiness for my family Good health for my loved ones and myself Peace and inner joy It’s not that Mahadev or Shivji cannot fulfil my desires—they have helped me immensely. I also worship Shyam Baba, Hanuman Ji, Shani Dev, and Bhairava Ji occasionally. I’m deeply grateful to all of them. But lately, I feel a strong pull toward Bhairava sadhana. There’s a deep curiosity rising within me. Please help me understand which form of Bhairava I should lovingly and wholeheartedly welcome into my life.
answer: Namaskaram, Bhavesh, Your question has been answered in another tab " Astami tithi sadhana"... The essence is this — to truly experience Bhairava, only Sādhanā can open that doorway. No one in this existence can show you your path to Him — only you can walk it. We have shared the detailed 144-day Sādhanā in the group. Begin there, with sincerity and surrender. In time, with His grace, all that is meant will align — effortlessly, silently, and completely. He is already calling you inward. Just listen. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: In the video about Batuk Bhairava, Mahant Ji spoke about a person who came to the temple daily for years and performed the rituals sincerely, yet saw no results. How is this possible? Could this happen to us too? Also, sir, in one of your videos you mentioned that even if Bhairava is not your Ishta, He will guide you to your Ishta. Then how could that person not be guided? Did he make some mistake in the rituals, or was it due to karma or something else? I’m not pointing out a flaw—this is a genuine doubt arising from the mind. I don’t know who my Ishta is. I asked our family priest in Haridwar about our Kuldevata, and he said it is Ujjain Mahakal. But I wonder—can Mahakal be a Kuldevata? Isn’t a Kuldevata supposed to be born in the lineage? My parents and grandparents were unsure about what Ishta or Kuldevata even means. They just said that in our village, there’s a deity at the entrance, and we simply bow down there. I was clueless when I began Bhairava sadhana. I just thought—somewhere I have to start. And since there’s clear information about Bhairava Baba’s rituals available online, I decided to try one or two Ashtamis and see what happens. After that, I started receiving small hints—and Kashi was a big one. That’s when I began the Mandala properly, as you know. Now, if Baba leads me in another direction meant for me, I’m okay with that. But I’m genuinely happy with “Om Bhairavaya Namah” and don’t want to change it. Still, I wonder—what if I’m doing the sadhana incorrectly and end up like the person Mahant Ji mentioned? It feels childish to ask such doubts, but if I don’t ask, my mind will keep amplifying them.
answer: Namaskaram, Your questions arise from sincere seeking, and that itself is Bhairava’s grace. Yes, sometimes even deep efforts seem fruitless—not due to error, but because the fruit ripens only when karma, bhava (inner feeling), and timing align. Let me share a short story: Once, a woodcutter unknowingly witnessed a mantrik performing Bhairava Tantra sadhana deep in the forest. The mantrik chanted for hours but met a tragic end as Tiger attacked and he was killed, while the simple woodcutter began chanting (Just 3 times) and Bhairava appeared within moments. Bhairava then revealed—it was the woodcutter’s devotion over many lifetimes that brought the divine darshan now... Bhairava does not ignore—He prepares. Even if He is not the ishta, He becomes the path to your ishta. Whether it is Mahakal or any unknown devata of your lineage, Bhairava will guide you there—when the connection is ready. Don’t worry about perfection of rituals. Focus on bhava (devotion) and ekagrate (one-pointedness). Keep walking with "Om Bhairavaya Namah"—with trust, not doubt. He is not testing you. He is tuning you. ~ Jai Bhairava

question: Namaskar Guruji, Your videos are truly wonderful—I’ve gained deep insights from many of them. I have two questions that have been lingering in my mind and creating some doubts: If I continue chanting Om Bhairavaya Namah, I understand that one of the 64 Bhairavas may bless me as the sadhana progresses. However, many of these forms are Ugra or Ati Ugra. What if an Ugra form blesses me and I’m unable to handle that energy? I also chant Om Batuka Bhairavaya Namah along with Om Bhairavaya Namah. Is it okay to use the same Rudraksha mala for both mantras, or should I keep a separate mala for each?
answer: Namaskaram, Gratitude for your deep reflection and devotion... It’s his grace on us to know! Baba's swarupas—ugra or saumya—are not here to frighten, but to transform. He is Karuṇākarāya, the ocean of compassion. Fear only blocks the grace. If your bhava is pure, the right form will unfold when you are ready. Even unknown swarupas will guide gently, as a mother leads her child. As for the mala—this has been shared earlier in the group, please revisit (Lot more you would know). But deeper than that, ask, whom are you calling? One or many? True connection grows when love is rooted in one form. When that love matures, Baba himself will guide you to the next nama! And it’s only you would not, no one else... Nāma Japa is not merely a repetition—it is a gentle surrender. Not for a day or a moment, but a daily journey of inner purification, where the self slowly dissolves into the Divine. Let me share a small story... There were two seekers—one kept digging in one spot with full dedication, while the other kept shifting places, hoping to find gold. In time, the one who stayed found a hidden treasure, while the other was left searching. Just like that, when we pour our whole being into one name, one path, it becomes a doorway. Hopping from one to another only scatters the energy. But stay, surrender, and go deep—what you're seeking will surely reveal itself. With grace, ~ Jai Bhairava

question: How should one proceed to the next sadhana after completing all the mandalas?
answer:
Namaskaram, After completing the 48-day Mandala sadhana—which is essential for all, once done, message me directly. A simple assessment will follow. If you're ready, you’ll be guided to the next stage. If not, you may be asked to perform the 144-day sadhana. Yet, 144 (3 Mandalas) will be a Adhara, Foundation for all we do! But remember—every journey is unique. Our Guru Bhairava opens the path for each one of us in his own time. Some today, some later. It is only by His Grace that we move ahead.
~ Jai Bhairava. ~ Jai Bhairava
