question: What’s the importance of head gear or covering your head during sadhana?
answer:
Namaskaram 

Excellent and commonly asked one, During sadhana, keeping the head covered is a way to support the energetic process. 

As we practice, the energy naturally begins to move upward through the chakras, and the head — especially the sahasrara or crown — is a very sensitive point. Covering it helps contain and stabilize the flow, allowing the energy to settle within rather than dissipate outward.

There are slight differences in how this is approached for men and women. Traditionally, women are more receptive in nature and their energy tends to be expansive; covering the head helps contain and stabilize this flow. For men, it serves to calm and balance the upward movement, maintaining focus and steadiness in the practice.

So this isn’t just a ritual — it’s a way of aligning with how the body and energy system function. That’s why it’s part of the process, helping us stay balanced and receptive to what the sadhana is meant to do.

Jai Bhairava

question: My question may seem a bit weird, but once I didn't pick up my asan, and my father was standing on it. Does that mean the urja that was generated for quite some years in that asan has leaked? Sorry if this question is not appropriate
answer:
Namaskaram,

Your question is not at all inappropriate — it comes from sincerity and reverence, which are the true marks of a seeker. 

Energy (urja) created through sadhana is subtle, but it is also deeply connected to your own intention and vibration. The asan acts as a medium, not the source itself — you are the true vessel of that urja.
If someone accidentally stood on it, don’t worry — such energy doesn’t simply “leak” away. It may momentarily shift, but it naturally realigns when you resume your practice with devotion and awareness.

If you feel concerned, you can simply purify the asan — by sprinkling a little Ganga jal, lighting a diya, or chanting your guru mantra before sitting again. That’s enough to harmonize the energy once more.

Remember, bhava (devotion) sustains all urja far more than any physical act.
Keep your faith strong.


Jai Bhairava

question: I wasn't able to finish my sadhana by 12 tonight on naraka chaturdashi so I continued for maybe 10 to 15 mins post twelve chanting kunjika stotram and exactly after twelve my mind and body felt extremely sluggish making it extremely difficult to chant and body felt weaker. I prayed for forgiveness if I made any mistakes I am concerned if I might have invoked any energies I am not capable of handling as I did this for removal of obstacles regarding physical health
answer:
Namaskaram 

It’s a fierce night of Maa Kali Chandas — a time when Her presence is powerful, raw, and deeply transformative. What you experienced need not be seen as a mistake; sometimes, even a small glimpse of Her energy can shake the body and mind. Maa’s fierceness burns through layers that we may not yet be ready to face, and that can feel heavy or draining.

Your devotion and sincerity are what truly matter. You didn’t err by continuing your sadhana a little longer — She knows the heart behind every act. Simply rest now, ground yourself with calm breath, maybe light a small diya, and offer a few moments of silence in gratitude.

Let Her energy settle within you gently. It’s just Her presence — not a harm, but a cleansing. 

Jai Maa. Jai Bhairava.

question: Can we use one and same mala for the japa of Bhairava and Maa Tara?
answer:
Namaskaram 

It is possible to use the same mala for both Bhairava and Maa Tara japa, but it must be done with awareness and purity. Each mantra carries a distinct vibration — Bhairava’s energy is fierce and protective, while Maa Tara’s is compassionate and nurturing.

If you use the same mala, please follow a few simple practices:

• Before starting japa, mentally dedicate the mala to the specific deity you are invoking.
• Cleanse the mala after completing one deity’s sadhana — you can gently wave it over incense, or keep it near your altar overnight.
•Avoid mixing both mantras in one sitting; complete one practice fully before starting the other.

Ideally, having separate malas helps maintain energetic clarity and deepens your connection with each deity. But if your devotion is steady and your intention is pure, using one mala with reverence is also acceptable.

But let me remind if want to perform intense Tantra Sādhana-(If you’ve been initiated with advance mantra and practice), then it is not recommended to use the same mala for different deities or mantras. Each deity embodies a distinct tattva (energy principle), and the mala becomes imbued with that specific vibration through repeated japa.

In essence, Tantra Sādhana values energetic precision. 

If you must use one due to circumstances, perform mala shuddhi (purification) before starting a different japa — such as sprinkling with Ganga water, wafting incense, or chanting a cleansing mantra like Om Apavitrah Pavitro Va…(Shuddikarana mantra)

Grace and his blessings for your continued sadhana. 

~ Jai Bhairava

question: While I was performing nitya upasana recently, I was hearing the bell sound while sitting in my asan. When I tilted my neck upwards I was hearing the bells very clearly, and as I tilted my head downwards the sound receded. Also it was a very pleasant sound to hear. At first I panicked, then after some time of hearing the bells I did mental prarthna to guru bhairava to gently stop this as I was content with it. Then it stopped. Guruji, what was it that I experienced
answer:
Namaskaram,

What you experienced is a gentle form of inner nāda — the divine sound that arises in deep stillness. It is a sign of Bhairava’s presence and your growing sensitivity in sādhana.. Such experiences come naturally on the path of sādhana.

Just witness it with gratitude — do not cling to it or seek it again. If the mind starts longing for the same experience, it can hold you back. Witness… and move ahead. These are natural stages every sādhaka may pass through.

Stay humble, steady, and surrendered. 

Jai Bhairava

question:
With your guidance and blessings of Guru Bhairava, completed 3 mandalas. I request/ seek your guidance further as to which sadhana or upasana would I be eligible to take up next and also seek your guidance on the step by step process for the same as you had guided me earlier. As you have mentioned the upcoming mahakala ashtami is very auspicious, I would seek your guidance as to what best sadhana or practice would I be eligible to perform or start on this day.
I wanted to perform prana pratista of linga bhairava as detailed in the document but I will be traveling in the month of December and will not be able to offer daily puja to the lingam. Kindly guide me on this too
answer:
Namaskaram,

With Guru Bhairava grace you have completed 3 mandalas beautifully. At this stage, more than adding any new sadhana, just stay deep in Bhairava Nāma Japa — it is the most powerful and complete. When your inner being is truly ready, Bhairava will make it known, and I will myself reach out to guide you into the next step of sadhana.

Mahākāla Ashtami is best observed by intensifying your japa and surrender.
Linga Bhairava Prāṇa Pratiṣṭhā can be started once you return from travel, so daily care can be offered properly.

Jai Bhairava

question:
I wanted to ask whether it is compulsory to do nitya sadhana or can I just do anushthans exclusively without any nitya
Further, before starting the anushthan - can I recite ganesh ashtottaranamavali (sankalp is of Bhairava naam japa) or the should I restrict myself to bhairav naam japa only
answer:
Namaskaram,

During Anuṣṭhāna, separate nitya sādhana is not compulsory — the Anuṣṭhāna itself becomes your daily discipline when done with proper saṅkalpa.

As per ācāra, we include Pañchopachāra and Daśopachāra in the Anuṣṭhāna, which makes the sādhana complete.

You may do a brief Gaṇeśa smaraṇa before starting, but after saṅkalpa, remain only in Bhairava nāma and bhāva throughout.

~Jai Bhairava

question:
Thanks alot Guruji
Further, during the gap days between two anushthaan, I must do nitya right?
During this nitya (in gap days), can I do ganesh ashtottaranamavali, or in nitya also I must remain only in bhairav baba throughout
answer:
Namasakaram, 

You may offer Ganesh Ashtottara in the beginning as āchāra, seeking removal of obstacles — but let the main focus and bhāva of the nitya remain with Bhairava Baba. That maintains the thread of your anushthāna beautifully.
~ Jai Bhairava

question: I got a beautiful photo of baba kaal bhairav can I take a print out of it and worship it on kaal bhairav jayanti
answer:
Namaskaram 

Yes, you may surely take a print of Baba Kālabhairava’s image and worship it on Bhairava Jayanti.

Let me remind you, in Bhairava Sādhana, Bhāva is the key. Whether one worships His image, Yantra, or Linga-svarūpa, it is the intensity of devotion and surrender within that invokes His living presence.

Where there is Bhāva, there Baba Himself resides. 

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
Unfortunately, my granny passed away nine days ago.
So my question is, I was performing a simple havan on every masik tithi, both Shukla and Krishna paksha, for Maa Durga and Baba Bhairava for dev preeti.
Can I continue doing so after 13 days of sutak? My family members are saying I can't perform anusthan and havan for one year (they are also unsure), hence asking here.
Please shed some light as I don't want to abandon my sadhana, though I am quietly performing my daily nitya ones in private
answer:
Namaskaram,

Bhairava…. She has now merged back into Him who is Kālātīta and Kāmatāṅka..

May your grandmother’s soul find its onward path with light and ease. Such transitions remind us of the fragility of life, and also of the sanctity of the path you are walking.

After the completion of the 13 days of sutak, you may resume your masik havan and devotional practices. There is no spiritual restriction that binds a sincere seeker from offering pure worship after this period. What some families observe for a year is a kul-parampara — a household custom — not a rule of dharma. Customs vary, but sadhana is governed by a deeper principle.

When the intention is clean, the fire of the havan never becomes inauspicious. In fact, your devotion lends strength to both you and the departed soul. You have done well to continue your nitya sadhana quietly during this time; this steadfastness is the mark of a true practitioner.

So, proceed without fear.
Resume your havan after the 13th day, with humility, with clarity, and with the remembrance of the Divine in your heart.

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
With the blessing of Bhairava baba I finished 21-day( Ardha Mandala) for the Batuka Bhairava moola mantra as per with Viniyoga guidelines.
Would you kindly help me with the next steps
answer:
Namaskaram 

With your devotion and by the grace of Bhairava, you have beautifully completed the 21-day Ardha Mandala. May His presence continue to guide and elevate your sadhana.

You may now proceed into the full 48-day Mandala completion, continuing the Batuka Bhairava Moola Mantra with the same sincerity and discipline as before. Maintain the previously given Viniyoga guidelines, the same time-space discipline, and the same inner attitude of offering.

If you need support or clarity at any point, I am here.

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
My grandmother’s sister-in-law passed away
I don’t have any anushtan/sankalpa going on currently
Am I supposed to stop my nitya for 13 days??
Further I also wanted to ask about what should one do in case of relative’s death during an ongoing anushtan/sankalpa
answer:
Namaskaram 

May her soul find peace and grace.

Since you are not in any anushtan or sankalpa right now, you may continue your nitya practices gently. There is no need to stop them unless your family tradition specifically requires it.

For future reference:
If a relative passes away during an ongoing anushtan/sankalpa, the sankalpa is generally not broken. You may pause external rituals if needed, but you continue the mantra/japa internally and resume the full process after the required days, as guided.

If you need clarity on a specific practice, I am here.

May his grace and Blessings be with you and your family. 

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
Many thanks for your advice, Guruji.
I have few doubts over next steps:
1) I actually have to travel for work, so I can't finish the 48-day Mandala in one go. Can I take a break in between? Is it ok if I break in between?
2) After 21 days of Ardha Mandala, Beeja Akshara (Parathama charan) would be shared according with the instructions provided in Viniyoga. Would you like me to keep using the Moola mantra? 3) I tried to visualise Batuka Bharava during Mantra Japa, but I was unable to do so. Do I need to focus on the mantra or the diety during Mantra Japa
answer:
Namaskāram 

Thank you for sharing your questions with such sincerity.

Ideally a 48-day Mandala is done without break, but if travel is unavoidable, you may pause with a humble prayer to Guru Bhairava and resume from where you stopped. Keep a little mental japa during travel if possible, Note bhava is the Key in sadhana not days or number of Japas.

After 48 days (Completion of Mandala sadhana), when Beeja Akshara is shared, you will continue as per Viniyoga, and still keep the Moola Mantra as the base unless instructed otherwise.

If visualization of Batuka Bhairava is difficult, do not worry. For now, stay focused on the mantra. With time, the form reveals itself naturally.

Walk gently. Bhairava is reached through bhāva and steadiness.

Jai Bhairava

question:
Is this correct
सह मयि अंतर्गतः अस्ति
अहम तस्मीन अंतर्गतः अस्मि?
answer:
Namaskaram.

Yes, the essence is correct. The feeling of ‘He is within me, and I am within Him’ is beautifully expressed. Let this understanding deepen in your (our) sadhana.

question:
I have been chanting the nama mantra for a long period, already crossed lakhs. I'm thinking about switching to the Batuk Bhagwan mool mantra since the manasik japa of the mool mantra is very fluid and aligning very well (though I have not started it yet I was just trying it while meditation).
My question is, Guru ji, I have an image of Kashi Kaal Bhairava Bhagwan, and I worship and do seva dearly. Would I have to bring another image of Baba's Batuk form? Is it mandatory?
As always your beautiful words can enlight my path once again guru ji thanks in advance. Blessed to have you
answer:
Namaskaram,

Forgive the delay in responding…

I can feel the sincerity behind your words, and that humility itself is Bhairava’s touch upon your heart. The nāma that you have been chanting for so long has already shaped your inner space — every japa has reached Him. Nothing is ever lost.

About the Batuk Bhairava moola mantra… when a mantra begins to flow effortlessly in meditation even before you formally begin, it is simply a sign that He is moving you from within. Allow it gently, without forcing anything. When Bhairava chooses to open a door, it always feels natural and soft.

About the image — listen carefully:

Bhairava never demands another form.
He only asks for a deeper heart.

If your devotion is resting on Kashi Kalabhairava, that is more than enough. There is no rule, no compulsion, no outer requirement.
When your inner bhāva ripens, Kalabhairava Himself appears as Batuk;
when innocence rises, the fierce becomes gentle;
when surrender deepens, the One becomes many and the many become One.

So do not worry about bringing another image.
If Baba wants it, He will place that longing in you unmistakably.
Until then, keep your worship the same — but let your bhāva become sharper, purer, more surrendered.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Hope you are well, Guruji I have to ask you that from last 1 year I have been doing 3 mala of Bhairava baba naam japa everyday and I even made the promise to baba that I'll do at least 3 mala everyday and I was doing my nitya upasana regularly. But Guruji from few days because of my work pressure I am unable to do my nitya upasana and next day I get up with a lot of guilt that I've broken my promise …and this has happened many times please help
answer:
Namaskaram 

In the path of Bhairava, a vow is not measured by counting malas…it is measured by the bhāva with which you return.

Work, pressure, tiredness… these are part of life, and even the most sincere sādhaka goes through such phases.
What matters is not the one day you missed, but the inner thread of connection that has never left you.

Remember —
Bhairava does not stand with a register.
He stands with karuṇā and presence.

Your guilt itself shows your sincerity, but don’t allow guilt to become a wall between you and Him. Also mistake should be once not as it’s an excuse to skip your sadhana! 


When time becomes tight, at least sit for a moment, place your right palm on your heart, and invoke mentally:

“Baba, I am here. My breath is my japa today.”

Even this keeps your sankalpa alive and your connection unbroken.

Slowly return to your nitya-upāsana as your energy stabilises.
But return with love, not with fear.
With bhakti, not with burden.

Bhairava walks with the one who walks with honesty.
And you already are… resume your sadhana and make sure you return with love not as commitment with burden.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Gurudev is it compulsory to go towards kali if I am in bhairava upasana
answer:
Namaskaram 

No, it is not compulsory. Bhairava upāsanā is complete in itself. Kali and Bhairava are deeply connected, but the movement toward Kali happens only if and when the sādhaka is naturally guided there. Follow Bhairava with sincerity and paddhati — he will lead you exactly where you need to be.

Jai Bhairava

question:
I am blessed with an opportunity to be in Kasi and go to Kala Bhairava temple for the next 6 months.
Could you please let me know the vidhi to read Kala Bhairava ashtakam or do nama japa inside the temple?
Like which direction can I sit, Aasan can be carried with me but I don't think I am allowed to light a diya, do achamanam and do samarpan inside the temple.
I want to use this opportunity at the best with the given circumstances.
Please let me know your insights on this
answer:
Namaskaram 

Being in Kāśī and coming into the space of Kāla Bhairava itself is the greatest blessing and anugraha. If you ask me personally, when you are in front of him, do not try to do anything special.

Just see him — as if it is the first and the last darśana of your life. Let your love, gratitude, and surrender soak into him completely. In that moment, nothing more is needed — no checklist of mantra, kavacha, direction, or vidhi.

If it feels comfortable and natural to you, you may softly do nāma smaraṇa or the Bhairava Aṣṭakam within the mind, without posture, without formality. No need to carry āsana, light dīpa, or perform samarpana inside the temple unless it genuinely supports your inner stillness.

This opportunity itself is his call. Simply be present, humble, and open. The rest will happen on its own.

Jai Bhairava

question: I am not able to sit before in daytime and only 12am after I come home for my daily japa and it is getting delayed to after 12am midnight does it mean I skipped a day or even is it ok for person to do sadhana day after 12am is it only for advanced people only
answer:
Namaskaram 

It is absolutely okay. Sādhana is not broken because of time; it is broken only by a break in sincerity.

You may continue your japa after 12 AM with the same devotion. It does not mean you have skipped a day, nor is this meant only for advanced sādhakas.

Remember, Bhairava is Kālātīta — beyond time itself. He is the governor of all timeframes. No moment can escape him, and nothing offered to him is ever forgotten. When you sit with honesty and remembrance, you are already within his kāla.

What truly matters is regularity, bhāva, and steadiness, not the clock. Let go of worry and sit peacefully.

Jai Bhairava

question: Sir Namaskaram I wanted ask you regarding the effects on sadhana due to planetary positions like currently rahu dasha my schedule is not same on any given day sometimes working in morning sometimes at night unable to stay consistent I believe sadhana helps us anytime but just curious
answer:
Namaskaram 

Your understanding is very true and goes to the heart of Bhairava tattva. Bhairava is the daiva of all eight directions (Aṣṭa-dikpāla adhipati) and stands beyond the grasp of time, grahas, and karmic knots. Among his forms, Saṃhāra Bhairava is the supreme controller of dissolution—the one who arrests chaos, cuts through confusion, and brings order where there is disturbance.

Planets influence the mind and circumstances, but Bhairava governs the very forces that govern the planets. When one takes refuge in him, graha doṣas lose their authority. Rahu, Śani, or any shifting planetary current cannot overpower a sādhaka who is held in Bhairava’s grace. He alone has the power to take charge of any situation—external or internal—and turn it toward protection, clarity, and liberation.

So even when routine breaks, timing fluctuates, or discipline feels challenged, remember this: when you hold Bhairava, he holds everything else. Sādhana done in surrender to him is already protected. There is no wrong time in Bhairava’s presence—only remembrance and trust.

Stay anchored. He is watching, guiding, and correcting everything.

question:
We all consider bhairav baba as our guru and want to reach him, do sadhana and ask for his grace. But I am worried and rather scared when my nityapuja sometimes becomes a mechanical process and don't feel close to bhairav baba. Though I know he will always be there for me but I want him as my mentor, instructor and my guide who will lead me to the correct path.
How can I get closer to baba and maa??
How can I become capable of connecting to them and seek guidance from them? And how can I understand or decode their message if it comes in from our surroundings?? How can we get anugraha of bhairav baba and maa kaali? How can I start with sadhana
answer:
Namaskaram 

What you are feeling is very natural, and in fact very honest. Every sincere sādhaka passes through this phase. Please don’t be afraid of it.

First, remember this gently: Bhairava does not measure us by intensity of emotion, but by sincerity of presence.
When pūjā feels mechanical, it does not mean Baba is far — it only means the mind is tired. The bond remains untouched.

Bhairava and Maa are not outside waiting to be reached. They are already seated within you. The desire you have to be guided, to be corrected, to be closer — that itself is their grace (anugraha) already working.

Do not force feeling. Do not chase experiences.
Instead, bring simplicity and truth into your nityapūjā.

Even if you sit for a few minutes and say with honesty:
“Baba, today I don’t know how to feel you, but I am here” —
that is a complete pūjā.

To get closer:
 • Be regular, not intense
 • Be humble, not demanding
 • Be silent, not restless

Guidance does not always come as voices or visions. Most often, Bhairava speaks through clarity, restraint, sudden understanding, or a quiet inner ‘no’ or ‘yes’. When the mind becomes calm and ego reduces, decoding becomes natural — it is not something to learn, it unfolds.

Do not worry about “starting big” in sādhana.
Start small, steady, and sincere. Let the relationship mature like a living bond, not a task.

Above all, know this:
If this question has arisen in your heart, Baba has already accepted you.
Fear does not arise in those who are distant — it arises in those who care deeply.

Walk slowly. Walk honestly.
Bhairava walks with those who do not pretend.

Always under his protection and Maa’s karuṇā 

~ Jai Bhairava

question: As I am feeling heavily distracted while doing Japam. Are we allowed to do Japam opening my eyes by looking into Baba photo in my Mandir
answer:
Namaskaram 

Yes, it is perfectly alright. When the mind feels distracted, you may gently open your eyes and do the japa while looking at Baba’s photo. Let the gaze be soft—not staring—just resting in His presence.

Bhairava understands the state of the sādhaka. What matters most is continuity and sincerity, not rigid form. As the mind settles, the eyes will naturally close again. Until then, let His form anchor you.

Do not judge the distraction—offer it to Baba and continue. He Himself steadies the japa. 

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Why is the Kālabhairava Aṣṭakam considered mandatory in Bhairava Sādhana? And why did Adi Śaṅkarāchārya give it to the world?
answer:
A Sadhaka Asked…

“Why is Kālabhairava Aṣṭakam mandatory in Bhairava Sādhana?”

It’s my personal belief and experience…. That it’s One cluster of Entire Tantra.

Because Bhairava is not approached by information.
He is entered through vibration.

And the Kālabhairava Aṣṭakam is not a hymn.
It is a lock.
A key.
And for the prepared sādhaka — a point of no return.


In Tantra Śāstras it is said:

One single line of Kālabhairava Aṣṭakam contains what volumes dare not explain.

Each line secretly holds:
• the mechanics of Time (Kāla)
• the grammar of Fear
• the exit points of Karma
• the permission to face Death without trembling

This is why it works even when the mind does not “understand”.
Bhairava does not wait for understanding.
He waits for resonance.


🔻🔺Second question was more interesting, Why did Adi Shankaracharya give this to world? 

It’s deepest secrets of Tantra! In fact, Because some truths cannot be taught —
they must be hidden in plain sight.

Śaṅkarācārya encoded raw Tantric fire into verses so perfectly balanced that:
• the casual chanter feels devotion
• the scholar feels philosophy
• but the sādhaka… feels Bhairava watching

This Aṣṭakam was designed so that only inner readiness unlocks it.

No initiation papers.
No outer permission.
Only Kāla deciding you are ready.


One line is enough.
One line is complete.
One line, repeated daily, becomes a living presence.

Kālabhairava Aṣṭakam is not knowledge.
It is an encounter.

And once Bhairava is encountered 
life no longer moves by your calendar!

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
Can you please provide shree bhairav deep daan prayog for simple upasakas like us.
Will always be indebted to you. Please accept my humble reverence?
answer:
Namaskaram 

If your sādhana naturally needs an external anchor, then follow it without hesitation. Tantra never denies action—it refines it. Not every sādhaka can remain inward at all times, and the Śāstras clearly accept this. Hence, safe external upāyas are given for stability and protection.

As per Tantric paddhati, the Deep Dāna prayog prescribed for simple upāsakas and householders is intentionally non-ugra, non-initiatory, and grounded. It does not awaken forces beyond one’s adhikāra, nor does it disturb ongoing japa or sādhana.

Deepa in Tantra is not merely a lamp.
It is jñāna made visible, kāla held steady, awareness given form.

When you offer a lamp, you are offering:
• distraction into light
• fear into steadiness
• ego into witnessing

No complex mantras.
No forceful sankalpa.
No ritual pressure.

Only bhāva, continuity, and sincerity.

If the mind needs form, Tantra gives form.
If the mind dissolves into silence, Tantra allows silence.

Both paths are valid when done with honesty.
Bhairava does not measure outer complexity—
He responds to truth of effort.

When the time is right, the exact prayog will be shared in the proper paddhati.
Until then, remain steady. The light is already accepted.

Bhairava Rakshatu 

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
If one is doing ahutis/homa or something like giving offering number of times, how to keep count of it if it is more? Because if I have to offer ashtagandha in about 3 malas of mula mantra of bhairava then in which hand to hold the mala as offerings have to be made from the right hand only? Or should I use japa counter instead of mala that I regularly use for japa?
Regards
answer:
Namaskaram, 

This is a very valid and sincere question. Kula Śāstra has already accounted for such situations.

When āhutis, homa, or repeated offerings are involved, and the right hand is engaged in offering, the japa mālā that you regularly use for internal japa should not be used. This is important.

As per paddhati:

• For offerings counted along with mantra, you may either
• keep the mālā in the left hand only for counting, without turning beads for japa, or
• use a separate japa counter / counting mālā meant exclusively for kriyā or offerings.

For upāsakas, the safest and cleanest method is to use a separate counter. This keeps the sanctity of your daily japa mālā intact and avoids confusion during kriyā.

Remember, during homa or āhuti:
• Right hand → offering
• Mind & mantra → steady
• Counting → supportive, not distracting

Tantra values clarity over rigidity. 

The offering should flow without mental strain. When the intent is clear, the count becomes supportive—not a burden.

Proceed calmly. Bhairava accepts discipline when it is aligned with ease and awareness.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Guruji I have seen Maa Varahi sometimes in my dream, I don't know why but I feel naturally drawn towards Maa, Guruji I want to ask you should I also add some mantra of Maa in my nitya upasana
answer:
Namaskaram,

Such experiences happen when a sādhaka’s inner awareness begins to open. Maa does not appear to initiate chanting immediately, but to reassure you that she is present and guiding you silently. It is a sign of inner readiness, not an instruction yet.

For now, remain aware and grounded. If the experience repeats or deepens, begin gently with Nāma japa of Maa. Remember, Maa has many swaroopas—any sincere approach ultimately reaches her.

Let things unfold naturally. Awareness always comes before action.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: I've been doing BATUKA BHAIRAVA mool mantra mandala for the past 17 days. I would like to travel to Arunachalam on December 30th, should I go or do I need to finish first? Actually, I'll be carrying Jappa Mala with me and will do there
answer:
Namaskaram 

Your sincerity in asking itself shows awareness and respect for the sādhanā.
There is no obstruction in traveling during the mūla-mantra maṇḍala.

If your japa is unbroken in bhāva, place, distance, or movement does not break it.
Carry your japa mālā with you and continue the japa there with the same niyama and inner steadiness.

In fact, Arunachalam is not away from Bhairava—
it is Śiva’s stillness itself, and when approached with awareness, it supports sādhanā rather than disturbs it.

Just one thing—
do not treat the journey as an escape from the maṇḍala,
but as walking with Bhairava into another sacred space.

Let the japa continue as a flow, not a task.

He is watching. He is guiding.
Nothing is broken.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Why does a sādhaka begin to love Bhairava more than any other Devatā?
answer:
An interesting question came to me in a DM…
and it stayed with me longer than expected.

Why did I start loving Bhairava more than any other Devatā?

This is not admiration.
This is not preference.
And this is certainly not imagination.

In Tantra Śāstra, there is a truth rarely spoken aloud—Pūrva Janma Saṁskāra.
What we worship deeply once does not end with one lifetime.
Mantra, pūjā-vidhi, sādhana, fear, surrender—these leave marks far deeper than memory. They become direction.

The Devatā you once stood before in silence…
the form you once trusted with your breath…
returns—not as belief, but as recognition.

Very often, this flows through kula—ancestral lines, blood-memory, hidden Tantric bonds. Bhairava is not chosen by the mind. He emerges when the soul is ready to remember who guarded it before.

And Bhairava is not just a Devatā.

He is grace that walks into chaos.
He is the compassion that wears terror so you may be protected.
He does not promise comfort—He promises truth.
He does not ask purity—He burns illusion.

Bhairava stands for all life.
For the sinner and the saint.
For the broken, the lost, the fearless, the fallen.
He does not reject—He claims.

When Bhairava enters your life, it feels like intensity.
But what it truly is… is homecoming.

This love is not new.
It is ancient.
And it has finally remembered you.

question:
B'ful question Guruji and you explained it very beautifully
Thank you Guruji.. One ques can we chant this anywhere?
Without asan or while travelling
answer:
Namaskaram,

Yes… you may chant it anywhere.

Mantra is not bound by place — it is bound by bhāva.
When the heart remembers Bhairava, the chant is already seated.

If you are travelling, walking, or sitting without an āsana, you can chant mentally or softly within. That itself is accepted. Bhairava does not wait for outer perfection — He listens for inner alignment.

Only one gentle guidance:
 • When possible, keep formal japa (with mālā, āsana, posture) for your regular sādhanā time.
 • Outside of that, let the mantra flow naturally — as remembrance, as breath, as quiet calling.

Nothing is broken.
Nothing is lost.

If the mantra arises on its own, it is not you chanting — it is Bhairava responding.

Jai Bhairava

question: Why is Shukla Pakṣa Aṣṭamī observed as Durgāṣṭamī, while Kṛṣṇa Pakṣa Aṣṭamī is connected to Bhairava ārādhana? Why does this distinction matter for sādhana?
answer:
An excellent and very genuine question came yesterday— one that almost every seeker experiences at some point in life:

Why is Shukla Paksha Aṣṭamī called Durgāṣṭamī,( Devi Aradhana)
and why is Kṛṣṇa Paksha Aṣṭamī connected to Bhairava ārādhana?

What is the real difference, and why should we follow this?

Let us explain in simple terms: 


Aṣṭamī — the turning point of life

In Sanātana and Tantra Śāstra, Aṣṭamī is not just a tithi.
It is a sandhi — a threshold.

Just like in our lives:
• when things are about to rise, or
• when things are about to break and transform

Aṣṭamī comes when pressure is high and truth is close.

That is why Aṣṭamī always invokes Shakti in her fiercest clarity and it’s a boon for all sadhakas. 


Shukla Paksha Aṣṭamī — Durgāṣṭamī

(When the Moon is growing)

Shukla Paksha represents:
• growth
• hope
• rebuilding
• support from unseen forces

This is the phase of life when you are trying to stand again.

Here, the Divine responds as Mā Durgā —
“Yā Devī Sarva Bhūteṣu Śakti Rūpeṇa Saṁsthitā”

She comes as:
• courage when fear dominates
• protection when life feels unsafe
• strength when responsibilities feel heavy

In everyday life, this is the phase when:
• you are handling family, work, health, emotions
• you want things to improve, not collapse
• you need balam (strength), not destruction

So Maa doesn’t break you here —
She holds you.

That is why Shukla Aṣṭamī is Durgāṣṭamī. 


Kṛṣṇa Paksha Aṣṭamī — Bhairava Arādhana

(When the Moon is dissolving)

Kṛṣṇa Paksha is about:
• letting go
• facing reality
• cutting attachments
• inner silence

This is the phase when life forces you to ask:

“What is false in me?”

Here the Divine appears as Guru Bhairava —
not soft, but satya-priya (lover of truth).

“Bhairavaḥ Kāla-rūpaḥ”
He is time itself.

In real life, this is when:

• illusions break
• people walk away
• fear rises from within
• loneliness teaches you more than crowds

Bhairava does not console the ego.
He protects the soul.

That is why His ārādhana belongs to Kṛṣṇa Paksha Aṣṭamī.


Why this difference truly matters

Most suffering happens when we ask:
• Durgā to destroy, or
• Bhairava to comfort

Each Devatā responds according to Kāla (time).

When you align:
• growth phases with Durgā
• destruction phases with Bhairava

Life stops resisting you.

This is not superstition.
This is tattva-jñāna — understanding how Shakti moves.


Today Māsika Durgāṣṭamī

Today, being Māsika Durgāṣṭamī, is a day to:

• ask for protection
• stabilize emotions
• strengthen inner resolve
• receive grace without struggle, she will be with you if you call her! 

Even a simple remembrance is enough.

Please follow the details already shared in the Devi documents on the webpage.

Do not rush.
Do not force results.

“Mā knows when to act.”


Remember this for life:
• When you are building — walk with Maa
• When you are breaking — stand with Bhairava

Both are not different.
They are two movements of the same Shakti.

One lifts you.
One guards you.

Jai Mā Kamakya, Jai Bhairava

question: Why is so much importance given to Bhāva in Bhairava and Maa Sādhana? Why is feeling placed above technique, and what does Bhāva really do?
answer:
I received a question by a young Sadhaka, and it stayed with me longer than expected, so let me explain what and how? 

Q: Why do you speak so much about Bhāva in Bhairava or Mā Sādhana?
Q: Why is feeling placed above technique?
And what does Bhāva really do?

I felt like sharing this because many people do everything correctly—japa, pūjā, discipline—yet still feel dry inside. And some people do very little, but something responds… something moves.

If you are okay to listen—
or rather, to read—may I share a short story and the deeper Tantric reason behind why, in Bhairava Anugraha, Bhāva is spoken of as the very foundation of Sādhana.

🔻First, the question itself

In Tantra, mantra is sound.
Yantra is form.
Kriyā is action.

But Bhāva is life.

Without Bhāva, mantra becomes repetition.
Without Bhāva, pūjā becomes routine.
Without Bhāva, even years of practice remain dry.

Bhāva is not emotion in the ordinary sense.
It is alignment.
It is the inner posture with which the Sādhaka stands before the Devatā.

Tantra says clearly:

Devatā responds not to the tongue, but to the state of being.

🔺🔻 A short story from Purāṇic memory and my gurus words…

There is a quiet moment spoken of in the Purāṇas and Tantra Upadesha 

A devotee once stood before Bhairava—not with fear, not with demand, not even with devotion as people understand it. He stood empty.

No request.
No complaint.
No expectation.

When asked why he had come, the devotee said:

“I do not know the right mantra.
I do not know the correct vidhi.
But I know this—
I have been held, protected, and corrected all my life without asking.
If this is your doing, then my silence is my offering.”

Bhairava did not speak.
He did not appear.
He did not bless outwardly.

But from that day, the devotee’s life moved with an unseen precision—danger diverted, truth revealed, false supports removed. Not comfort, but clarity arose.

That silence… was Bhāva.

🔺 What Bhāva actually does in Sādhana

In Tantra, Bhāva is the bridge between Śakti and Śarīra (energy and body).
 
• Bhāva tunes the nervous system to receive mantra
• Bhāva tells the Devatā who you are being, not what you are saying
• Bhāva dissolves ego without effort
• Bhāva makes even a simple japa alive

This is why two people chant the same mantra for years—
one transforms, the other repeats.

The difference is not discipline alone.
It is inner orientation.


🔻 Why Bhairava responds so deeply to Bhāva

Let me remind, Bhairava does not respond to what you do.
He responds to how you stand before Him.

Fear offered honestly becomes protection.
Silence offered sincerely becomes guidance.
Gratitude offered deeply becomes guardianship.

This is why, in Bhairava Anugraha, we speak of Bhāva again and again—
because Bhāva decides whether Sādhana becomes grace
or remains effort.

To end, a gentle question

Before the next mantra…
before the next mālā…
before the next ritual…

Ask yourself:

Am I chanting to get something?
Or am I sitting because I am already held?

That answer…
is where Bhairava begins.

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
There is one curiosity that has stayed in my mind.
In the paddhati shared by you, which I follow in my practice, I notice the constant presence of the “Hreem” bija—whether it is in the mantras of Bhairava Baba or Maa Kamakhya. It appears repeatedly and prominently.
Guruji, I humbly seek your grace to enlighten us on this sacred bija—its origin, tattva, inner working, and deeper meaning—and any insight that may help us truly understand, internalize, and visualize its essence during sadhana
answer:
Namaskaram 

This is a precise and mature observation. Such questions do not arise from curiosity of the intellect, but from tattva beginning to reveal itself through sādhana.

Understand this clearly first:

The subject of Bīja tattva—especially Hreem—is traditionally revealed only after Prathama Charaṇa Sādhana, because before that stage the mind tries to interpret, whereas bīja must be entered.
However, let me disclose this much, as it is necessary for alignment.

In Tantra Śāstra, Hreem is not a devotional syllable.
It is classified as a Śakti-bīja, more specifically the Mahāyā Bīja—the seed of yoni, spanda, and prakaṭīkaraṇa (manifestation).

From a Tattva standpoint:

• Hreem represents Śakti in her undifferentiated womb-state
• It belongs to Śuddha–Śuddha tattva junction, where Śiva-tattva begins to move
• It is the bindu where time, space, and form have not yet separated

This is why Hreem is inseparable from Kamakhyā.
Kamakhyā is not worshipped as form — she is worshipped as garbha-sthāna, the unmanifest womb.
And Hreem is the vācika expression of that womb-awareness.

When Hreem appears in Bhairava mantras:

It indicates Bhairava not as destroyer, but as Kāla-sthāna-pati — the governor of time within Śakti.

In Tantra, Bhairava without Hreem is śūnya-sthiti (pure stillness).
Bhairava with Hreem is spanda-yukta śūnya — stillness that vibrates.

Bhairava and Kamakhyā are not two tattvas —
Bhairava is the witness of the womb, Kamakhyā is the womb of the witness.

Structural breakdown of the bīja (tattva-wise):
• Ha — dissolution of limitation (ākāśa-vyāpti)
• Ra — agni of movement and activation (spanda)
• Ee — inward pull of consciousness (nivṛtti-mārga)
• M̐ — bindu sealing awareness into stillness

Thus Hreem moves outward to create and inward to dissolve simultaneously.
This dual motion is why it is dominant in Devi and Bhairava paddhatis, but absent in purely Vaiṣṇava or karma-oriented rituals.

For sādhana application (important):

Do not visualize form when chanting Hreem.
Visualization comes later.
At this stage, Hreem must be held as density, not imagery —
a gravitational pull inward, as if awareness is returning to its own womb. This is just foundational note.

Remember this principle from Tantra Śāstra:

Bīja is not pronounced — it ripens.

More will be revealed only after Prathama Charaṇa, because beyond this point the explanation shifts from knowledge to direct transmission.

Stay aligned with the paddhati.
Let the bīja work. 

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
Thank you, Guruji. You explain it so well; I am always thankful for your support and guidance.
The way you explained HREEM, I had the same conception in my mind, but that understanding was earlier for “Om.” After reading the above, I now feel that “Om” is half, whereas HREEM is complete, as it has the power to both create and dissolve.
# Is my understanding correct?
# When we pronounce KIREEM, the flow of air and vibration similar to HREEM?
Also, KI + REEM seems partially similar to H + REEM. If possible, please enlighten us on how both differ in their nature and effects.
# Also, how does the BAM beej work in the Bhairava Baba mantra?
With humility and reverence at your lotus feet
answer:
Namaskaram 

Your observation itself shows that your inner listening has begun to mature.

Yes—your understanding is moving in the right direction. Om is the primordial seed, the doorway into vibration. It opens awareness. HREEM, however, carries both movement and return—creation held within dissolution. That is why in Śākta and Bhairava paths, HREEM is experienced not only as sound, but as living śakti.

Regarding KIREEM—your sensitivity to breath and vibration is good. While the outward flow may feel similar, the inner direction differs. HREEM draws awareness inward and downward into the core, uniting Śiva–Śakti. KIREEM, by its nature, activates and sharpens—it projects rather than absorbs. So the resemblance is in sound, not in tattva.

KI + REEM may appear close to H + REEM, but understand this gently:
the “Ha” in HREEM carries space, dissolution, and containment.
The “Ki” introduces separation and movement.
One melts boundaries; the other defines them.

As for BAM (BUM)—in Bhairava mantra it works silently, like a stabilizing force. It anchors energy in the body, protects the sādhaka from excess dispersion, and gives firmness to the practice. BAM does not rise quickly—it holds. That holding is Bhairava’s compassion! 

These bija are not meant to be fully grasped by intellect alone. What is important now is not comparison, but bhāva and steadiness. Deeper layers naturally unfold after sustained sādhana; till then, let experience ripen without strain.

Continue your practice with humility and attentiveness. Bhairava reveals only what the sādhaka is ready to carry.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Recently my father's younger brother passed away. So the 16 days ritual process is going on. 3rd day tomorrow going to dissolve ash in the river. The black mourning prayer already completed. After third day shall I start Bhairava Upasana? Or Shall I wait till the 16th day to complete? As I'm being advised to not to lit deepam in house. What shall I do? For 1 year there's block to visit hill temples, not to do archana in temples and to break coconuts etc. Can I do full fledged Bhairava Upasana after 16th day or 3 rd day? Kindly advise
answer:
Namaskaram 

first—please accept my silent prayers for the departed soul. May Guru Bhairava hold him gently beyond time.

Remember this truth softly: Bhairava is Kālātīta—beyond time, beyond impurity, beyond ritual boundaries. As the Lord of Kāla, He alone holds every soul, whether in birth, transition, or release. Nothing moves outside His gaze.

Now, coming to your question—listen without fear.

During the initial days of ritual observances, it is right to honor family discipline and not create conflict in the household. There is no need to force external upāsanā. Bhairava does not demand lamps, coconuts, or temple steps to remain present.

From the 3rd day onward, you may resume Bhairava smaraṇa internally:
• Silent japa in the heart
• Mental offering
• Remembering His name with surrender

This does not violate any observance, because remembrance is not ritual—it is awareness.

If advised not to light dīpa or perform external pūjā, honor that advice outwardly. Bhairava is not offended by restraint; He is pleased by balance.

After the 16th day, you may gradually return to full external Bhairava upāsanā at home—lamp, japa, and regular practice—as feels natural and peaceful.

Regarding temple visits and archana restrictions for a year: understand this gently—Bhairava does not reside only in hills or sanctums. He walks with the sādhaka. Your inner connection, your discipline, and your bhāva remain untouched.

Do not rush. Do not suppress. Just stay connected.

When the time is right, practice will bloom again—untouched, unblocked, and protected.

May Bhairava, the holder of all souls and all moments, hold you too.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Can I do the nāma japa of Baba Bhairava while walking on the road?
answer:
Namaskaram 

Yes, you may chant Bhairava’s Nāma while walking or during travel. Nāma-japa is not bound by place or posture. Wherever there is remembrance with śraddhā, the mantra becomes alive.

Chant gently, with awareness, and without haste or distraction. When the mind is steady and the bhāva is pure, Bhairava accepts the japa in all states.

Let His Nāma accompany your steps—it itself becomes protection and guidance.

Jai Bhairava

question: I have been reciting the Vatuka Bhairava Brahma Kavacham and Aṣṭottara Śatanāmāvali 8–11 times daily, mostly between 3–4 am. Could such practice unknowingly invoke any ugra or śmaśāna-tattva energies?
answer:
A revived question came in a DM and is shared for the benefit of all sādhakas.

🔺A sādhaka shared that they are reciting Vatuka Bhairava Brahma Kavacham and Vatuka Bhairava Aṣṭottara Śatanāmāvali 8–11 times daily, mostly during 3–4 am, and sought clarity on whether such practice could unknowingly invoke any ugra or śmaśāna-tattva energies.

🔻Let me share in details as why and what: 

Vatuka Bhairava is Bāla-svarūpa.
He is niyama, rakṣā, and saṁyam embodied.
The Brahma Kavacham and Aṣṭottara Śatanāmāvali are prescribed for protection and stabilisation, not for awakening ugra or cremation-ground currents.

When mantra is approached with śraddhā, sustained by bhāva, and sealed in vinaya, it does not disturb the sādhaka. It orders him.

In śāstra, kavacha is defined as rakṣā-bandhana—a conscious sealing of the sādhaka within ordered śakti.
Its function is stability, protection, and restraint, never agitation.

Vatuka Bhairava, as Bāla-svarūpa, presides over:
– niyama (discipline)
– rakṣā (protection)
– saṁyama (containment of energies)

Therefore, recitations such as Brahma Kavacham and Aṣṭottara Nāma-japa do not activate śmaśāna or ugra currents. Such currents arise only through specific vidhis, bhāva, and saṅkalpa, never accidentally.

Brahma-muhūrta is recognised in śāstra as sāttvika and supportive.
Yet even this is secondary—the sādhaka’s inner orientation is primary.

Where bhāva is surrender and refuge, Bhairava appears as guardian, not destroyer.

Hence:
– Rakṣā-vidhi stabilises
– Bāla-devatā disciplines
– Fear dissolves through clarity

Let sādhana be steady, not strained.
Let mantra protect, not be tested.

Therefore, remember:

Ācharaṇa (Do’s)
– Maintain Swatchata of body and mind
– Sit with steadiness, facing east or north
– Keep bhāva of rakṣā and refuge
– Conclude with namaskāram or silent surrender

Varjana (Don’ts)
– Do not practise with fear or mental projection
– Do not combine with ugra or experimental sādhana
– Do not inflate numbers through restlessness

Bhairava does not agitate the sincere.
He grounds, protects, and clarifies.

Proceed without anxiety.
Let the sādhana carry you—do not try to carry it.

~ Jai Bhairava.

question:
Guruji Pranam –
I find myself goal-oriented and driven. This trait serves me well professionally and has supported the discipline required for sadhana, sometimes during periods of sickness and exhaustion. As I read your Q&As and develop my intuition through the mandala, I am seeing that this same drive carries a subtle ego and a rajasic urge to achieve and “progress” in sadhana.
How does one relinquish this rajasic achievement-orientation while retaining discipline and seeking Bhairava with intensity, dissolving ego and surrendering to the truth that nothing moves without His grace?
Is there anything specific I could do, or an intention I could hold during the viniyoga, to help address this
answer:
Namaskaram 

What you have seen in yourself is not a flaw — it is clarity arising.
The same force that gives you discipline and perseverance is now maturing into discernment. This itself is Bhairava’s grace.

In the early stages of sādhana, rajas is not an enemy.
It is rajas that brings you to the seat, even when the body is tired and the mind resists. But as awareness deepens, Bhairava slowly reveals the subtle knot — the doer who wishes to progress, attain, arrive.

Understand this gently:
Bhairava does not ask you to abandon discipline.
He asks you to release ownership of it.

Do your sādhana with the same steadiness, but shift the inner axis from “I am doing” to “This is being done through me.”
Discipline remains; ambition dissolves.

During viniyoga, you may hold a very quiet sankalpa — not as a demand, but as an offering:
Na kartā asmi — tvad-anugraheṇa sarvam.
(I am not the doer; all moves by Your grace.)

Then let the mantra breathe you, rather than you repeating the mantra.

When rajas rises, do not suppress it and do not judge it.
Simply place it at His feet — the urgency, the effort, the wish to progress — and continue. Bhairava does not break the ego by force; He starves it of authorship.

Intensity in Bhairava is not speed.
It is depth without expectation.

You are not correcting your path.
You are being lightened on it.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Why does Bhairava hold Chandra, the Moon, on His head?
answer:
Namaskaram, 

A few days ago, a little sadhaki sent me a DM, soft, sincere, and filled with genuine curiosity.
She asked: Why does Bhairava hold Chandra (the Moon) on His head?”

The question stayed with me. Not because it was simple.. But because it was so pure.
So I felt called to share it here, for everyone.

Guru Bhairava is never depicted by chance. Every symbol on Him is a Tantric instruction and deeply connected to siva tattva.

Chandra is not decoration.
Chandra is Manas — the mind.
Chandra is Time — movement, rhythm, fluctuation.

The Moon waxes and wanes.
Just like emotions. Just like fear. Just like desire.

Bhairava is Kālātīta — beyond time.

When Bhairava holds Chandra on His head, it means:

Time itself is restrained by Consciousness.
- Mind cannot dominate Him.
- Emotions cannot disturb Him.
- Cycles cannot bind Him.

This is why Bhairava is the Guardian of thresholds — birth, death, fear, dissolution.
That is why Bhairava sādhana, is a foundation for everyone:

- Stabilizes our mind
- Dissolves any anxiety in life and life situation
-Burns fear at its root
-Brings clarity without softness

Not comfort.... A Clarity.

Even in Kaula and Nath tradition 

Chandra is Soma — Amṛta (nectar).
The skull garland is Mṛtyu — death.

Together they reveal a Tantra truth:

One who accepts death fully, becomes the holder of immortality...

Why the Moon Is Calm on Bhairava

Because Bhairava absorbs turbulence.
He anchors the fluctuating mind.

That is why He is invoked in:

-Mental unrest
-Unseen fears
-Psychic disturbances
-Black magic afflictions

Where Chandra trembles, Bhairava stabilizes.
This is Laya not control, not suppression, dissolution.

Bhairava does not remove your mind. He gently places it at His feet

~ Jai Bhairava

Om Bam Bhairavāya Namaḥ

question:
I have a question related to Durga devi sadhana
Some say that completely surrendering to devi and to consider whatever happens in life is her grace shall be the approach
whereas some say that as a devi sadhak, one shall have a fighting attitude and shall fight reality
Which one is the correct approach or is it that I have misunderstood some concept and both go simultaneously
answer:
Namaskaram 

This is a very sincere and beautiful question, and I’m glad it has arisen in you.

In truth, there is no contradiction between the two approaches. What appears as two paths are actually two expressions of the same inner state.

When we say “complete surrender to Devi”, it does not mean passivity or helplessness. Surrender means trusting that whatever unfolds is within Her awareness and grace, even when it is challenging. It is the inner acceptance that She is guiding the journey.

At the same time, Durga Devi is the embodiment of śakti, courage, and righteous action. A Devi sādhaka naturally develops a fighting spirit—not driven by ego or anger, but by clarity, dharma, and inner strength. This is not fighting reality, but standing firmly within it, with Devi as your force.

So both go together:

Inner surrender — “Mother, I trust You completely.”
Outer courage — “Mother, act through me.”

When surrender is genuine, the fighting spirit becomes pure.
When courage is aligned, surrender becomes effortless.

You have not misunderstood—your question itself shows that your understanding is maturing. Let your sādhana slowly reveal this balance through experience rather than concepts.

Maa will teach you directly and Bhairava will hold you.. In there own way

~ Jai Bhairava

question: I came across some reels online and, inspired by them, began chanting a Kālabhairava bījākṣara mantra for prosperity. Since then I have been sensing a presence beside me while sleeping and behind me at night. What is happening, and what should I do?
answer:
Namaskaram 

Recently, I received a sincere question from a new sādhaka. He shared that he had come across a few reels on Instagram and, inspired by them, began chanting a Kālabhairava bījākṣara mantra. The content he saw mentioned that chanting this mantra could bring prosperity, luck, health, and wealth.

Over the past couple of months, however, he started noticing certain experiences. While sleeping, he felt as though someone was beside him. At times—especially while climbing stairs or walking late at night—he sensed a presence behind him. Even at home, he felt that there was something subtle yet perceptible around him.

When I gently enquired whether he was following any specific sādhana, he confirmed that he had been chanting the Kālabhairava bījākṣara mantra he learned online.

With care and clarity, I advised him to pause this practice immediately and instead return to safer, foundational chanting—specifically Kālabhairava Aṣṭakam or the Mūla Mantra. I felt it was important to explain why this foundation is essential, especially for a new sādhaka.

The Mūla Mantra – “Om Bhairavāya Namaḥ” provides stability and grounding. It helps the sādhaka’s energy settle and align gradually. Bījākṣara mantras, on the other hand, are powerful invocations. When chanted, they naturally attract subtle energies. Kālabhairava is accompanied by his gaṇas, and invoking such mantras brings their presence closer.

To hold, understand, and remain balanced around such energies, one must be adequately prepared—physically, mentally, and energetically.

This is why, according to the Anustana, Bhairava Anugraha Paddhati, it is always advised to begin with the foundational practices. Only after proper grounding and assessment should one move forward into the Prathama, Dvitīya, or Tṛtīya Charaṇa stages.

For every sādhaka, safety, stability, and inner readiness come first. Invocation without preparation can be overwhelming. Hence, beginning with the Mūla Mantra allows the sādhaka to grow steadily and consciously before engaging with deeper energies.

May this serve as a gentle reminder to all who are beginning their journey.

Jai Bhairava

question:
I have a question about the iconography and perception of Guru Bhairava.
Bhairava is consistently depicted as a powerful, muscular, human-like figure with blazing eyes, standing at smashans, accompanied by a dog. These forms are clearly not fully human, yet still "human-like".
At the same time, Bhairava is understood as all pervasive formless consciousness. In that light, the physical imagery feels symbolic rather than literal.
Your recent Q&A to another shishya who began perceiving Bhairava’s gaṇas, along with many accounts of gaṇas appearing fearsome, raises a related question for me.
Are these iconographic forms of Bhairava based on actual darśan by rishis / perception experienced by someone, OR symbolic mental constructs used to give form to something that is formless?
I also personally ask this because sometimes during dhyaan I wonder am I imagining a human or a burning flame
answer:
Namaskaram 

Your question is very sincere and arises from genuine inner contemplation — that itself is a sign of start of Anughara. 

Bhairava is indeed nirākāra, all-pervasive chaitanya. At the same time, compassionately, He allows Himself to be experienced through form so the limited human mind and heart can relate, surrender, and stabilize in dhyāna. The iconography you mention is both revealed and symbolic — revealed in the sense that ṛṣis and siddhas have perceived Him through inner darśan, and symbolic because no single form can ever limit or contain Him.

Forms like the muscular body, blazing eyes, smashāna, dog, gaṇas — these are doorways, not boundaries. They communicate tattva: fearlessness, mastery over time and death, guardianship, and raw awareness beyond social conditioning.

Regarding gaṇas and fearsome visions — remember, perception varies according to the seeker’s inner samskāras and stage of sādhana. What appears fearsome to the mind is often energy without filters. As awareness matures, fear dissolves into clarity.

Your experience in dhyāna — whether a human-like presence or a burning flame — is natural. Do not over-question it. Bhairava may appear as form, light, flame, vibration, or even vast silence. What matters is not what you see, but whether your mind becomes still, surrendered, and grounded.

Let the experience unfold without labeling it as imagination or reality. Bhairava is beyond both.

Continue your practice with steadiness and humility. When the time is right, understanding will arise on its own — not as an answer, but as certainty.

He is always watching us! 

Jai Bhairava

question: I have a quick question- I am offering lemons to Lord Bhairava ( for ashtami ) for the first time - normally with Bhog I consume it after but unsure what to do with the lemons afterwards ( leaving 5 at lords feet during sadhana )
answer:
Namaskaram,

That’s a very sincere and beautiful question. 

For Guru Bhairava, lemons are usually offered as a symbol of surrender and protection. 
Let me tell why? Lemons are offered not as bhog but as a grahaka dravya—they absorb and carry away dosha, bhaya, and negative vāyus invoked or released during sādhanā. For this reason, they are not consumed after worship.

Placing the five lemons at Bhairava’s feet symbolizes surrender of pancha-dosha and protection of the pancha-prāṇa. After the sādhanā, they should be disposed respectfully—traditionally at a temple, under a tree, at a crossroads, or in flowing water—without bringing them back into household use.

This aligns with the Bhairava tattva, where certain offerings are meant for śamana and rakṣhā, not prasādha.

Trust your bhava more than rules—if the offering is made with devotion, Bhairava surely receives it. 

~ Jai Bhairava

question: What is the place of Sankalpa in Bhairava Ārādhana and Bhairava Sādhanā?
answer:
Namaskaram.

A beautiful question arose from a sadhaka—about the place of Sankalpa in Bhairava Ārādhana and Bhairava Sādhanā. Let me share this gently, from the space of Bhairava Consciousness.

Sankalpa is not a rule you impose upon yourself.
It is the ground you stand on so that the sādhanā can stand within you.
Whether it is for 11 days, 21 days, 48 days, or one mandala, two mandalas, or more—
the measure is not time, but the truth of your promise to the Devatā.

Sankalpa is your quiet assurance to Bhairava:
“I will return… every day… with the same intention, the same surrender, the same love.”
It is continuity of the heart, not discipline of the mind.

Vinayoga is the living breath of that promise.
Each day, you sit again in the same inner space and say—not in words, but in presence—
“I have come. I am here. I am honoring what I vowed.”
It grounds you. It steadies you. It brings you back to yourself.

So do not see Sankalpa and Vinayoga as separate.
In Bhairava Anugraha, they are part of a single process of remembrance—
a process that goes far beyond nāma-smaraṇa or nāma-japa.

When you take a Sankalpa, you are saying:
I am willing. I will stay. I will not turn away.
And Bhairava, in His own way, keeps drawing you back—not by force,
but by love.

The deeper the surrender, the deeper the bow,
the more effortlessly He enters your life.
Bhāva is born when Sankalpa is lived, not merely taken.

May your sādhanā mature in this quiet space, just remember… He is watching you! 

Jai Bhairava.
Jai Mahā Kāmākhyā.

question: I need you guidance on how to change baba's photo from a small one to a bigger frame, as I'm planning to shift to a different house by baba's and Maa grace and I always had this desire that whenever I'll shift to a different house I will get a bigger photo of baba and get a new biggest(in my pooja graha) photo frame of Maa Dakshineshwar Kali(I don't have any photo of Maa yet), please guide me on how to do and what to do with the small photo of baba, I've been worshipping baba on this small photo since last almost 2 years now and it's Kashi Kaal Bhairava baba's photo
answer:
Namaskaram

This thought itself has come only by Baba’s and Maa’s grace. There is nothing wrong in your desire—it is very natural when devotion matures, it wishes to express itself more fully.

Changing Baba’s photo from a small one to a bigger frame is not a change in connection. Baba is not in the size of the photo; He is in the bhāva with which you have been worshipping Him all these years. That bond remains untouched.

When you shift to the new house, on an auspicious day, you may bring the bigger photo and frame it with calmness and gratitude. Before placing it, sit quietly for a few minutes and mentally thank Baba for the last two years of guidance and protection through the small photo.

The small photo should not be discarded. You may:

Keep it respectfully in your pooja graha, or
Place it inside a clean cloth and keep it safely, or
Continue to use it for travel or personal japa if you feel connected ( its will have a urjā (energy) in it)

Regarding Maa Dakshineshwar Kali, bringing Her photo for the first time is also a blessing. Place Maa and Baba with equal reverence. No elaborate ritual is needed—simplicity, gratitude, and steadiness are enough.

Let everything happen naturally, without hurry or anxiety. Baba and Maa are guiding this transition.

Jai Bhairava.

question:
I have a question
Is Hanuman ji a form of Bhairava, or are they related tantrically? Please elaborate.
answer:
Namaskaram,

This is a very good question, and it arises only when one’s understanding is slowly moving from outer worship to inner tattva.

Hanumān ji is not a direct form of Bhairava, but tantrically they are deeply connected through the principle of Vīra-tattva and Śakti-sevā.

In Tantra Śāstra, Bhairava represents Kāla-tattva (time, dissolution, guardianship of thresholds), while Hanumān represents Prāṇa-tattva (life-force, courage, unwavering service).
One governs fear and death, the other governs strength and fearlessness—but both stand on the same axis of Vīra bhāva.

Tantrically:

- Bhairava is the Kṣetrapāla (guardian of the field, cremation ground, liminal spaces).
- Hanumān is the Mahāvīra, the awakened prāṇa that can move freely through those spaces without fear.

This is why in many Kaula and Nātha traditions:

Hanumān is invoked before intense Bhairava or Śmaśāna sādhana, and Bhairava is worshipped to stabilize the immense prāṇa that Hanumān awakens.

Symbolically:

- Bhairava cuts fear.
- Hanumān burns fear through devotion and strength.

Both are brahmacārī ( Virachi Vatuka Swaroopa) in essence, both are protectors of sādhakas, and both respond strongly to discipline, humility, and steadiness, not display.

So understand it this way:
Hanumān is not Bhairava, but one who can stand before Bhairava without fear, because his prāṇa is completely surrendered.
When devotion matures, these distinctions dissolve into experience rather than concept.

Continue your sādhana simply. Let realization come through anubhava, not only through knowing.

~ Jai Bhairava.

question:
Kali Kali Kali
Thank you very much Swamiji 
In one of you video you mentioned that Maa and Baba are two sides of the same coin, which means ultimately when one has reached that highly advanced state in sadhana both Shiva and Shakti is one but as a new Upasakas like us we have an ishta devata or devi(ultimately both will become one, where Bhairava baba is worshipped, Maa comes and vice-versa), and specifically for Kalika Maa, it's always said to keep her photo as the biggest if she is you ishta. I very strongly feel some deep connection with Maa and it keeps on increasing as a move the path of Bhairava upasana, but the strange thing is I've not even chanted a single mala of any Maa's mantra or nama japa of Maa, while I love baba immensely and it is only because of him I'm able to walk the path, but when I think about Maa I start melting, things fade away, body becomes weak in surrender, I don't know how to explain these in words. 
You just mentioned to keep both Maa and Baba at equal reverence, but I feel the bhava for Maa slightly more than baba. I know this is my limited human understanding, it's all baba's play. In one of you videos you also said that for some upasaka's baba will show the way to their ishta and for some he himself will become the ishta, as time unfolds itself baba only knows what will happen, my simple yet silly question is can I keep Maa's photo biggest even bigger than baba or it has to be of the same size?
Jai Maa Kali
answer:
Namasakaram,

What you are experiencing is very natural and very sacred.
When the heart melts without effort, it is Maa herself responding — not through mantra, but through bhāva. That weakness you feel is not weakness; it is śaraṇāgati.

Bhairava and Kali are not divided. Bhairava opens the path; Maa draws the heart. Sometimes the Guru walks ahead, sometimes the Mother pulls us inward — both are the same play.

Keep this simple and free of doubt:
If your heart bows more deeply to Maa right now, let it. You may keep Maa’s photo bigger if that is where surrender naturally flows. There is no rule above bhāva. When devotion is true, balance happens on its own — not by force, but by grace.

Do not analyze this too much. Just stay honest, humble, and surrendered.
Baba is watching. Maa is holding.

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
Swamy prana devaru is been a guiding force all my life and I still start my day with his anugraha.
But subtle level some where I feel I have changed the course of approch after bhairava baba has entered now being in baba path most of my energy and focus has shift drastically and it seems to be unwavering yet my day start with Nitya karma kula devru, prana devru and other deities which is been worshiped for long time at same level of bhakti bhava and ista
Does this understanding of being in loop of sadhana and Nitya karma are in same page. Kindly help us to understand this aspect
answer:
Namaskaram,

Yes, your understanding is very true and very healthy. 

Nitya karma and kula–prāṇa devatā worship form the foundation of life; they keep us rooted, protected, and balanced. Bhairava sādhanā is the current that awakens and redirects inner energy. One does not cancel the other — they move on the same page, but at different levels.

When Bhairava enters, focus naturally becomes intense and unwavering. This does not mean earlier devotions are reduced; they become contained within this deeper current. Starting your day with Prāṇa Devru and kula devatā is dharma. Walking the path of Bhairava is grace. Dharma gives stability; Bhairava gives direction.

There is no loop or conflict here — only maturation.
What has changed is not devotion, but center of gravity.

Remain as you are: steady in nitya karma, surrendered in sādhanā.
When alignment is true, all devatās walk together.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: I fell ill badly a couple of weeks ago, so I was just continuing my sadhana with bare minimum procedures for the sake of continuation. Now that I have recovered, I feel kind of lazy and not meditative. I feel mechanical whenever I sit for sadhana. But I carry on regardless. Is there a fix for it
answer:
Namaskaram Maa,

Do not fight this phase. Illness drains the prana, and when prana is rebuilding, the mind feels dull and mechanical. This is not a fall, it is recovery.

The most powerful thing is this: you did not stop.
Even in weakness, you held on to your sadhana. That sincerity carries more weight than any deep meditation.

Do not chase experience. Do not demand bhava. Just sit. Even if it feels dry, offer the dryness also at His feet. Mechanical repetition done with honesty is greater than emotional intensity done for experience.

For some time, be gentle. Reduce pressure. Increase surrender.
Before beginning, simply say from the heart, “Baba, I have nothing not even devotion. I offer You even this.”

This is not a problem to fix. It is a phase to pass through.
Stay steady. The rasa will return deeper, purer, and without ego.

He is not measuring your meditation. He is holding your intention. 

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
Shri guru bhairavaya namah
I had a question guru ji
Are bhairava and lord Kartikeya same if yes then how and why there is a sudden awakening of skanda energy
Like bhairava energy came on the surface in 2024 and skanda in 2025 in the year of mars
answer:
Namaskaram 

Sorry for late response 🙏🏽

Excellent one, Bhairava and Skanda are not the same in form, yet they arise from the same Param Tattva - Supreme Consciousness.

Bhairava as known is the fierce, soft and Guru Kripa, where he is time transcending awareness the dissolver of fear and ego, the power of Kala Tattva as Ksetrapala that breaks limitation.

Skanda (Kartikeya) is the awakened divine warrior the disciplined, radiant fire of action, the expression of Shakti Pravaha in motion as Ksetra Adhyaksa

When Bhairava awakens, there is purification and inner dissolution. But, When Skanda, Murga awakens, there is direction, courage, and focused will…!

What seems like different yearly energies is often the unfolding of one deeper process
first inner clearing… then organized divine movement.

This is not sudden. It is Anugraha working through time… and this will rise more! 

Stay steady. Stay sincere.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: What are the Aṣṭa Siddhi connected to Bhairava Sādhana?
answer:
Namaskaram,

I have received a DM question about “Ashta Siddhi connected to Bhairava sadhana”. Instead of answering it individually, I felt it is important to share this with the entire group, because many may carry the same doubt silently.

In Bhairava sadhana, Ashta Siddhi is not about display of power. It is about the transformation of your very being. Below are deeper pointers for each Siddhi.

Anima
This is the power of becoming subtle. In Bhairava sadhana, it means the shrinking of the ego, where the loud sense of “I” dissolves and awareness becomes finer than a thought. You begin to enter the innermost space of your own consciousness.

Mahima
This is expansion. When Bhairava’s grace unfolds within, your awareness no longer feels confined to body and identity. You begin to experience vastness, as if your presence is not limited to where you stand.

Garima
This is spiritual weight. Not heaviness of body, but depth of being. You become so rooted in truth and dharma that no external situation can shake your inner balance.

Laghima
This is lightness. As karmic impressions burn in the fire of sadhana, mental burdens drop. You feel inwardly light, free from unnecessary reactions and emotional density.

Prapti
This is the ability to access. In deeper states of Bhairava sadhana, knowledge, clarity, and subtle perception arise without struggle. What needs to be known comes at the right time.

Prakamya
This is fulfilled intention. When your desires are purified and aligned with dharma, your sankalpa gains strength. What you resolve from a pure state begins to manifest naturally.

Ishitva
This is inner sovereignty. You stop being controlled by impulses and tendencies. Instead, you begin to govern your own mind with steadiness and awareness.

Vashitva
This is natural influence. Without domination or force, your presence itself carries power. Your words hold weight because they arise from tapas and inner clarity.

Let us all understand this clearly. In our Bhairava sadhana, Siddhis are not the destination. They are signs that the inner transformation is happening. The highest Siddhi is mastery over oneself and unwavering awareness in every moment.

Siddhi is a milestone, not the destination.

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
Watched all videos on ashta bhairva
But Kapala Bhairava Video not found
Any plans on making that special video soon??
Also I have a question
What is the significance of Narshima in tantra path?
Many people say that Narsimha behaves like Bhairava aadhara in Kali upasana?? However Bhairava and Kali are same
kindly shed some light on this with your views
answer:
Namaskaram 

So happy to know you have watched all the Ashta Bhairava videos with sincerity. That itself shows your involvement in the path.

Regarding Kapala Bhairava the video has already been shared earlier.

Now coming to your beautiful question, let me give my best to share my views on this: 

In Tantra, Narasimha represents Ugra Chaitanya, the explosive force that destroys adharma instantly.

He is:
• Krodha of Dharma
• Protection in extreme situations
• Sudden removal of inner and outer negativity

In tantric understanding, Narasimha is not just a Vishnu avatara, he is a protective frequency of consciousness that manifests when bhakta’s surrender becomes absolute.

Bhairava and Kali: Are They Same?

In tattva, yes — Bhairava and Kali are non-different (Abheda Tattva).

• Bhairava is the dynamic awareness (Chit)
• Kali is the dynamic power (Shakti)
• They are Shiva–Shakti inseparable

However, in upasana:
• Kali sadhana invokes intense transformative Shakti
• Bhairava acts as Aadhara (supporting consciousness) that stabilizes that Shakti

This is why some traditions say:

“Narasimha behaves like Bhairava Aadhara in Kali upasana.”

Meaning — just like Bhairava grounds Kali’s fierce energy, Narasimha grounds and protects the sadhaka in intense Shakti paths.

But understand carefully 
These are not competitions of devatas.

At higher level:

• Narasimha = Protective Ugra Vishnu Tattva
• Bhairava = Ugra Shiva Tattva
• Kali = Ugra Shakti Tattva

All arise from one Paramatattva.


In simple words:

When Shakti becomes intense → you need stable consciousness.

That stable consciousness is called Bhairava.

In Vaishnava tantra, that stabilizing force appears as Narasimha.

Hope this brings clarity, not confusion.
Beautiful question, it shows you are contemplating beyond surface level.

~ Jai Bhairava
