question:
I was studying the Bhairava Ashtottara Shatanamavali and compared three commonly available sources:
Stotranidhi
Kamakhya Bhairava Upasaka Foundation
DrikPanchang
About 70 names appear identical across all three, forming what seems like the stable core of the namavali. However 20–30 names vary slightly between sources (examples: Kshetrajña/Kshetrada, Nagakesha/Nagapasha, Kaladhara/Shadadhara).
Humbly asking for clarity:
1. Is there a preferred Bhairava namavali used in the Nath tradition?
2. Are these differences due to different lineages or puja vidhi
answer:
Namasakaram,

Very beautiful observation, and I truly appreciate the sincerity with which you are studying the Bhairava Ashtottara. This itself shows your bhava and shraddha towards Baba. 

Yes, what you have noticed is absolutely natural. In many stotras, namavalis, and paddhatis, such differences do happen. Around the core names, some names may vary slightly depending on:

- Sampradaya-bheda (lineage-specific transmission)
- Paddhati-bheda (ritual method differences)
- Uccharaṇa / paṭha-bheda (recensional or oral transmission variations)
- Regional textual preservation
- Specific Bhairava focus (tattva, ksetrapala, ugra, rakṣaka, kāla, or mantra-devata aspect)

So yes, these variations are not necessarily “wrong,” but often reflect different streams of Bhairava upasana.

In the Nath parampara, what matters most is not intellectual comparison alone, but what has been received through Guru kripa and the specific paddhati given in your sadhana stream. If a namavali is being practiced within a living lineage or a Guru-guided method, that becomes the stronger reference than publicly available compilations.

So my humble suggestion would be:

If you are doing this as general Bhairava bhakti, you may use any one authentic version consistently with shraddha.
If you are doing it as sadhana / upasana under a specific parampara, then always follow the version given in that lineage, even if some names differ elsewhere.

Bhairava is not limited by wording alone bhava, consistency, and Guru-anusarana carry far more weight than getting disturbed by every textual variation.

Do not get caught in over-analysis.
Take the stable core as a blessing, and for the varying names, understand them as different doors opening to the same Bhairava tattva.

In sadhana, alignment is more important than comparison.
In bhakti, surrender is more important than perfection.

Bhairava is not attained by collecting versions.
He is revealed when one version is entered with depth, discipline, and surrender.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: I was wondering how to approach this. If we’re missing an offering, say the candles can we get diya lights when we can and visualize ourselves giving him a mental offering (manasa puja) as a substitute. So visualized candles, incense, etc. I’ll run out of candles soon and won’t be able to get any do a few days. How will that effect sadhana progress
answer:
Namaskaram,

This is a very genuine question, and I’m glad you asked it with sincerity.

See, Mānasa Pūjā (mental offering) is powerful, and it absolutely has its own place in the spiritual path. If due to unavoidable circumstances, for a short period, you mentally offer with bhāva, Bhairava surely sees the devotion behind it.

But let me explain this very simply…

Imagine you are sitting in a hotel, extremely hungry… almost starving.
Food is right in front of you, the opportunity to physically eat is available… but instead of actually eating, you simply close your eyes and say,
“I am eating… I am eating…”

Will that satisfy your hunger?
Will your body receive nourishment?
No.

In the same way, when the opportunity exists to offer physical dravya candle, deepam, incense, naivedya and we choose only mental substitution out of convenience, then it does not carry the same weight as actual physical offering.

Why?
Because a true Sadhaka does not remain only in thought 
he/she engages body, mind, prāṇa, discipline, and devotion together.

That is what makes a Sadhaka different from a simple Aradhana.
 
• Aradhana may pray with feeling.
• Sadhaka enters into discipline, effort, physical offering, tapas, and sankalpa.

So if candles are temporarily unavailable for a day or two due to genuine reasons, do not panic.
Offer with full bhāva mentally for that period and continue sincerely. Bhairava is not counting to punish you. He sees your intention.

But whenever the opportunity is there, always prefer the physical vedi and physical offering.
That is the path of commitment.
That is what deepens the current.
That is what gradually transforms a seeker from devotee to Upasaka… and from upasaka to Sadhaka.

So for a short unavoidable gap:
• Do Manasa Puja with full sincerity
• Mentally offer deepa, dhūpa, and naivedya
• Resume physical offerings as soon as possible

But as a principle, remember this lovingly:

If the means are available, always go with the physical offering.
Because Bhairava Sādhana is not just about feeling Him…
It is about showing up for Him with your body, your effort, and your discipline.

That physical vedi is not decoration it is your declaration.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: I am new to this group and very glad to have found it. For the past 5 months I have been practising Bhairava sādhana on my own, relying mostly on internet readings — often uncertain, but proceeding with devotion. I do nāma japa of 'Om Bhairavāye Namah', read the 108 names of Vatuka Bhairava, and chant the Kāla Bhairava Aṣṭakam daily (more on Kālāṣṭamī tithis). I have completed 100,000 japas of the nāma mantra. I would like a more structured, step-by-step roadmap for going deeper. Two questions: (1) What sādhana process and Charana stages would you recommend for someone like me? (2) What exactly does one Mandala include — what japa, chanting, and dhyāna are done in a 48-day Mandala
answer:
Namaskaram, 

Very good that you are asking before proceeding.

Please understand one thing very clearly the 144 days Mandala sadhana/ Purashcharana shared in Bhairava Anugraha is not just another chant or simple japa.

It is a paddhati, a sankalpa, and a sacred commitment with very specific steps, discipline, and inner alignment involved in it. So it should be followed with sincerity and reverence, not approached casually.

About your Shivling:
Since you are already doing regular nitya abhishek, Kaal Bhairava Ashtakam, and Panchopachara with shraddha, continue it as it is for now.

At the same time, we already have clear details on Bhairava Linga Prana Pratisthana, made in a way that is easy for everyone to follow step by step. But again, this is not something to be done casually or treated like an optional add-on.

Prana Pratisthana, is Prana, a Jiva sakti process!
Once done properly in the right steps, the Linga begins to hold and emit its own frequency / spanda / presence. So such a process must be entered with respect, clarity, and readiness not as “something that can be tried.”

About Panchopachara and japa:
Yes, you can absolutely do physical Panchopachara first and then sit a little away for japa/sadhana. & yes, you may also do manasik offering during japa after the physical offering.

Just remember:

When physical offering is possible, always do that first.
Manasik offering should deepen the connection, not replace the physical out of convenience.

About your 2–3 years of Bhairava Naam Japa:
That is beautiful and definitely Baba’s grace. But still, complete the 144-day mandala first.

Because your earlier japa is your foundation, but this 144-day Mandala sadhana process is a structured doorway not just more chanting.

Do not rush ahead.
Let the paddhati itself prepare the sadhaka.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: I hope you are well, Guruji last year I completed my first mandala sadhana and after sometime I got a job because of which I was very busy so I could not do second and third mandala sadhana. This year I want to complete second mandala sadhana, Guruji please give your guidance what I should do? And there is one more thing due to my busy schedule there have been days where I have missed my nitya upasana and somewhere I feel my consistency and discipline is broken there is always guilt in my mind due to this
answer:
Namaskaram 

Very happy to know you completed your first mandala sadhana and also that Bhairava Baba blessed you with a job. That itself is His grace.

Do not hold guilt in your heart for what happened in between. Life responsibilities also come by His will. What matters now is that the inner calling has awakened again that itself is His pull.

For now, restart gently and sincerely.
First, bring back your nitya upasana with steadiness for a few days, and once your rhythm becomes stable, then begin your Second Mandala sadhana with fresh sankalpa and full commitment.

Do not think about missed days too much.
Bhairava does not look at your fall… He looks at whether you returned to Him.

This time, take only what you can sustain with your current schedule, but do it with niyama, shraddha, and consistency. Even with a busy life, if the bhava is true, He accepts every sincere offering.

Leave the guilt. Resume the path. Re-enter with surrender.
That itself is your correction.

Jai Bhairava

question: Sir I checked and read the entire document for mandala sadhana I didn't find the recommended count in it but in astami sadhana I found 11 mala so I humbly request you to clarify the preferred count should I start with 1 mala or 11 or in between whatever I am capable
answer:
Namaskaram 

For Mandala sadhana, 11 malas daily is the traditional Purva Charana Sankalpa for the mandala.

But this is purely based on your time and availability for Him. If 11 malas is possible comfortably, take that sankalpa. If not, take only what you can maintain sincerely without break for the full mandala.

Better a smaller count with full niyama than a bigger count broken midway.


Jai Bhairava

question: Guruji, I need your guidance. Yesterday I took a 41-day saṅkalpa of the Kāla Bhairava Aṣṭakam. Today our street dog, who has been with our family for 10 years, had a serious accident — one of her eyes came out. Is there any connection with my sādhana? She is very special to me; my family and I have cared for her for 10 years. Please guide me
answer:
Namaskaram,

Please do not worry or let your mind become disturbed. First of all, stay calm. What happened to the dog is very painful to see, especially when there is such a deep bond and years of care, so naturally your heart is shaken.

But please do not immediately connect this incident with your Kal Bhairava Ashtakam sankalpa or sadhana. In many cases, such events happen due to prarabdha and the natural flow of karma, and not necessarily as a sign that something is wrong in your sadhana.

Sometimes, when we begin a sacred sankalpa, the mind becomes highly alert and starts relating every external event to the sadhana. But what is most important is to remain steady, prayerful, and inwardly surrendered.

At this moment, your first dharma is to take full care of the dog with love, treatment, and compassion, because seva to such a being is also very dear to Bhairava. Do not stop your sankalpa out of fear. Instead, continue with shraddha, calmness, and Bhairava smarana.

While doing the Ashtakam, pray from the heart:
“Bhairava, please protect this soul, reduce its suffering, and give strength to all of us.”

If your heart still feels heavy, we can connect once and I will explain what to observe during these 41 days, so that your mind becomes clear and stable.

Be steady. Do not fear. Continue with faith.

Jai Bhairava

question:
If we wear Rudraksha mala around neck while going japa does it get energised the same way as the japa mala?
Is it helpful to wear rudraksha mala during japa? ( Wearing it only during japa, taking it off after japa)
answer:
Namaskaram,

Yes, wearing a Rudraksha mala during japa is definitely helpful. It supports the mind in becoming more centered and inward during the practice.

If you wear it around the neck while doing japa, it does receive the spanda and subtle energy of the mantra, but it is not considered exactly the same as the japa mala that is directly used for counting and mantra sankhya. The japa mala gets energized in a more direct and deeper way through continuous mantra contact.

So yes, you may wear it during japa and remove it afterwards if you wish that is fine. It is beneficial, but keep the dedicated japa mala separate and treat it with its own sanctity.

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
I have a quick question. I was about to head to sleep when I saw a spontaneous image of Baba Bhairava. It wasn't anything major, just a spontaneous sort of image held externally to me, but I did want to know if that's considered a light form of darshan or just a sign that my practice is deepening and I'm starting to uncover and awaken this form of consciousness.
I'm also aware this could be Hypnogogic activity, but it's also happened with other dieties in the past
answer:
Namaskaram,

Yes, such experiences can happen when the connection with the deity is deepening. It may be a subtle indication that your inner awareness is becoming more receptive to that form of consciousness.

Whether we call it a light darshan, a glimpse, or a sign of inner awakening, there is no need to over-analyze it too much. The important thing is to receive it with gratitude, stay humble, and continue the sadhana steadily.

Sometimes these moments can arise in subtle states like sleep-wake transition also, but even then, for a sadhaka, such states can still become openings through which the deity’s presence is felt.

So simply take it as Bhairava’s grace and a gentle encouragement to go deeper, without attachment or expectation.

Continue your practice calmly

question:
Guruji.. Please provide details regarding mandala sadhana of bhairav baba and can unmarried girls do this sadhana??
And which swaroop of baba to be worshipped during this mandala.. Since last year I am doing on bhairavaya namah nam jap.. And doing kal bhairav ashtakam these days.. Please guide
answer:
Namskaram, 

Since you are already doing “Om Bhairavaya Namah” japa and Kalabhairava Ashtakam, Baba’s grace is already guiding you.

For Mandala sadhana, please first follow the webpage where the full sadhana structure has been shared that is called Purvacharana.

Purvacharana comes even before Prathamacharana, and it is what has been asked for all Bhairavanugraha sadhakas as the true foundation.

Yes, unmarried girls can certainly do Bhairava sadhana 
Baba sees shraddha, bhava, discipline, and purity, not outer status.

First follow Purvacharana sincerely.
Then, step by step, the right next guidance will naturally unfold through Bhairava Baba’s grace.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: During last night's japa my awareness spontaneously moved to a spot in the room outside my body for a short time. I did not think much of it and completed my japa. Right after, I felt drawn to check your YouTube channel — and the very first community post shown to me, from 8 months ago, was on this exact dhārana on VBT: dhārana on people, animals, things outside oneself. I never searched this term, so I knew it was not the algorithm. Is this a sign from Bhairava that I should practise this dhārana more during japa? I usually focus on the mantra, on Bhairava's form, or on awareness within my body — should I also try focusing on awareness outside the body? Would this help or distract my sādhana
answer:
Namskaram Maa, 

What you experienced is beautiful, but please approach it with calmness and no excitement.

In sincere japa, awareness can sometimes spontaneously shift beyond the body or into subtle space. This is not something to fear, but also not something to immediately chase.

And yes, the fact that right after this, you were shown the exact dharana post is definitely a very meaningful synchronicity. You may take it as a gentle indication from Bhairava but not necessarily as an instruction to force that experience during japa.

For now, please keep your main anchors the same:

- Mantra-bhava
- Bhairava swaroopa
- Awareness within the body

If awareness naturally expands outside the body during japa, simply notice it without fear, without clinging, and let the japa continue.

But do not deliberately try to force outer-body awareness as a technique yet, unless that dharana has been specifically taken up in the right way.

In Bhairava sadhana, stability is more important than unusual experiences.
So receive it as a blessing… but stay grounded.

If this current is truly meant for you now, Bhairava will make it clearer again, naturally and with steadiness.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Today I just came across a podcast of Ranveer allahbadia and Shishir Kumar talking about Kashi and Kalbhairav. While talking abt bhairava sadhana at home he mentioned that we cannot keep a photo or sketch of bhairav baba at home. Only mandir photo is allowed. But as I don't have any photo, I am keeping a sketch of baba for my sadhana which my daughter has drawn. Is this wrong? Am I doing any mistake? Need your guidance on it Guruji
answer:
Namaskaram,

Please be at peace, you are not doing anything wrong.

In Bhairava sadhana, many things are said according to different lineages, temple traditions, or personal paddhatis, so not every statement heard in a podcast becomes a universal rule for every sadhaka.

If you are keeping a respectful sketch of Bhairava Baba, lovingly drawn by your daughter, and using it only for sadhana with shraddha and maryada, then this is not a mistake.

What matters most is:

bhava
reverence
cleanliness of the space
and the paddhati you are following

A sacred image kept for upasana is very different from a casual decorative image.

So please do not allow fear to enter the heart.
Bhairava does not reject sincere devotion because it came through a lovingly offered sketch.

Just keep it respectfully, in a clean place, and continue your sadhana with trust.
If later a clearer instruction comes through your own paddhati or Baba’s grace brings a proper image, you may shift naturally.

Do not let outside opinions create fear where Baba has already created connection.

Also, let me tell Once the Purvacharana is completed in the sadhana Paddhati, then in the Prathama Charana, a Yantra Image is formally provided to the sadhaka to perform the Puja Paddhati. This is part of the Sampradaya maryada.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Is Nagna Sādhana, as sometimes described within Bhairava Paddhati, something a sādhaka can take up?
answer:
Namaskaram,

I believe most of you need to hear this very carefully... I’m sorry, but this message is a little more deep and detailed, so I would request you to please read it slowly and with patience.

We recently received a message related to "Nagna sadhana" within the context of Bhairava Paddhati. And I want to make this absolutely clear to every sadhaka here.

Do not jump into anything that sounds ugra, tamasika, or sensational just because it feels powerful, mysterious, or exciting when heard in a podcast, article, video, or document.

Many things sound intense.
Many things sound secretive.
Many things sound spiritually high level.

But hearing about something and being adhikari for it are two very different things.

If such acts are performed without proper awareness, without initiation, without sampradaya maryada, and without direct guidance, then understand this very clearly.

It will not only affect the sadhaka, it can disturb the entire energy of the home and the people living in that space.

Do not take this lightly.

You are not playing with symbolism.
You are invoking a Daiva connected to deeper and often tamasika currents.
And when you invoke such currents without understanding, without containment, and without protection, you are not doing sadhana.
You are opening doors you do not know how to close.

This is exactly why, in Bhairava Anugraha, we are taking time to explain each swaroopa of Bhairava in detail....

Before you try to experience Bhairava,
before you try to imitate a ritual,
before you try to perform something that sounds occult or intense,

you must first understand what you are invoking.

Without that understanding, it is not Bhairava sadhana.
It becomes recklessness in the name of spirituality.

And let me say this very directly.

I have never asked any of you to do dead malas.

Rolling mala after mala after mala without awareness,
completing 100 malas like a mechanical act,
counting numbers and feeling proud,

none of that is useful.
Not for you.
Not for your inner growth.
Not for your Bhairava sadhana.

Here, bhava is the key.

I have said this again and again.

Few (Sankalpa) mala done with full awareness, surrender, and Bhairava bhava is far more powerful than hundreds of dead malas done like a machine.

Similarly, any ritualistic or ugra form of sadhana must be performed only:

under the guidance of Guru..... Within the maryada of the Sampradaya,
according to the specific paddhati being followed,
and only when the sadhaka is truly prepared and permitted.

This is not about curiosity.
This is not about spiritual thrill.
This is not about trying something because it sounded powerful in a podcast.

This is about the paddhati.
This is about adhikara.
This is about consequences.

Please understand this deeply.

Aghora Bhairava sadhana Paddhati cannot be casually shared online.

It is a completely different stream.
A different process.
A different procedure.
A different level of containment.
A different form of Bhairava engagement altogether.

It is not something to be copied.
It is not something to be experimented with.
It is not something to be imagined and attempted at home because it feels powerful.

So I am rising the flag to all of you very clearly.

Do not attempt such practices.

Do not fantasize about them.
Do not imitate them.
Do not assume that what is hidden is automatically meant for you.

Because when such sadhanas are performed improperly, subtle energies do gather.
And once they begin to gather, your nervous system, your sleep, your emotional state, your home atmosphere, and even the people around you can start feeling the impact.

You may call it excitement in the beginning.
But later, you may not have the stability to handle what you have invited.

If you perform what you are not prepared for, then be ready to face the consequences.

And do not say later that nobody warned or guided you.

This path is not for imitation.
This path is not for performance.
This path is not for thrill-seeking.

This path is for disciplined sadhakas who walk under guidance, in sequence, with bhava, humility, and sampradaya maryada.

So once again,

Do not attempt Nagna sadhana on your own.
Do not jump into ugra or tamasika rituals because they sound occult.
Do not confuse secrecy with readiness.
Do not mistake intensity for eligibility.
Do not bring unknown energies into your home without proper containment.

Stay with the paddhati that has been given.
Stay with the bhava that has been taught.
Stay with the maryada of the sampradaya.

That alone will protect you.
That alone will deepen you.
That alone will make Bhairava sadhana fruitful.

Do not create problems for yourself in the name of sadhana.

~ Jai Bhairava.

question: Why is my sādhana not giving fruitful results? And why has my Swarna Akarshana Bhairava Sādhana not changed my financial situation at all?
answer:
Namaskaram

Today, I have received two questions from one of our Sadhakas.
The first question is, why is my sadhana not giving fruitful results?

The second question is, I have been doing Swarna Akarshana Bhairava sadhana but it is not working for me. There is no change in my financial situation at all.

Let us understand both of these questions in detail and depth. 

Because I know these two questions are not just from one person. Many of you sitting here are carrying the same doubt silently.
So let me speak to you from the heart.

First thing I want you to understand is this.
Bhairava does not fail. The vessel fails.
Before the mantra, before the yantra, before any ritual, there is you. And if you are sitting in front of Bhairava with fear and desperation inside your chest, saying please give me something, I am struggling, I am suffering, then you are not doing sadhana. You are begging. And Bhairava does not respond to begging. He responds to readiness.

The divine does not listen to the words coming from your mouth. It responds to the feeling vibrating inside you. If your words say give me wealth but your inner state is full of fear and lack, then what reaches Bhairava is not your mantra. It is your fear. And existence sends back to you exactly what you are carrying inside.
This is not a punishment. This is simply how the Tattva works. It is a pure mirror.

Now let me talk about something that goes even deeper.
Between the ages of seven and twelve, most of us absorbed beliefs from our family and surroundings without any choice.
We heard things like money does not come easily. Rich people are not good people. It is not in our family’s fate. We are not meant for that kind of life.
These words, heard again and again as children, became part of our subconscious mind. And now, years later, you are sitting in front of Swarnakarshana Bhairava doing your japa sincerely, but your own subconscious is doing counter-sadhana with every single thought.
Think about this carefully. If somewhere deep inside you believe that wealthy people are corrupt or dishonest, then your own mind will keep you away from money just to protect your image of yourself as a good person. This is the most invisible block. This is the most dangerous block. And most Sadhakas never even recognise it in themselves.

Now let me tell you what Bhairava Tattva actually teaches about wealth.
Swarnakarshana Bhairava is not sitting in the heavens waiting to pour gold on whoever chants enough rounds of japa. His Tattva teaches something far deeper than that.
Wealth is a flow. It returns to you in proportion to the value and service you are offering to the world around you.
If you are sitting and waiting for Bhairava to bring money to your door while you are taking no action in your life, then you have misunderstood the sadhana completely. The mantra aligns your energy. It opens the inner channels. It removes karmic blocks. But you must move. You must act. A river needs a slope to flow. You have to create that slope in your own life.

Ask yourself honestly. What problem am I solving for the people around me? What real value am I bringing? Wealth is always an exchange. The bigger the problem you solve, the more wealth flows back to you. This is not modern thinking. This is the Tattva itself speaking.

Now let me also be honest about the sadhana level.
Many Sadhakas do not receive results because the sadhana is incomplete at its very root.
The mantra of Bhairava is not just sounds you repeat. Every seed syllable inside that mantra has an associated devata that needs to be properly awakened and satisfied. Without this activation, the mantra remains a dry shell. The energy inside it never fully releases.
Beyond this, without proper Guru Diksha, without a living transmission through a genuine lineage, the mantra carries no Shakti. You can chant for years and feel nothing. Because you are trying to light a fire without a spark.

And one more thing I want you to hold in your heart.

When Bhairava appears to not be responding, sometimes it is because He is actually working on you. He burns what is impure before He fills what is empty. The delay you are experiencing may not be rejection. It may be a transformation happening at a level your eyes cannot yet see.

So what must you do now?

Stop approaching Bhairava from fear and desperation. Sit before Him and say with full sincerity, I am ready. Make me worthy. Align me. Prepare me for what I am asking.

Take a piece of paper and write down every belief you carry about money. Every thought that says it is not possible for me, I never have enough, money always escapes me. Write them down and consciously replace each one with a stronger and truer belief. Repeat these new beliefs in the morning when you wake and at night before you sleep. The subconscious learns only through repetition.

Stop using the language of lack. Stop saying I have no money, I have no time, nothing works for me. Instead say I am finding new ways, I am opening new channels. This shifts your mind from searching for excuses to searching for solutions.
Spend five minutes every morning in genuine gratitude. 

Not as a ritual. Actually feel it in your body. This single practice slowly removes the fear that is gripping your nervous system and opens your mind to possibilities.

And on the sadhana side, check your practice honestly. Verify your mantra, your protocol, your timing. If you have not received proper Diksha, seek it. Do not keep building on a foundation that was never properly laid.
Hold this mantra close to you.
Om Bhairavaya Namaha
Chant it with surrender. Not with demand. Not with desperation. With the quiet trust of someone who knows they are already held by the highest.

Let me close with what matters most.
Bhairava is not simply a deity you call upon for money. He is Maha Bhairava. He is the Supreme Light appearing in golden form. In His Tattva, true poverty is not the absence of money. True poverty is the absence of divine grace. And true wealth is not what sits in your bank account. True wealth is becoming a person through whom the divine can flow freely and fully.
You are not cursed. You are not unlucky. You are not being ignored by Bhairava.
You are being prepared.
Stop begging at the door of Bhairava. Become worthy of walking through it.
The wealth will come. But first, the one who is asking must transform.

~ Jai Bhairava​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

question:
I have been doing nama mantra for the past 1 year starting with Batuka Bhairava Brahma Kavacham once. I see somehow I am always drained out all the day even during japa. Now I want to increase my daily count of japa.
So could you please let me know how to recite kavacham multiple times to facilitate my self to the increase in my japa and ensure I am not vulnerable. I mean generally my pattern is I recite mula mantra 11 or 21 times and then start reading kavacham and then end it by reciting mula mantra 11 or 21 times as per the kavacham.
So if I want to recite multiple times do I need to recite the mula mantra 11 or 21 times for every time before and after kavacham. Also the actual kavacham starts in the second page from 10th stanza to 21 stanza. Please guide me on this as well if reading only these stanzas multiple times is enough and rest of all once
answer:
Namaskaram 

If you are already feeling drained, please do not put pressure on yourself to increase the count right now. 

In Bhairava sadhana, what matters first is sthirata, not numbers….. For repeating Batuka Bhairava Brahma Kavacham multiple times, you can simply do mula mantra 11 or 21 times once at the beginning, then repeat the kavacham as needed, and again once at the end no need to do it before and after every repetition. And yes, the main protective portion (10th to 21st stanza) can be repeated multiple times, while the rest may be done once. 

But understand this deeply Guru Bhairava is not sitting with a tracker or checklist of how many times you completed something. 

He is looking at how much surrender, steadiness, and true bhava you are placing at His feet. Sometimes one sincere japa offered with fullness reaches Him more than many done with strain. So first become steady… then let the count grow naturally.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: I have a narmadeshwar shivlinga so can I do the prana pratistha process on it
answer:
Namaskaram 

Yes, you can proceed and for this, please follow the document shared on the webpage properly. Go through each statement and every step with attention and connection, not just as a formality. If you stay connected to what is being done at every stage, the entire process will become easier, more aligned, and your with the Bhairava lingam will deepen naturally.

Do not rush it mechanically. Read, understand, connect, and then perform. That itself will make the sthapana much smoother and more powerful in bhava.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Matsyendranath, Dattatreya Swami, Bhairava, and Shiva — are they all one and the same tatva?
answer:
Namaskaram 

At the level of Tatva, yes these forms may appear different, but the source is One.

Matsyendranath, Dattatreya Swami, Bhairava, and Shiva are understood in many paramparas as different expressions of the same Adi Consciousness, revealed through different paths and anusthana.

Also, we have a dedicated YouTube video where Adi Bhairava and Guru Matsyendranath are explained clearly in detail.

Please do go through that video once it will help you understand their deeper importance much better.

Jai Bhairava

question:
What does true surrender mean in Bhairava sadhana? When things seem to go wrong, we naturally push harder and try to fix everything, is that against surrender?
If tamasic inertia is one extreme and rajasic over-effort is the other, where does surrender sit?
How does one know they are surrendering or simply giving up?
answer:
Namaskaram

Excellent one!

In Bhairava sadhana, surrender is not stopping effort…
it is stopping the ego that claims the effort.

Tamasic mind withdraws.
Rajasic mind controls.
The surrendered one acts… but does not interfere with Bhairava’s Will.

Giving up says: “Nothing is possible.”
Surrender says: “Everything is His.”

The highest surrender is not:
“Bhairava, fulfill my path.”
It is:
“Bhairava, dissolve the one who still stands apart from You.”

That… is where sadhana becomes Bhairava.

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
Guruji, a follow up question to that if I may
When a desire to act arises, how do we know if it is Bhairava’s will, our ego wanting something, or simply our own nature expressing itself?
And when a difficult situation comes, how do we discern whether to accept it as Bhairava’s will or to act on it to change it
answer:
Namaskaram 

A very good question.. But, if you’re fine let me explain little detailed..

This is exactly where sadhana begins to mature 

when one stops asking only “What should I do?”
and starts asking,
“From where within me is this action arising?”

The truth is in the beginning, it is not always easy to clearly distinguish whether something is Bhairava’s sankalpa, ego’s movement, or simply one’s svabhava expressing itself.

That clarity does not come first.
Purification comes first.
And from purification, discernment is born.

A simple way to understand it:

If the impulse is arising with restlessness, urgency, fear, personal insistence, or inner agitation, it is often mixed with ego or attachment.

If the impulse arises with clarity, steadiness, humility, and an inner quietness, even if the action itself is strong, then it is closer to Bhairava’s alignment.

Bhairava’s will does not always feel soft but it always carries inner stillness.
Ego may look powerful but inside it always carries contraction.

And regarding difficult situations…

Not every situation is meant to be passively accepted, and not every situation is meant to be forcefully changed.

The deeper question is not:
“Should I accept or act?”

The deeper question is:
“Can I first become inwardly still enough to see without fear, anger, or personal compulsion?”

Because once the mind becomes quiet in sadhana, the response becomes clearer.

Sometimes Bhairava’s will is to make you bow.
Sometimes Bhairava’s will is to make you stand.
Sometimes He teaches through acceptance.
Sometimes He teaches through action.

So the key is this:

First surrender. Then see. Then act or do not act.

Sit before Him.
Offer the desire, the confusion, and the situation at His feet.

Pray simply:

“If this is arising from ego, dissolve it.
If this is arising from dharma, strengthen it.
If this is Your will, make it clear within me.”

Then watch what remains after the inner noise settles.

What survives after surrender is usually much closer to Truth
than what shouts loudly before surrender.

In short:

Ego pushes
Fear reacts
Nature expresses
Bhairava aligns

And the one who stays in sadhana, Shraddha, and surrender
slowly learns the difference not by theory…
but by inner ripening.

Do not be afraid if you cannot discern perfectly right away.
Even this sincere questioning is itself His grace.

The more you remain at His feet,
the more your inner instrument becomes pure…
and then what is His will no longer needs to be guessed —
it begins to be felt.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: ಗುರುಗಳೆ, I was also inspired by Rajashri Nandi about Bhairava nama japa, and started doing 10 malas every day with full interest for more than one year, the first day when I started without control I have cried for about 20minutes for no reason I found so difficult to control myself, I don't know why, but now recently maybe a month ago I am slowly loosing interest, now I am doing only one maala but I can't stop it, I love Bhairava Baba, but still why is this happening? Can you please help me, along with this I am doing Maa Lalitha Sahasranama parayana, Thrishathi, Khadgamala n Durga saptashati beeja mantratmaka saadana, nowadays all these I am doing mechanically, but I can't stop, it will force me to do, but why is happening like this, please help me
answer:
Namaskaram, 

What you are experiencing is not wrong, and it is actually a very natural phase in sadhana.

In the beginning, when Bhairava Nama first touched your heart, the tears came because something deep inside you was being opened. That was grace, purification, and inner connection awakening.

But after some time, sadhana does not always remain emotional or sweet. Sometimes Bhairava takes the rasa away and makes it feel dry, mechanical, or heavy not because He has gone away, but because He is taking you from emotion to steadiness.

Earlier you were doing with bhava.
Now He is teaching you to do with nishtha.

This dryness is also part of His krupa.
Because true sadhana is not tested when the heart is overflowing it is tested when the heart feels empty, yet the japa continues.

The fact that you cannot stop, even when interest is low, itself shows that Bhairava has already caught hold of you.
That inner force making you continue is also His presence.

Do not be disturbed.
Do not judge yourself.
Do not force too much.

For some time:

Reduce the pressure on yourself
Do fewer malas, but do with softness and sincerity
Sit for a minute before japa and simply pray:

“Bhairava Baba, today I have no bhava, no strength only Your name. Please accept even this.”

Even those one mala with complete awareness, surrender is greater than many done with strain.

This phase will pass.
Sometimes Bhairava hides His sweetness so that the devotee learns to love Him beyond feeling, beyond excitement, beyond experience.

You are not moving away from Him. He is deepening your sadhana.

Stay gentle. Stay regular. Stay surrendered.

His grace is very much there.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: I often feel drowsy and doze off while sitting for sādhana. Why does this happen, and are Nyāsa and Viniyoga required every single time we sit?
answer:
Namaskaram,

I thought of sharing something important here with all of you, as I recently received a DM from one of our sadhikas regarding a very common experience during sadhana feeling drowsy or dozing off while sitting for practice.

I felt this is not just for one person, but something that may help many in the group, so let me share this clearly for everyone.

When it comes to Mandala sadhana, there are certain essential steps already shared in the group especially Nyasa and Vinyoga. These are not just formalities. They are extremely important because they help in activating the chakras, awakening the sukshma sharira (subtle body), and making oneself receptive to the energies that begin to guide us deeper into the journey of sadhana.

Now, if you ask in a very simple way is Nyasa and Vinyoga required every single time we sit for daily sadhana? 
Traditionally, it may be done once in the morning or once in the evening.

But personally, I would strongly recommend that during Mandala sadhana, if possible, you perform it during all the Nishchita Kalas / Sandhya Kalas whenever you sit. 
This will be extremely helpful in keeping your awareness active and your inner system aligned.

Also, one important reminder:

Mandala sadhana is not merely about finding one free time in the day and somehow finishing it. 
If time, situation, and your practical circumstances allow, then try to sit during the Sandhya Kalas / Nishchita Kalas as much as possible. These timings hold tremendous spiritual support.

And one more thing I want to remind everyone with simple and firmness:

This path is not about mechanically rolling the malas. 
Yes, I have said the count may be 11 or 22 malas, depending on the Mandala and the sadhana paddhati. But simply completing numbers without awareness will never take us deeper into true connection with Bhairava.

Bhairava responds to conscious presence, not mechanical repetition.

Always enter the sadhana with awareness, bhava, and inner participation.

In the Dwitya Charana, we have also shared the practice of Bhairava Trataka. This has been specifically taught so that every sadhaka can make their awareness sharper, more stable, and more inwardly alive during the sadhana paddhati.

So please, I request all of you:

- Follow the exact protocol shared in the sadhana Paddhati
- Do Nyasa & Vinyoga sincerely
- Sit during Sandhya / Nishchita Kalas as much as possible
- Do not make the practice mechanical
- Use Bhairava Trataka where instructed
- If drowsiness still comes, take short mindful breaks and resume again with awareness

Please continue exactly as shared in the paddhati documents. 

May Bhairava keep your awareness alive, your inner body receptive, and your sadhana deeply connected.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Guruji can nyasa and viniyoga of any devata and Devi be done without guru diksha
answer:
Namaskaram,

Good one

Nyasa and Viniyoga are not just words or ritual steps, they are sacred processes of invoking, aligning, and installing devata tattva within oneself.

That is why, in Bhairava Anugraha, we have enabled them not to replace Guru diksha, but to help sincere sadhakas understand the maryada, bhava, and inner discipline behind mantra sadhana.

Especially in start of Prathama Charana, the extended Nyasa and Viniyoga are kept in a diksha-oriented format very consciously not as full diksha, but as a sacred preparation that helps the sadhaka move from casual chanting to conscious invocation.

Diksha gives the current.
But before that, the vessel must be prepared.

That is exactly the purpose here: to make the sadhaka more aligned, more receptive, and one step closer to deserving Guru Bhairava Kripa and deeper Bhairava sadhana. 

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
What does “increasing intensity” or “going deeper” in sadhana mean in practical terms?
I assume a longer dhyaan duration and mala repetition are part of it but is it about tighter discipline and harder sankalpa conditions?
Humbly asking for tactical guidance on applying intensity correctly?
answer:
Namaskaram 

Very beautiful question.

Going deeper in sadhana does not only mean doing more it means becoming more available to Bhairava.

Yes, longer dhyaan, more japa, and stronger discipline are part of it. 

But true intensity means:

- More consistency, irrespective of outside circumstances 
- More awareness in every mantra
- Less distraction and energy leakage
- Cleaner food, speech, and conduct
- Stronger inner sankalpa
- Holding Bhairava bhava beyond the sadhana seat

So intensity is not force it is sacred sharpness.
Not just quantity, but purity, steadiness, and surrender.

When sadhana stops being something you do, and starts becoming the state you live in that is going deeper.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: A friend of mine recently received an online Bhairava diksha. During the mantra-chanting portion of the diksha video, the Guru's microphone dislodged and the audio was almost muted. The Guru did not realise and sent it anyway. When my friend raised this, the Guru declared it the will of the devatā, did not repeat the diksha, and asked him to begin the sādhana, which he has. He has accepted his Guru's word, but I can see he is worried and unsure — it is his first formal initiation. What would you advise him to do
answer:
Namaskaram

First of all, this is actually a very good sign.

The fact that your friend noticed what happened during the initiation shows sincerity, alertness, and genuine respect for the process. Not everyone pays attention with that level of involvement. So in that sense, it is a beautiful observation, and it shows he is taking the path seriously.

But now let me say this very clearly.

Once the Guru has been informed, has fully understood the situation, and has still clearly instructed him to begin the sadhana, then the matter is no longer in the disciple’s hands to keep analysing.

From that point onward, continuing to sit in doubt is not sincerity.

It becomes interference.

And if it continues, it slowly becomes disrespect towards Guru vakya.

Diksha is not a technical event.
Diksha is not dependent on audio quality.
Diksha is not dependent on outer perfection.
Diksha is not a transfer of words alone.

Diksha is Shakti.
Diksha is Sankalpa.
Diksha is transmission.

If the Guru has said begin, then begin.

If the Guru has said it is the will of the Devata, then that statement must be received with reverence, not mentally debated in private.

The disciple is not the authority over whether Diksha happened.
The Guru is.

This is where many seekers make a mistake in the beginning.
They think the mind must first feel satisfied before grace can be accepted.

That is not how this path works.

On the path of Bhairava or Tantra Marga, surrender comes before certainty.
Understanding comes after practice.
Real transmission often happens beyond the reach of the mind.

So tell your friend this very lovingly, but very firmly.

Do not insult Guru vakya by doubting what has already been sanctioned.

If the Guru has not repeated the Diksha and has still allowed the sadhana to begin, then either nothing was incomplete, or whatever appeared incomplete outwardly was never essential inwardly.

In both cases, the instruction remains the same.

Do the sadhana.
Do it properly.
Do it steadily.
Do it without mental drama.

The first test after Diksha is often not the mantra.
It is the mind.

Doubt enters.
Confusion enters.
Analysis enters.

And if the disciple keeps feeding that, he blocks his own receptivity.

If Bhairava wants to enter, He does not need a microphone.
If the Guru’s sankalpa has moved, no outer defect can stop it.

A disciple’s work is not to audit the Guru.
A disciple’s work is to obey, practice, and become available.

So let him stop replaying the incident.
Let him stop measuring the infinite with the mind.
Let him stop trying to verify grace through logic.

Let him sit.
Let him chant.
Let him surrender.

The proof of Diksha is not in how perfect the initiation looked from outside.
The proof of Diksha is in what begins to awaken within him through sincere sadhana.

Tell him to trust the process completely and move forward without hesitation.

Jai Bhairava

question: Does this mean we can do nyāsa of any Devi without diksha?
answer:
Namaskaram, 

Good observation.

Some simple nyasas can be done in bhava and preparation, if given properly. But not every Devi swaroopa nyasa should be done without diksha.

Deeper mantra-specific or tantrika nyasas need adhikara, guru-anumati, and often diksha because diksha is not just permission, it is shakti-transmission.

What is given here is only what is safe, aligned, and meant to take the sadhaka one step closer not to bypass the traditional process.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: I am new to Bhairava aradana. In March I went to Kashi and then I visited kalbhairav temple. From then I got hope in life. I am very much now attracted to Bhairava baba. Wanted to do sadana under a guru. I m from Bangalore. Can someone guide me a guru who can teach me bhairava sadana
answer:
Namaskaram 

Very beautiful… sometimes Baba calls in just one darshan, and life begins to change from that moment itself. If after Kashi and Kalabhairava darshan you are feeling this inner pull, it means his kripa has already started working.

Take your time and first go through the website properly, especially the Q&A section. Many important aspects are already shared there which every sadhaka should know and understand before stepping deeper into the path.

You can begin with Purva Charana itself. Please check the website for the full details and go through it with patience and bhava.

Https://sites. Google. Com/view/daiva-anughara/home

https://bhairavaanugraha. Com/

When the longing is genuine, Baba himself arranges the right guidance at the right time.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Guruji, why does it happen that in the starting of sadhana one can get some experiences but at later stage, it fades away.. Please enlighten
answer:
Namaskaram

This happens very naturally in sadhana.

In the beginning, sometimes Baba gives small experiences, feelings, or signs just to make the sadhak more receptive, more trusting, and more inwardly connected. It is like grace giving assurance that the path is alive.

But later, when the sadhana starts becoming deeper, those outer experiences may reduce or fade not because the grace is gone, but because now the sadhak is being taken from experience-seeking to inner stability.

In the beginning, experiences attract the mind.
Later, silence matures the soul.

Real sadhana is not in chasing sensations, visions, or feelings. It is in remaining steady even when nothing seems to happen. Many times, when nothing is felt outwardly, the deeper work is actually happening inwardly in a much stronger way.

So do not measure your sadhana by experiences.
Measure it by your sincerity, consistency, inner grounding, and how much you are becoming more aligned.

Bhairava first attracts… then He tests… then He transforms.
Remain steady. That itself is the real sign of grace

Keep doing the sadhana without expectation.
When Bhairava sees that you want Him more than the experience of Him, then the real doorway begins to open. 

~ Jai Bhairava

question: I did not have any spiritual experience since I started sadhana of nama japa since around 3 years back. So this isn't matching with me Guruji. And also when I met astrologer he told me that my ishta is vishnu/vitthal, but want to walk in baba's path forward, I am not doubting bhairava baba but why does this path not show in my janma patrika. Also Guruji can we take the 21 days sadhana mantra to ask for one experience for someone who hasn't had it yet just for the experience of it
answer:
Namaskaram

Do not be disheartened. 

Not receiving experiences does not mean Baba is absent. Many times, His deepest work happens in silence first … He prepares, purifies, and strengthens the sadhak before giving outer signs.

Janma patrika or Jatakam may show certain inclinations, but let me remind Our Guru Bhairava’s kripa is beyond charts. If your heart is being pulled to Bhairava Baba, that itself is His calling. Do not doubt that..

And yes, you may take the 21-day sadhana with a humble prayer at His lotus feet not to force an experience, but simply to say, “Baba, if it is Your wish, bless me with Your presence in any form.” He will show up…

Walk with patience, love, and steadiness. Sometimes the one who waits the longest receives the deepest touch.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Gurudev, I used to pray to Bābā to come to me in dreams and give me guidance. For some time it worked, but later I was unable to remember anything that appeared in my dreams. I used to ask Bhairava Bābā for direction when I did not know what was right or what choice to make. What can I do now
answer:
Namaskaram 

What you experienced is svabhavika (natural but subtle- sukshma). In the beginning, when the mind is fresh and eager, sometimes Baba may guide through dreams or subtle feelings. But over time, as the sadhana deepens, that outer way of communication may reduce.

It is not that Baba has stopped guiding you… it is that He is slowly turning you inward.

Dreams are not always the most reliable path. The real guidance begins to arise in your awareness, your clarity, your inner feeling of right and wrong. Baba does not want you to become dependent on signs, He wants to make you stable and aware within.

From now on, instead of asking for answers in dreams, sit quietly before Him, offer your confusion, and then move forward with sincerity. Even if you feel unsure, walk with trust…He is still guiding you, just in a subtler way.

Do your sadhana with love, without anxiety. Slowly, your inner clarity will become your true guidance.

Baba is not silent… He is just speaking in a deeper language now.

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
The Ashtottara Shatanamavali, there are many names describing different aspects of Bhairava. Then there are the Ashta Bhairava with the directional guarding of the field. What is the primary purpose of this multiplicity?
Are the names different so a Sadhak may invoke aspects of the tattva?
Or are they primarily bhakti-based descriptive expressions to show devotion by chanting?
Maybe the latter eventually leads to the former?
Thank you for your guidance
answer:
Namaskaram 

Your understanding is already moving in the right direction. 

Both what you have shared are true but they unfold in stages.

The multiplicity of names in the Ashtottara Shatanamavali and the presence of the Ashta Bhairava are not merely poetic or devotional expansions. 

They are a map of the tattva itself.

In the beginning, for a Sadhaka, these names feel like bhakti-based expressions, a way to connect, to soften, to surrender. Through repetition, the mind becomes aligned, and devotion deepens.

But gradually, something shifts.

Each name is not just a description it is a specific doorway into a vibration of Bhairava tattva. When invoked with awareness, the sadhak is not just praising… but touching that aspect within and around oneself.

Similarly, the Ashta Bhairavas are not separate deities in a fragmented sense. They represent the complete protection and structuring of consciousness across all directions inner and outer. It is about stabilizing the field of the sadhak.

So yes…
what begins as bhakti naturally matures into tattva-anubhava.

Not by force, but by grace and consistency.

You can hold it like this:

At first, you chant to connect
Then, you chant to align
Finally, you chant and become aware of what is already present

There is no contradiction between devotion and realization one gently leads into the other.

Continue your upasana with this openness. No need to over-analyze during practice… understanding will reveal itself in experience.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Swamy upasana, aradhana, anustana, sadhana is their any difference are these different forms of paddhati is this understanding right if so kindly help us to understand
answer:
Namaskaram 

Your understanding is very beautiful, and the question itself shows a sincere seeking. But, We have already shared this in the Q&A section and also spoken about it in detail in the sadhana video. Still, let me briefly share the essence here again for clarity.

Yes, these words: Upasana, Aradhana, Anusthana, and sadhana are connected, but they indicate different depths and stages of approaching the Divine.

Upasana means “sitting near.” It is about drawing yourself closer to the Devata through remembrance, japa, or simple presence. It is more internal and relationship-oriented.

Aradhana is worship with devotion. It includes offerings, puja, and expressions of bhakti. Here, the heart expresses reverence more actively.

Anusthana refers to a disciplined and structured practice followed with specific rules like a fixed count, time, or method. It brings consistency and sankalpa into the path.

Sadhana is the larger journey itself. It includes all of the above and goes deeper, it is the process of inner transformation, where the sadhaka is being shaped by the Divine.

So yes, you are right, they can be seen as different paddhatis or stages, but more than that, they are layers of deepening connection.

In simple words:

Upasana brings you closer,
Aradhana expresses your devotion,
Anusthana disciplines your path,
sadhana transforms your being.

Over time, all of them naturally merge into one flow….

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
I have questions
Kaartikeya Swami is called Chand Bhairava in Tantra
1)Why is it that Worship of God of war is limited to Southern geographical area??
2) Skanda upasana is advisable as seperate or the Bhairava sadhana is the same to it??
3) Why Sri Ganesh is revered every where and not skanda?
4) Both are tattva of same Shiv Shakti then why are they so different?
5) Is Shri Ganesh Related to tantra and Bhairava? How?
One more additional question
Are Skanda and Khandoba Related
Similarities
1) Both live on a hill
2) Both have two wives
3). Shashti is important for both deoties
4) Both are Bhairava
Please explain Guruji
answer:
Namaskaram

Your questions are very beautiful because they are not about devatas, but about understanding the one tattva behind all forms.

If we see through a Bhairava drishti, then everything begins to simplify... But as its multiple layer of topcis and connections, let me share on very high level. 

In Kashi Khanda, Kashi is not just a place it is a state of consciousness where Bhairava alone operates as the kshetrapalaka. All devatas there are functioning within His field. So whether it is Ganapati, Skanda, or any form, they are not separate identities, but expressions of Bhairava tattva in different kriya.

On Kartikeya being Chand Bhairava

In Tantra, Kartikeya is not merely “God of war.”
He is the sharp, piercing kriya-shakti of Bhairava.

That is why in the Bhairava parampara, as we already explained in the 64 Bhairava swaroopa:

Sikhivahana Bhairava is directly linked to Skanda (peacock vahana)
This current flows into Chanda Bhairava (intense, aggressive transformation)
And also touches Unmatta Bhairava (beyond logic, beyond structure)
In some traditions this is also seen as Martanda Bhairava expression

So calling Kartikeya as Chand Bhairava is not different, it is identifying his position within the Bhairava krama.

Why is Skanda worship seen more in the South?
From Bhairava perspective, energy expresses where it is held strongly.

In the South, the Veera–Bhairava current (as Skanda) was preserved in a pure parampara form.
In the North, the same energy is absorbed more directly into Bhairava and Kshetrapala upasana.

So it is not absence it is absorption into a more mula (root) Bhairava form.

Skanda upasana vs Bhairava sadhana
From Bhairava drishti: There is no separation.

Skanda = Bhairava in directed, focused kriya (like a spear)
Bhairava = Totality, including that kriya and beyond it

If someone does Skanda upasana deeply, it will naturally open into Bhairava.
If someone is in Bhairava sadhana, Skanda tattva arises as an inner shakthi of action and piercing clarity.

Why Ganapati is everywhere?
Ganapati is mula and he is the begining of all, wherein in Bhairava krama:

Ganapati = threshold guardian of manifestation (Muladhara stability)
Bhairava = ultimate dissolution beyond all thresholds
Without Ganapati, Bhairava cannot be approached.
Without Bhairava, Ganapati remains only at the level of stability.

So Ganapati is universal because He is the entry gate of Bhairava mandala, so we at Bhairva Anughara during Dwitiya Charana we have dedicated upasana of Ucchishta Ganapati sadhana.

Why do they appear different if same tattva?
Let me explan as its on simple level, as difference is only in spanda (vibration mode) of each devata and its anughara on sadaka and sadhana. 

Ganapati → Grounding spanda
Skanda → Penetrating spanda
Bhairava → Dissolving spanda
Same consciousness, different movement.

Ganapati and Tantra–Bhairava connection
Ganapati is not separate from Tantra, He is the first awakening of Kundalini within Bhairava krama.

Skanda and Khandoba ( Also As Khandoba Bhairava - we have shared more details in Marthanda bhairava Video on this swaroopa) are not exactly “the same deity,” but they are deeply connected expressions of the same Bhairava current, manifesting through different regions and paramparas.

Both live on a hill
Hill (giri ksetra) always represents ucchavastha, elevated consciousness and tapas.
Both Skanda and Khandoba are not laukika devatas, but tapasvi–yoddha forms, so their sthana reflects that.

Both have two wives
This is not just a story element, it represents dual Shakti:

Iccha Shakti (will)
Kriya Shakti (action)
Both deities operate strongly in dynamic Shakti, so this symbolism appears.

Shashti is important
Shashti is directly connected to Skanda tattva for Kandoba its similar to Bhairava guna its Astami 
Wherever this current flows, even regionally it carries that same energetic marker.

Both are Bhairava
This is the most important point.

Khandoba is traditionally understood as Malhari Martand, which is linked to Martanda Bhairava.
Skanda in Tantra is seen through Sikhivahana Bhairava, flowing into Chanda and Unmatta Bhairava swaroopa.

So rather than saying “both are Bhairava” as a label, it is more accurate to say: both are expressions of Bhairava in a Veera (warrior) mode.

So let me sumup with this, Here Skanda → Bhairava as pure, sharp jnana and disciplined kriya
Khandoba → Bhairava as localized, protective, warrior-Ksethrapala energy

Its some what like a same current, different expressions based on desha (region), kala (time), and sampradaya (tradition).
So your similarities are not coincidence, they are signs of the same underlying tattva revealing itself in different forms.

It is really good to see the depth of your study and the way you are connecting these points.

At the same time, just to gently add, in this path, information and understanding are only the first step. They help the mind become clear, but they don’t complete the journey.

What truly transforms is anubhava (direct experience).
Sadhana is not about collecting concepts, but about allowing the tattva to reveal itself within.
Until then, everything remains at the level of thought.

So whatever you have understood is valuable but its real purpose is to guide you deeper into practice, where these distinctions dissolve and become a living reality.

Jnana prepares, but sadhana transforms

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Swami, if we perform Mandala sadhana immediately after Nitya Puja, do we need to offer a different diya, bhog, and dhoop, or would the same suffice and we just re-offer them mentally? Or should another set be kept ready?
answer:
Namaskaram 

If the Mandala sadhana is being performed immediately after your Nitya Puja, then in most cases the same diya, dhoop, and bhog can continue; you may simply re-offer them mentally with bhava and sankalpa before beginning the sadhana. Bhairava looks more at your awareness and sincerity than at external repetition alone.

However, if the sadhana is very specific, intense, or mentioned separately in the paddhati, then keeping a fresh offering can also be done as a mark of deeper devotion and alertness.

Do not get lost in overcomplication. 

Consistency, purity of bhava, and presence during sadhana are more important.

Jai Bhairava

question: Guru please guide.. I have started kal Bhairava ashtakam for 40 days and now today is the 37th day.. I am about to complete it.. But I have to go to urgently at my sister's home tomorrow.. Very much worried, can I do remaining pooja there itself? Please guide
answer:
Namaskaram 

Yes, you can continue and complete the remaining days there itself. 

Do not carry fear in your heart. Bhairava is not limited by place your sincerity, discipline, and bhava are what truly matter.

Before starting there, just mentally pray to Baba and say with humility that due to unavoidable circumstances you are continuing the sankalpa from another place. Maintain the same devotion and as much purity and consistency as possible.

Do not break the sadhana out of anxiety. Complete it with steadiness and trust. Baba understands the heart of the sadhaka more than outer situations.

Jai Bhairava

question: After starting Bhairava Sādhana, why has my nature changed — why am I becoming more furious, more intense, and more reactive towards the people around me?
answer:
A question was received from one of the sadhakas and I felt like sharing it with all of you, because somewhere this may also be a question silently arising within many of you on this path..

After starting Bhairava sadhana, why has my nature completely changed
Why am I becoming more furious, more intense, more reactive towards people around me
Why do I suddenly feel like pointing out what is wrong in everyone
Is this really a part of Bhairava sadhana

Yes…

This phase can happen to many sadhakas

Because Bhairava is not a soft comforting energy alone
Guru Bhairava is the fire that tears illusions apart…

The moment Bhairava enters your consciousness, suppressed anger, hidden pain, emotional poison, fear, ego, frustration and unhealed darkness slowly start rising to the surface

What was sleeping inside you for years suddenly starts waking up

Especially during Bhairava sadhana, Agni Tattva becomes highly active
Not only spiritually, even Jataragni inside the body starts becoming intense…

Your energy becomes sharper
Your reactions become stronger
Your tolerance towards lies, manipulation and fake behaviour reduces

But this is where a Bhairava Sadhaka must become extremely conscious

Because there is a very thin line between transformation and destruction

If awareness is missing, the same fire that was meant to burn your ego will start burning your relationships, your peace and your words

Bhairava never teaches hatred towards people
He destroys the darkness within you first

A real Bhairava Sadhaka slowly becomes silent
Deep
Fearless
Stable
Detached
Powerful
But never unnecessarily cruel

Remember this deeply

The fire of Bhairava is not given to attack the world
It is given to burn the darkness you carry within yourself

Agni without awareness becomes anger
Agni with awareness becomes tapas

That is why grounding is very important during Bhairava sadhana

Watch your speech
Watch your food
Watch your emotions
Watch your ego
Watch your reactions

Because Bhairava does not only test devotion
He tests whether you are capable of holding his fire without losing your humanity

And the day this fire becomes conscious inside you
Your presence itself becomes protection

question: From few days I am seeing seetha bhairaveshawra boards which seems is little unusual if poosible will it be ok to make us understand how seetha mata is connected to bhairava baba
answer:
Namaskaram 

First of all, that’s actually a very beautiful observation regarding Seetha (Seethi) Bhairaveshwara.

This swaroopa is not completely explained in the mainstream Puranas, but it strongly lives through Karnataka’s local sthala-puranas and oral Shaiva traditions.

The connection mainly comes through places like Avani, Seethi Betta, Kaiwara, Pandava Betta, and Sathi Betta.

As per the local belief, during the Bhasmasura episode, Shiva moved through these sacred hill regions carrying the fierce Bhairava tattva, which we have explained in one of episode of Mahadeva Bhairava connected to Yana kshetra.. In some traditions, Devi in Sathi swaroopa performed aradhana to this Bhairaveshwara form, and because of that the name “Sathi Bhairaveshwara” became connected to the kshetra.

Now connecting to Avani ( Kolar) kshetra which is believed to be the place where Lava and Kusha were raised local traditions say that Ma Sita used to perform Shiva aradhana in these nearby hill regions during her exile period.

The Shiva there was not seen as only peaceful Shiva, but as a guardian form Bhairaveshwara, the protector of the kshetra.
So over time, devotees started calling the deity:

“Seetha Bhairaveshwara”
Bhairava worshipped through Seetha Devi’s tapas and devotion.

That’s why this swaroopa feels different from a purely ugra Bhairava.
There is fierceness, but also softness. Protection, but also compassion.

And when you connect Pandava Betta and Sathi Betta traditions, one thing becomes common in all these hills:

tapas,
Devi aradhana,
Shiva presence,
and Bhairava as kshetrapala.

In tantra and old Shaiva traditions, wherever Devi does tapas, Bhairava stands guard.
That is the deeper essence behind this swaroopa.

So Seetha Bhairaveshwara is less about scripture alone, and more about a living spiritual memory carried through the land, hills, temples, and devotees of Karnataka.

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
Greetings
I am new to this group and I am very thrilled to have found this group and your insightful videos.
I have been doing bhairav sadhana on my own for the past 5 months – relying mostly on internet readings.  Many times, I am unsure of what I am doing but I do it anyways with faith devotion discipline that baba will guide me.
I am doing the following: nama japa “om bhairavaye namah”,  reading 108 names of batuk bhairav,  and chanting kala bhairav ashtakam.
I have completed 100,000 japa of the nama mantra “om bhairavaye namah”.  In addition,  I am doing daily chanting of 108 names of batuk bhairav and chanting kala bhairav asthakam – once each daily and I do more repetition on Kala Ashtami tithes.
I want to follow a more structured disciplined step by step roadmap to this inner awakening journey and go deeper.  I feel like I am walking alone and I’ve no one to talk to or ask question.  So I am grateful to find this sangha community to connect with.  
Here are my two questions: 
1). What sadhana process and Charanah stages you recommend for someone like me?  I am still a new sadak – eager to learn, grow and connect.  Please guide me.
2). What activity does one mandala include? I know it’s 48 days but what exactly is performed in terms of japa/chanting/dhyana in a single mandala?
Thank you so much for your mentorship guidance.  
May Baba’s Kripa and compassion always shine upon us all
answer:
Namaskaram,

First of all, understand that becoming part of Bhairavanugraha itself is Baba’s grace. Even the ability to do his Nama Smarana with devotion and discipline happens only through his Anugraha.

The sincerity with which you have continued your sadhana for the past 5 months is truly beautiful. Please continue steadily without rushing.

The questions you are asking regarding sadhana Paddhati, Mandala, and Charana stages are already covered in the Purvacharana ( Adhi Charana), which is the foundational stage of the sadhaka’s journey.

Https://sites. Google. Com/view/daiva-anughara/home

Please take some time to carefully go through the webpage and Q&A section where all these details are explained step by step. Most of your questions will become clear there itself.

And even after that, if you still need any clarity, feel free to ask. You are no longer walking alone. Baba always connects a sincere sadhaka to the right sangha at the right time.

~ Jai Bhairava

question:
Thank you for the sadhana link.
The instructions are very clear for all Charanas.
What is still not clear to me is:
However, after doing the initial prayers what exactly is the main sadhana - doing 108 malas of Om Bhairvaya Namaha, Kaal Bhairava Ashtakam or Batuk Bhairava Namavali? Or
How many times do we have to so and what exactly we have to chant as main sadhana?
Can you please guide in that respect
answer:
Namaskaram,

Glad the instructions and Mandala Paddhati details were clear to you.

After the initial prayers and sankalpa, the main sadhana for this Mandala is primarily the mantra japa of “Om Bhairavaya Namaha” with discipline and consistency. The other stotras like Kaal Bhairava Ashtakam or Batuk Bhairava Namavali are supportive offerings of devotion and can be done before or after the main japa based on your capacity and bhava.

Regarding the count:

- The core practice is the daily mantra japa.
- The total count and daily discipline should be maintained as mentioned in the Mandala instructions shared in the link.
- Ashtakam / Namavali are not compulsory replacements for the main mantra japa, but devotional additions that help deepen connection and invoke His presence more strongly.

Please go through the Q&A document once, as almost all the commonly asked queries.

More than quantity alone, sincerity, steadiness, and maintaining the Mandala discipline with humility is important. Slowly, the flow of the sadhana itself will become clearer through His anugraha and inner guidance.

~ Jai Bhairava

question: Guruji Pranam — most of my life has been focused around effort, discipline, and progress. In Bhairava sādhana I do not know how to orient myself. Old identities, habits, and achievement-oriented thinking continue to arise, and I cannot tell whether that is natural conditioning, ego, or something that must be overcome. How should a Bhairava sādhaka think about progress? How does one know whether they are moving in the right direction, stagnating, going off course, or over-thinking? And how does one distinguish between incorrect understanding and actual Guru Tattva while intuition itself is still developing
answer:
Namaskaram, 


What you are experiencing is very natural for many sincere sadhaks. Most of us have been shaped by a life where effort, achievement, comparison, and progress became the way we understood value and growth. So when one enters Bhairava sadhana, the mind initially tries to approach even the sacred through the same framework, “Am I progressing? Am I doing enough? Am I reaching somewhere?”

But Bhairava slowly shifts the orientation from achievement to awareness, from control to surrender, and from outer accomplishment to inner transformation.

This does not mean discipline is wrong. In fact, discipline, consistency, and sincerity are very important in Bhairava sadhana. But over time, the center of the practice changes. Earlier the effort comes from personal will; gradually it becomes an offering. Earlier one practices to attain; later one practices simply to remain connected.

The arising of old identities, habits, ambitions, and mental patterns does not automatically mean failure or ego. Often, sadhana first brings awareness to what was already inside. Bhairava does not always remove things immediately, sometimes He makes the sadhak see clearly before transformation happens.

Regarding progress in sadhana:
A Bhairava sadhak should observe less “What extraordinary experience did I get?” and more:

- Is awareness increasing?
- Is reaction reducing slowly?
- Is sincerity becoming deeper?
- Is remembrance becoming more natural?
- Even amidst difficulty, is there still movement towards Him?

These are subtler signs of movement.

There will also be phases where everything feels dry, unclear, or stagnant. That too is part of the path. Not every silence means you are off course. Sometimes Bhairava teaches through intensity, and sometimes through emptiness.

As for intuition and Guru Tattva, true guidance usually carries clarity, steadiness, humility, and grounding. Ego-driven impulses often create restlessness, urgency, superiority, fear, or emotional excitement. In the beginning, it is completely okay if discernment is not fully developed. That is why steadiness in mantra, discipline, humility, and patience are so important. Over time, through His anugraha, inner clarity matures naturally.

Do not become overly occupied with measuring yourself at every step. Continue sincerely, remain grounded, and let the sadhana gradually shape you from within. Bhairava sadhana is not merely about becoming “more capable”; it is about becoming more aligned, more aware, and more truthful within oneself.

Jai Bhairava

question: I am going to be as brave as possible and say this here rather than privately, in case someone else has the same problem. I have an active weed addiction that is extremely hard to kick. I do not smoke before ritual in the morning — I keep that pure — but it remains a real issue. Use has lessened with sādhana, and I am not looking to spiritually bypass. I have a therapist who specialises in addiction and a support system. I just do not feel strong enough to stop on my own. If you tell me to stop, I will try. What is your guidance, Guruji
answer:
Namaskaram

First, thank you for speaking honestly with such openness. That itself takes strength and sincerity. Please do not fall into guilt or self-hatred over this. 

Bhairava does not reject someone who is struggling sincerely and still trying to walk toward Him.

You already have awareness, support, and the willingness to try that itself is grace moving. Do not force yourself through violence toward your own mind. Slowly, with steadiness, discipline, prayer, and sincerity, reduction will happen naturally. Continue protecting the purity of your morning sadhana as you are doing now.

More than sudden suppression, sincerity and consistency in sadhana matter deeply. Stay truthful, stay aware, and keep offering the struggle itself at His feet. His anugraha can carry us where our personal strength feels insufficient.

All will be well, trust him! 

Jai Bhairava.

question: Guruji, please guide me. Two days ago a small puppy fell ill. People said she had rabies, so the doctor advised isolating her for precaution; they placed her in a vacant plot in front of my home. From that night through the next day she was trying to come out. I saw her and placed milk near her, but I was scared it might really be rabies. She died in front of me. I cannot forget this incident — her face keeps coming before me. Later I realised she was only suffering from heat stroke. I was watching her struggle, hungry and thirsty, and could not act because of my fear. I feel I was responsible too, along with the others. Please guide me to find peace. Thank you also for sharing your own experiences with us so openly — it is not easy to relive those feelings, and I am very grateful
answer:
Namaskaram Maa,

Sorry for the delay in responding. 

Please do not hold this pain entirely against yourself. The very fact that your heart is feeling this deeply shows the compassion and sensitivity within you. Many would have simply ignored it, but you stayed present, you cared, and you even tried to help despite your fear.

At that moment, you acted with the awareness and understanding you had. Fear of rabies is natural, and it does not make you heartless. Sometimes life places us in situations where afterwards we feel “I could have done more,” but carrying guilt endlessly will only wound your own heart further.

Offer a small prayer for the little soul. Light a diya, offer water or food to another animal in her memory, and pray that Bhairava guides that being peacefully onward. 

Let this become compassion, not self-punishment.

Some souls come briefly only to awaken deeper karuna within us. Do not let this incident close your heart in pain. Let it make your compassion even more conscious and courageous moving forward.

Sarvam Bhairava Swaroopam 

Jai Bhairava
